## παγιδεύω (id: 003706000000000)

παγιδεύω
========

παγιδεύω (G3802)

παγίς

27\.30 · Learn · Learn Something Against Someone

to trap, to catch off guard, to catch in a mistake (figurative extension of meaning of a term meaning literally ‘to hunt,’ ‘to trap,’ not occurring in the NT) to acquire information about an error or fault, with the purpose of causing harm or trouble In a number of languages it is possible to retain the figurative meaning of ‘trap’ or ‘catch,’ but in a number of instances other figures are used, for example, ‘to cause to trip,’ ‘to cause to fall,’ ‘to hear wrong words from his mouth,’ or ‘to use his words against him.’

[MAT 22:15](https://ref.ly/Matt22:15)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 22:15


## παγίς (id: 003707000000000)

παγίς
=====

παγίς (G3803)

noun \| \-ίδος \| παγιδεύω

6\.23 · Artifacts · Traps, Snares

trap, snare a device used for trapping or snaring, principally birds Since snares or traps for capturing birds are almost universal, there should be no difficulty involved in obtaining an appropriate term, though in the context of [LUK 21:34](https://ref.ly/Mark21:34)\-[LUK 21:35](https://ref.ly/Mark21:35) the function of παγίς is figurative and clearly marked as a simile. It may be necessary in some instances to expand the simile somewhat as ‘as a snare is used to catch birds.’

[LUK 21:35](https://ref.ly/Mark21:35) · [ROM 11:9](https://ref.ly/Acts11:9)

21\.4 · Danger, Risk, Safe, Save · Danger

danger, trap (a figurative extension of meaning of παγίς\[a] ‘trap,’ [6\.23](#6.23)) that which brings or is a means of sudden danger Though in [1TI 6:9](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:9) παγίδα and ἐπιθυμίας πολλάς are syntactically coordinate, the semantic relationship involves dependency, since the danger consists of the many foolish and harmful desires. One could translate, therefore, ‘the danger of many foolish and harmful desires’ or ‘the danger of being trapped by many foolish and harmful desires.’ In this way, something of the literal as well as the figurative meaning of παγίς may be retained.

[1TI 3:7](https://ref.ly/2Thess3:7) · [1TI 6:9](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:9) · [2TI 2:26](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:26)

37\.15 · Control, Rule · Control, Restrain

means of control, way of trapping (figurative extension of meaning of παγίς\[a] ‘trap’ [6\.23](#6.23)) an instrument or means for gaining control, implying an element of surprise In a number of languages, it is possible to reflect something of the figurative meaning of παγίς by translating the term as a verb, for example, ‘may they be caught and trapped at their feasts’ or ‘may their feasts snare and trap them’ (see [6\.23](#6.23) and [6\.24](#6.24)).

[ROM 11:9](https://ref.ly/Acts11:9)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 21:35; Romans 11:9; 1 Timothy 3:7; 1 Timothy 6:9; 2 Timothy 2:26


## πάθημα (id: 003708000000000)

πάθημα
======

πάθημα (G3804)

noun \| \-τος \| πάσχω

24\.78 · Sensory Events and States · Pain, Suffering

pain, suffering, to suffer, to be in pain to suffer pain In [1PE 4:13](https://ref.ly/Jas4:13) the phrase τοῖς τοῦ Χριστοῦ παθήμασιν ‘in the sufferings of Christ’ may mean ‘the sufferings experienced at the time of the Messiah,’ that is to say, ‘the Messianic woes.’ See also [24\.87](#24.87).

[ROM 8:18](https://ref.ly/Acts8:18) · [2CO 1:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:5) · [2CO 1:6](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:6)1 · [2CO 1:7](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:7) · [PHP 3:10](https://ref.ly/Eph3:10) · [COL 1:24](https://ref.ly/Phil1:24) · [2TI 3:11](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:11) · [HEB 2:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:9) · [HEB 2:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:10) · [HEB 10:32](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:32) · [1PE 1:11](https://ref.ly/Jas1:11) · [1PE 4:13](https://ref.ly/Jas4:13) · [1PE 5:1](https://ref.ly/Jas5:1) · [1PE 5:9](https://ref.ly/Jas5:9)

25\.30 · Attitudes and Emotions · Desire Strongly

passion, lust, lustful desire, to have lust to experience strong physical desires, particularly of a sexual nature

[ROM 7:5](https://ref.ly/Acts7:5) · [GAL 5:24](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:24)

1 2CO 1:6 It is also possible to interpret πάσχομεν in [2CO 1:6](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:6) as being essentially a marker of a case relationship meaning ‘to experience’ (see [90\.66](#90.66)).

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 8:18; 2 Corinthians 1:5; 2 Corinthians 1:6; 2 Corinthians 1:7; Philippians 3:10; Colossians 1:24; 2 Timothy 3:11; Hebrews 2:9; Hebrews 2:10; Hebrews 10:32; 1 Peter 1:11; 1 Peter 4:13; 1 Peter 5:1; 1 Peter 5:9; Romans 7:5; Galatians 5:24

παθητός
=======

παθητός (G3805)

adj \| \-ή, \-όν \| πάσχω

subject to suffering pertaining to being subject to suffering

[ACT 26:23](https://ref.ly/John26:23)

## πάθος (id: 003710000000000)

πάθος
=====

πάθος (G3806)

noun \| \-ους \| πάσχω

25\.30 · Attitudes and Emotions · Desire Strongly

passion, lust, lustful desire, to have lust to experience strong physical desires, particularly of a sexual nature

[ROM 1:26](https://ref.ly/Acts1:26) · [COL 3:5](https://ref.ly/Phil3:5) · [1TH 4:5](https://ref.ly/Col4:5)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 1:26; Colossians 3:5; 1 Thessalonians 4:5


## παιδαγωγός (id: 003711000000000)

παιδαγωγός
==========

παιδαγωγός (G3807)

noun \| \-οῦ \| παίζω \+ ἄγω

36\.5 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Guide, Lead

guardian, leader, guide a person who guides, directs, and shows concern for It may be difficult to render appropriately ἐπίτροπος\[b] and παιδαγωγός with a combined meaning of guiding and caring for. It may, in fact, be necessary in some instances to use two verbal expressions, for example, ‘to guide and to help’ or ‘to help by leading’ or ‘to care for by leading.’  
In classical times, a παιδαγωγός was a man, usually a slave, whose task it was to conduct a boy to and from school and to supervise and direct his general conduct. He was not a teacher.

[1CO 4:15](https://ref.ly/Rom4:15) · [GAL 3:24](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:24) · [GAL 3:25](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:25)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Corinthians 4:15; Galatians 3:24; Galatians 3:25


## παιδάριον (id: 003712000000000)

παιδάριον
=========

παιδάριον (G3808)

noun \| \-ου \| παίζω

9\.42 · People · Children

child (diminutive of παῖς\[a] ‘child,’ [9\.41](#9.41), but in the NT παιδάριον has seemingly lost at least most of this diminutive force, but may have retained some implications of affectionate concern or interest) a child, normally below the age of puberty Note that παιδάριον in [JHN 6:9](https://ref.ly/Luke6:9) denotes merely a child; it is the referential pronoun ὅς which indicates masculine gender, and therefore one may translate ‘boy.’  
It is also possible that in this context the diminutive παιδάριον carries some connotation of endearment.

[JHN 6:9](https://ref.ly/Luke6:9)

* **Associated Passages:** John 6:9


## παιδεία (id: 003713000000000)

παιδεία
=======

παιδεία (G3809)

noun \| \-ας \| παίζω

33\.226 · Communication · Teach

to teach, to instruct, to train, teaching, instruction to provide instruction, with the intent of forming proper habits of behavior1

[2TI 3:16](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:16) · [HEB 12:5](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:5) · [HEB 12:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:11)

36\.10 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Discipline, Train

to discipline, to train, discipline, training to train someone in accordance with proper rules of conduct and behavior

[EPH 6:4](https://ref.ly/Gal6:4) · [HEB 12:5](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:5) · [HEB 12:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:7) · [HEB 12:8](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:8) · [HEB 12:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:11)

38\.4 · Punish, Reward · Punish

to punish, punishment to punish for the purpose of improved behavior

[HEB 12:5](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:5) · [HEB 12:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:7) · [HEB 12:8](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:8) · [HEB 12:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:11)

1 παιδεύω\[a] and παιδεία\[a] ([33\.226](#33.226)) differ in meaning from νουθετέω\[a] and νουθεσία\[a] ([33\.231](#33.231)) and from ἐντρέφω ([33\.242](#33.242)). παιδεύω\[a] and παιδεία\[a] focus upon the forming of proper habits of behavior, while νουθετέω\[a] and νουθεσία\[a] focus upon instruction in correct behavior. ἐντρέφω appears to focus more on continuous instruction and training in the areas of skill and practical knowledge.

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Timothy 3:16; Hebrews 12:5; Hebrews 12:11; Ephesians 6:4; Hebrews 12:7; Hebrews 12:8


## παιδευτής (id: 003714000000000)

παιδευτής
=========

παιδευτής (G3810)

noun \| \-οῦ \| παίζω

33\.244 · Communication · Teach

instructor, trainer, teacher (derivative of παιδεύω\[a] ‘to train,’ [33\.226](#33.226)) one who provides instruction for the purpose of proper behavior

[ROM 2:20](https://ref.ly/Acts2:20) · [HEB 12:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:9)

38\.5 · Punish, Reward · Punish

punisher, one who punishes (derivative of παιδεύω\[c] ‘to punish,’ [38\.4](#38.4)) a person who punishes for constructive purposes

[HEB 12:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:9)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 2:20; Hebrews 12:9


## παιδεύω (id: 003715000000000)

παιδεύω
=======

παιδεύω (G3811)

παίζω

33\.226 · Communication · Teach

to teach, to instruct, to train, teaching, instruction to provide instruction, with the intent of forming proper habits of behavior1

[ACT 7:22](https://ref.ly/John7:22) · [ACT 22:3](https://ref.ly/John22:3) · [TIT 2:12](https://ref.ly/2Tim2:12)

36\.10 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Discipline, Train

to discipline, to train, discipline, training to train someone in accordance with proper rules of conduct and behavior

[1CO 11:32](https://ref.ly/Rom11:32) · [2TI 2:25](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:25) · [HEB 12:6](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:6) · [HEB 12:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:7) · [HEB 12:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:10)

38\.4 · Punish, Reward · Punish

to punish, punishment to punish for the purpose of improved behavior

[LUK 23:16](https://ref.ly/Mark23:16) · [LUK 23:22](https://ref.ly/Mark23:22) · [2CO 6:9](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:9) · [1TI 1:20](https://ref.ly/2Thess1:20) · [HEB 12:6](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:6) · [HEB 12:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:7) · [HEB 12:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:10) · [REV 3:19](https://ref.ly/Jude3:19)

1 παιδεύω\[a] and παιδεία\[a] ([33\.226](#33.226)) differ in meaning from νουθετέω\[a] and νουθεσία\[a] ([33\.231](#33.231)) and from ἐντρέφω ([33\.242](#33.242)). παιδεύω\[a] and παιδεία\[a] focus upon the forming of proper habits of behavior, while νουθετέω\[a] and νουθεσία\[a] focus upon instruction in correct behavior. ἐντρέφω appears to focus more on continuous instruction and training in the areas of skill and practical knowledge.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 7:22; Acts 22:3; Titus 2:12; 1 Corinthians 11:32; 2 Timothy 2:25; Hebrews 12:6; Hebrews 12:7; Hebrews 12:10; Luke 23:16; Luke 23:22; 2 Corinthians 6:9; 1 Timothy 1:20; Revelation 3:19


## παιδιόθεν (id: 003716000000000)

παιδιόθεν
=========

παιδιόθεν (G3812)

adv \| παίζω

67\.153 · Time · Indefinite Units of Time: Age, Lifetime, Interval, Period

since childhood the period from the time a person is a child until the time of the context1 In παιδιόθεν, the stem παιδιο would suggest an age somewhat older than in the case of βρέφος\[b] ‘infancy’ ([67\.152](#67.152)). παιδιόθεν, accordingly, would probably refer to a period of time which begins after a child has been weaned.

[MRK 9:21](https://ref.ly/Matt9:21)

1 The meanings of terms in [67\.152](#67.152)\-[67\.158](#67.158) could almost be treated equally well in Domain 23 Physiological Processes and States, since these could be regarded as simply derivatives of physiological states. However, the focus of meaning in the respective contexts seems to be more upon a particular period of time rather than upon a physiological state or condition.

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 9:21


## παιδίον (id: 003717000000000)

παιδίον
=======

παιδίον (G3813)

noun \| \-ου \| παίζω

9\.42 · People · Children

child (diminutive of παῖς\[a] ‘child,’ [9\.41](#9.41), but in the NT παιδίον has seemingly lost at least most of this diminutive force, but may have retained some implications of affectionate concern or interest) a child, normally below the age of puberty

[MAT 2:8](https://ref.ly/Matt2:8) · [MAT 2:9](https://ref.ly/Matt2:9) · [MAT 2:11](https://ref.ly/Matt2:11) · [MAT 2:13](https://ref.ly/Matt2:13) · [MAT 2:13](https://ref.ly/Matt2:13) · [MAT 2:14](https://ref.ly/Matt2:14) · [MAT 2:20](https://ref.ly/Matt2:20) · [MAT 2:20](https://ref.ly/Matt2:20) · [MAT 2:21](https://ref.ly/Matt2:21) · [MAT 11:16](https://ref.ly/Matt11:16) · [MAT 14:21](https://ref.ly/Matt14:21) · [MAT 15:38](https://ref.ly/Matt15:38) · [MAT 18:2](https://ref.ly/Matt18:2) · [MAT 18:3](https://ref.ly/Matt18:3) · [MAT 18:4](https://ref.ly/Matt18:4) · [MAT 18:5](https://ref.ly/Matt18:5) · [MAT 19:13](https://ref.ly/Matt19:13) · [MAT 19:14](https://ref.ly/Matt19:14) · [MRK 5:39](https://ref.ly/Matt5:39) · [MRK 5:40](https://ref.ly/Matt5:40) · [MRK 5:40](https://ref.ly/Matt5:40) · [MRK 5:41](https://ref.ly/Matt5:41) · [MRK 7:28](https://ref.ly/Matt7:28) · [MRK 9:36](https://ref.ly/Matt9:36) · [MRK 9:37](https://ref.ly/Matt9:37) · [MRK 10:13](https://ref.ly/Matt10:13) · [MRK 10:14](https://ref.ly/Matt10:14) · [MRK 10:15](https://ref.ly/Matt10:15) · [LUK 1:59](https://ref.ly/Mark1:59) · [LUK 1:66](https://ref.ly/Mark1:66) · [LUK 1:76](https://ref.ly/Mark1:76) · [LUK 1:80](https://ref.ly/Mark1:80) · [LUK 2:17](https://ref.ly/Mark2:17) · [LUK 2:27](https://ref.ly/Mark2:27) · [LUK 2:40](https://ref.ly/Mark2:40) · [LUK 7:32](https://ref.ly/Mark7:32) · [LUK 9:47](https://ref.ly/Mark9:47) · [LUK 9:48](https://ref.ly/Mark9:48) · [LUK 18:16](https://ref.ly/Mark18:16) · [LUK 18:17](https://ref.ly/Mark18:17) · [1CO 14:20](https://ref.ly/Rom14:20) · [HEB 11:23](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:23)

10\.37 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations Involving Successive Generations

child, offspring (diminutive of παῖς\[b] ‘child,’ [10\.36](#10.36), but with the loss of at least most of the diminutive meaning) one’s immediate offspring One need not assume in [LUK 11:7](https://ref.ly/Mark11:7) that the children were in the same bed with their father. One may render this passage simply as ‘I have gone to bed and so have my children’ or ‘I am already in bed and so are my children,’ but see [6\.108](#6.108).

[MRK 7:30](https://ref.ly/Matt7:30) · [MRK 9:24](https://ref.ly/Matt9:24) · [LUK 11:7](https://ref.ly/Mark11:7) · [JHN 4:49](https://ref.ly/Luke4:49) · [JHN 16:21](https://ref.ly/Luke16:21)

9\.46 · People · Persons For Whom There Is Affectionate Concern

my child, my dear friend, my dear man, my dear one, my dear lad (extension of meaning of παιδίον\[a] ‘child,’ [9\.42](#9.42)) a person of any age for whom there is a special relationship of endearment and association In a number of languages παιδία in [JHN 21:5](https://ref.ly/Luke21:5) is better rendered as ‘my good friends’ or ‘my dear friends’ or ‘my dear comrades.’ In this context, not age, but the connotation of affection and endearment is in focus.

[JHN 21:5](https://ref.ly/Luke21:5) · [HEB 2:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:13) · [HEB 2:14](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:14) · [1JN 2:14](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:14) · [1JN 2:18](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:18)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 2:8; Matthew 2:9; Matthew 2:11; Matthew 2:13; Matthew 2:14; Matthew 2:20; Matthew 2:21; Matthew 11:16; Matthew 14:21; Matthew 15:38; Matthew 18:2; Matthew 18:3; Matthew 18:4; Matthew 18:5; Matthew 19:13; Matthew 19:14; Mark 5:39; Mark 5:40; Mark 5:41; Mark 7:28; Mark 9:36; Mark 9:37; Mark 10:13; Mark 10:14; Mark 10:15; Luke 1:59; Luke 1:66; Luke 1:76; Luke 1:80; Luke 2:17; Luke 2:27; Luke 2:40; Luke 7:32; Luke 9:47; Luke 9:48; Luke 18:16; Luke 18:17; 1 Corinthians 14:20; Hebrews 11:23; Mark 7:30; Mark 9:24; Luke 11:7; John 4:49; John 16:21; John 21:5; Hebrews 2:13; Hebrews 2:14; 1 John 2:14; 1 John 2:18


## παιδίσκη (id: 003718000000000)

παιδίσκη
========

παιδίσκη (G3814)

noun \| \-ης \| παίζω

87\.83 · Status · Slave, Free

slave girl, slave woman a female slave1

[MAT 26:69](https://ref.ly/Matt26:69) · [MRK 14:66](https://ref.ly/Matt14:66) · [MRK 14:69](https://ref.ly/Matt14:69) · [LUK 12:45](https://ref.ly/Mark12:45) · [LUK 22:56](https://ref.ly/Mark22:56) · [JHN 18:17](https://ref.ly/Luke18:17) · [ACT 12:13](https://ref.ly/John12:13) · [ACT 16:16](https://ref.ly/John16:16) · [GAL 4:22](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:22) · [GAL 4:23](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:23) · [GAL 4:30](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:30) · [GAL 4:30](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:30) · [GAL 4:31](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:31)

1 παιδίσκη probably refers to someone younger than would be the case for δούλη.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 26:69; Mark 14:66; Mark 14:69; Luke 12:45; Luke 22:56; John 18:17; Acts 12:13; Acts 16:16; Galatians 4:22; Galatians 4:23; Galatians 4:30; Galatians 4:31


## παίζω (id: 003719000000000)

παίζω
=====

παίζω (G3815)

ἀπαίδευτος · ἐμπαιγμονή · ἐμπαιγμός · ἐμπαίζω · ἐμπαίκτης · παιδαγωγός · παιδάριον · παιδεία · παιδευτής · παιδιόθεν · παιδίον · παιδίσκη · παῖς

50\.8 · Contests and Play

to play to engage in an activity for the sake of amusement and/or recreation The specific reference of παίζω in [1CO 10:7](https://ref.ly/Rom10:7) is probably to dancing, but some scholars interpret παίζω in this context as a euphemism for sex.

[1CO 10:7](https://ref.ly/Rom10:7)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Corinthians 10:7


## παῖς (id: 003720000000000)

παῖς
====

παῖς (G3816)

noun \| παιδός \| παίζω

9\.41 · People · Children

child a young person, normally below the age of puberty and without distinction as to sex The term παῖς may occur with either masculine or feminine articles and corresponding adjectival attributes. These gender distinctions indicate whether the person referred to is male or female. With the masculine article or attributives one may translate παῖς\[a] as ‘boy,’ and similarly with female attributives one may translate παῖς\[a] as ‘girl.’

[MAT 2:16](https://ref.ly/Matt2:16) · [MAT 21:15](https://ref.ly/Matt21:15) · [LUK 8:54](https://ref.ly/Mark8:54) · [LUK 9:42](https://ref.ly/Mark9:42) · [ACT 20:12](https://ref.ly/John20:12)

10\.36 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations Involving Successive Generations

child, offspring one’s immediate offspring, but without specific reference to sex or age In languages in which there is no generic term for ‘child’ or ‘offspring,’ one may use ‘son’ or ‘daughter’ or ‘son and daughter,’ singular or plural, depending upon the context.

[MAT 17:18](https://ref.ly/Matt17:18) · [LUK 1:54](https://ref.ly/Mark1:54) · [LUK 1:69](https://ref.ly/Mark1:69) · [LUK 2:43](https://ref.ly/Mark2:43) · [LUK 8:51](https://ref.ly/Mark8:51) · [JHN 4:51](https://ref.ly/Luke4:51)

87\.77 · Status · Slave, Free

slave a slave, possibly serving as a personal servant and thus with the implication of kindly regard

[MAT 8:6](https://ref.ly/Matt8:6) · [MAT 8:8](https://ref.ly/Matt8:8) · [MAT 8:13](https://ref.ly/Matt8:13) · [MAT 12:18](https://ref.ly/Matt12:18) · [MAT 14:2](https://ref.ly/Matt14:2) · [LUK 1:54](https://ref.ly/Mark1:54) · [LUK 1:69](https://ref.ly/Mark1:69) · [LUK 7:7](https://ref.ly/Mark7:7) · [LUK 12:45](https://ref.ly/Mark12:45) · [LUK 15:26](https://ref.ly/Mark15:26) · [ACT 3:13](https://ref.ly/John3:13) · [ACT 3:26](https://ref.ly/John3:26) · [ACT 4:25](https://ref.ly/John4:25) · [ACT 4:27](https://ref.ly/John4:27) · [ACT 4:30](https://ref.ly/John4:30)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 2:16; Matthew 21:15; Luke 8:54; Luke 9:42; Acts 20:12; Matthew 17:18; Luke 1:54; Luke 1:69; Luke 2:43; Luke 8:51; John 4:51; Matthew 8:6; Matthew 8:8; Matthew 8:13; Matthew 12:18; Matthew 14:2; Luke 7:7; Luke 12:45; Luke 15:26; Acts 3:13; Acts 3:26; Acts 4:25; Acts 4:27; Acts 4:30


## παίω (id: 003721000000000)

παίω
====

παίω (G3817)

19\.1 · Physical Impact · Hit, Strike

to hit, to strike, to beat to strike or hit an object, one or more times

[MAT 26:68](https://ref.ly/Matt26:68) · [MRK 14:47](https://ref.ly/Matt14:47) · [LUK 22:64](https://ref.ly/Mark22:64) · [JHN 18:10](https://ref.ly/Luke18:10)

19\.16 · Physical Impact · Pierce, Cut

to sting, to strike to sting (as of a scorpion)

[REV 9:5](https://ref.ly/Jude9:5)1

1 REV 9:5 It is possible that παίω in [REV 9:5](https://ref.ly/Jude9:5) should be understood in the more generic sense of ‘to strike’ (see παίω\[a], [19\.1](#19.1)). It is difficult to tell, especially in apocalyptic literature, precisely what particular meaning fits a context.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 26:68; Mark 14:47; Luke 22:64; John 18:10; Revelation 9:5


## πάλαι (id: 003722000000000)

πάλαι
=====

πάλαι (G3819)

adv \| παλαιόω

67\.24 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

long ago, of long ago pertaining to a point of time preceding another point of time, with an interval of considerable length1 References to a point of time preceding another point of time may express a relationship to an event in the discourse or to the time of the discourse itself. In [HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1) πάλαι\[a] marks a point of time long before the actual writing of this Letter to the Hebrews. The same is true of ἔκπαλαι in [2PE 3:5](https://ref.ly/1Pet3:5). In some languages it is necessary to indicate clearly whether the interval is (1\) between a prior time and the event mentioned in the discourse or (2\) between a prior time and the discourse itself. For example, in [HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1) it may be necessary to translate ‘long ago before now God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets’ or ‘long ago before now that I am writing ...’

[MAT 11:21](https://ref.ly/Matt11:21) · [LUK 10:13](https://ref.ly/Mark10:13) · [HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1) · [2PE 1:9](https://ref.ly/1Pet1:9) · [JUD 1:4](https://ref.ly/3John1:4)

67\.141 · Time · Duration of Time with Reference to Some Unit of Time: During, In, While, Throughout

all this time, all that time a considerable and continuous extent of time in the past

[2CO 12:19](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:19)

67\.22 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

already, already for some time a point of time preceding another point of time and implying completion, but with a longer interval of time than in the case of ἤδη ([67\.20](#67.20)) The use of πάλαι in [MRK 15:44](https://ref.ly/Matt15:44) would seem to suggest that Jesus‘ death had taken place considerably sooner than what Pilate had thought would happen. In trying to render the implication of πάλαι\[c], it may be possible to translate [MRK 15:44](https://ref.ly/Matt15:44) as ‘Pilate called the army officer and asked him if Jesus had indeed already died.’

[MRK 15:44](https://ref.ly/Matt15:44)

1 It is possible that ἔκπαλαι\[a] is somewhat more emphatic than πάλαι\[a], but this cannot be determined from existing contexts.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 11:21; Luke 10:13; Hebrews 1:1; 2 Peter 1:9; Jude 1:4; 2 Corinthians 12:19; Mark 15:44


## παλαιός (id: 003723000000000)

παλαιός
=======

παλαιός (G3820)

adj \| \-ά, \-όν \| παλαιόω

67\.97 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

old pertaining to having existed continuously for a relatively long time παλαιός\[a] in a context involving wineskins focuses upon the use of such wineskins over a long period of time, and therefore it may be necessary in some languages to translate ‘used wineskins’ or ‘wineskins which have been used for a long time.’

[MAT 13:52](https://ref.ly/Matt13:52) · [MRK 2:22](https://ref.ly/Matt2:22) · [1JN 2:7](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:7) · [1JN 2:7](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:7)

58\.75 · Nature, Class, Example · New, Old \[primarily non\-temporal]

old pertaining to that which is old, obsolete, and hence inferior

[MAT 9:16](https://ref.ly/Matt9:16) · [MAT 9:17](https://ref.ly/Matt9:17) · [MRK 2:21](https://ref.ly/Matt2:21) · [MRK 2:21](https://ref.ly/Matt2:21) · [MRK 2:22](https://ref.ly/Matt2:22) · [LUK 5:36](https://ref.ly/Mark5:36) · [LUK 5:36](https://ref.ly/Mark5:36) · [LUK 5:37](https://ref.ly/Mark5:37) · [LUK 5:39](https://ref.ly/Mark5:39) · [LUK 5:39](https://ref.ly/Mark5:39) · [ROM 6:6](https://ref.ly/Acts6:6) · [1CO 5:7](https://ref.ly/Rom5:7) · [1CO 5:8](https://ref.ly/Rom5:8) · [2CO 3:14](https://ref.ly/1Cor3:14)

67\.24 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

long ago, of long ago pertaining to a point of time preceding another point of time, with an interval of considerable length References to a point of time preceding another point of time may express a relationship to an event in the discourse or to the time of the discourse itself. In [HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1) πάλαι\[a] marks a point of time long before the actual writing of this Letter to the Hebrews. The same is true of ἔκπαλαι in [2PE 3:5](https://ref.ly/1Pet3:5). In some languages it is necessary to indicate clearly whether the interval is (1\) between a prior time and the event mentioned in the discourse or (2\) between a prior time and the discourse itself. For example, in [HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1) it may be necessary to translate ‘long ago before now God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets’ or ‘long ago before now that I am writing ...’  
Though in some translations τῆς παλαιᾶς διαθήκης is rendered as ‘the old covenant,’ it is important to distinguish in the meanings of ‘old’ between that which has lasted for a long time and that which comes from a period long ago. It is the latter meaning which is relevant in the context of [2CO 3:14](https://ref.ly/1Cor3:14).

[2CO 3:14](https://ref.ly/1Cor3:14) · [1JN 2:7](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:7)

41\.43 · Behavior and Related States · Particular Patterns of Behavior

**Collocation:** παλαιὸς ἄνθρωπος

old self, old pattern of life (an idiom, literally: old person, or: former person) the old or former pattern of behavior, in contrast with a new pattern of behavior which people should conform to In a number of languages one can best render this expression in [EPH 4:22](https://ref.ly/Gal4:22) as ‘don’t live the way you used to.’

[ROM 6:6](https://ref.ly/Acts6:6) · [EPH 4:22](https://ref.ly/Gal4:22) · [COL 3:9](https://ref.ly/Phil3:9)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 13:52; Mark 2:22; 1 John 2:7; Matthew 9:16; Matthew 9:17; Mark 2:21; Luke 5:36; Luke 5:37; Luke 5:39; Romans 6:6; 1 Corinthians 5:7; 1 Corinthians 5:8; 2 Corinthians 3:14; Ephesians 4:22; Colossians 3:9


## παλαιότης (id: 003724000000000)

παλαιότης
=========

παλαιότης (G3821)

noun \| \-ητος \| παλαιόω

67\.100 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

oldness, old, being obsolete the state of being old, with the implication of obsolescence For a somewhat different interpretation of παλαιότης in [ROM 7:6](https://ref.ly/Acts7:6), see [58\.74](#58.74).

[ROM 7:6](https://ref.ly/Acts7:6)1

58\.74 · Nature, Class, Example · New, Old \[primarily non\-temporal]

old, obsolete, old way the state of that which is old, obsolete, and hence inferior and unsatisfactory (in contrast with that which is either καινός\[b] or νέος\[b] ‘new,’ [58\.71](#58.71))

[ROM 7:6](https://ref.ly/Acts7:6)1

1 ROM 7:6 Whether παλαιότης in [ROM 7:6](https://ref.ly/Acts7:6) is to be classified in Domain 58 or in Domain 67 Time depends upon which of the principal features of meaning seems to be focal. Judgments may obviously differ.

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 7:6


## παλαιόω (id: 003725000000000)

παλαιόω
=======

παλαιόω (G3822) — παλαιόομαι

ἔκπαλαι · πάλαι · παλαιός · παλαιότης

67\.103 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

to make old, to make out of date to cause to become old and obsolete, and hence no longer valid

[LUK 12:33](https://ref.ly/Mark12:33) · [HEB 8:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm8:13) · [HEB 8:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm8:13)

67\.104 · (παλαιόομαι) · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

to become old, to deteriorate to become old, with the implication of becoming obsolete and/or useless In some languages it may be useful to translate παλαιόομαι in [HEB 1:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:11) as ‘they will become old and worn out.’

[HEB 1:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:11)

## πάλη (id: 003726000000000)

πάλη
====

πάλη (G3823)

noun \| \-ης

39\.29 · Hostility, Strife · Strife, Struggle

to struggle, to fight to engage in intense struggle, involving physical or nonphysical force against strong opposition In a number of languages a marked distinction is made between physical fighting and nonphysical contending with someone. It is therefore often impossible to carry over any terminology involving war or physical struggling to contexts in which one is speaking of interpersonal hostility or contention.

[EPH 6:12](https://ref.ly/Gal6:12)

* **Associated Passages:** Ephesians 6:12


## παλιγγενεσία (id: 003727000000000)

παλιγγενεσία
============

παλιγγενεσία (G3824)

noun \| \-ας \| πάλιν \+ γίνομαι

41\.53 · Behavior and Related States · Change Behavior

to be born again, to experience new birth, rebirth to experience a complete change in one’s way of life to what it should be, with the implication of return to a former state or relation The metaphor of ‘new birth’ is so important in the NT that it should be retained if at all possible. In some languages ‘new birth’ can be expressed as ‘to cause to be born all over again’ or ‘to have a new life as though one were born a second time.’ See also [13\.55](#13.55).

[TIT 3:5](https://ref.ly/2Tim3:5)

67\.147 · Time · Indefinite Units of Time: Age, Lifetime, Interval, Period

new age, Messianic age an era involving the renewal of the world (with special reference to the time of the Messiah)

[MAT 19:28](https://ref.ly/Matt19:28)

* **Associated Passages:** Titus 3:5; Matthew 19:28

1 MRK 15:13 There are certain difficulties involved in the interpretation of πάλιν in [MRK 15:13](https://ref.ly/Matt15:13), for this is the first instance in this Marcan context in which reference is made to the crowds shouting. The introduction of πάλιν may be the result of harmonization, or it could mean ‘they shouted back.’ It is also possible to interpret πάλιν in the sense of ‘and in turn they shouted.’ One may, however, assume a first occurrence of such a shout in verse 11\.

## πανοπλία (id: 003735000000000)

πανοπλία
========

πανοπλία (G3833)

noun \| \-ας \| πᾶς \+ ὁπλίζομαι

6\.30 · Artifacts · Weapons and Armor

weapons and armor a complete set of instruments used in defensive or offensive warfare (usually, however, with emphasis upon defensive armament, including helmet, shield, breastplate) πανοπλία is also used figuratively in the NT as a reference to the virtues of Christian character used in the strife against evil: ἐνδύσασθε τὴν πανοπλίαν τοῦ θεοῦ πρὸς τὸ δύνασθαι ὑμᾶς στῆναι πρὸς τὰς μεθοδείας τοῦ διαβόλου ‘put on all the armor that God gives you, so that you will stand up against the Devil’s evil tricks’ [EPH 6:11](https://ref.ly/Gal6:11).  
Since certain ancient types of armor are no longer used to any extent in fighting, it may be necessary to employ descriptive expressions in order to make clear the types of weapons and/or instruments which were involved. Offensive weapons are often spoken of as ‘tools with which one kills or harms an enemy,’ while defensive armor may be described as ‘what one wears to defend oneself when fighting.’ The complete sense of πανοπλία may then be described in some languages as ‘tools with which one fights against the enemy and what one uses to defend oneself’ or ‘... to protect oneself.’

[LUK 11:22](https://ref.ly/Mark11:22) · [EPH 6:11](https://ref.ly/Gal6:11) · [EPH 6:13](https://ref.ly/Gal6:13)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 11:22; Ephesians 6:11; Ephesians 6:13

## παντελής (id: 003740000000000)

παντελής
========

παντελής (G3838)

adj \| \-ές \| πᾶς \+ τελέω

67\.95 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

**Collocation:** εἰς τὸ παντελές

always, forever, forever and ever, eternally unlimited duration of time, with particular focus upon the future For another interpretation of εἰς τὸ παντελές in [HEB 7:25](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:25), see [78\.47](#78.47).

[LUK 13:11](https://ref.ly/Mark13:11) · [HEB 7:25](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:25)

78\.47 · Degree · Completely, Enough \[Completive Degree]

**Collocation:** εἰς τὸ παντελές

completely, totally, entirely, wholly a degree of completeness, with the possible implication of purpose or result It is also possible to understand the phrase εἰς τὸ παντελές as meaning ‘forever’ (see [67\.95](#67.95)).  
It is possible to translate this expression in [LUK 13:11](https://ref.ly/Mark13:11) as ‘she could not straighten up at all.’ The difference of interpretation in [LUK 13:11](https://ref.ly/Mark13:11) depends upon the scope of the phrase εἰς τὸ παντελές. If this phrase qualifies the negation, then the rendering is ‘she could not straighten up at all,’ but if the phrase εἰς τὸ παντελές qualifies just ἀνακύψαι, then the appropriate meaning would be ‘she could not straighten up completely.’

[LUK 13:11](https://ref.ly/Mark13:11) · [HEB 7:25](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:25)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 13:11; Hebrews 7:25


## πάντῃ (id: 003741000000000)

πάντῃ
=====

πάντῃ (G3839)

adv \| πᾶς

83\.8 · Spacial Positions · Where, Somewhere, Everywhere

everywhere, anywhere, all over all possible positions The expression πάντῃ τε καὶ πανταχοῦ in [ACT 24:3](https://ref.ly/John24:3) is repetitive for the sake of emphasis. Some scholars, however, interpret πάντῃ in [ACT 24:3](https://ref.ly/John24:3) as ‘at all times,’ in which case [ACT 24:3](https://ref.ly/John24:3) would be a rare example of this meaning of πάντῃ in Greek.

[ACT 24:3](https://ref.ly/John24:3)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 24:3

===========

noun \| \-ορος \| πᾶς \+ κρατέω

12\.7{N:001} · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

the Almighty, the One who has all power a title for God, literally: all powerful In some languages the Almighty as a title for God may be expressed as ‘the one who controls everything’ or ‘the one who commands all things and all people.’

[2CO 6:18](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:18) · [REV 1:8](https://ref.ly/Jude1:8) · [REV 4:8](https://ref.ly/Jude4:8) · [REV 11:17](https://ref.ly/Jude11:17) · [REV 15:3](https://ref.ly/Jude15:3) · [REV 16:7](https://ref.ly/Jude16:7) · [REV 16:14](https://ref.ly/Jude16:14) · [REV 19:6](https://ref.ly/Jude19:6) · [REV 19:15](https://ref.ly/Jude19:15) · [REV 21:22](https://ref.ly/Jude21:22)

1 Items [12\.4](#12.4)\-[12\.8](#12.8), ([12\.9](#12.9)) and [12\.12](#12.12) in this domain are titles for θεός\[a] God ([12\.1](#12.1)). As titles, they have precisely the same reference as θεός\[a], but they each contribute distinctive meanings which are legitimate qualifications of the referent in question.

89\.132 · Relations · Contrast

rather than, instead of a marker of contrast by means of an alternative In a number of languages an alternative contrast must be expressed by a positive\-negative sequence. 59\.76 · Quantity · Add, Subtract

less, minus the state of being less than a given number or quantity In a number of languages it may seem strange as well as illogical to speak of ‘forty lashes minus one.’ Accordingly, one may translate ‘thirty\-nine lashes’ in [2CO 11:24](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:24).

[2CO 11:24](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:24)

10\.9 · Kinship Terms · Groups and Members of Groups of Persons Regarded as Related by Blood but without Special Reference to Successive Generations

**Collocation:** οἱ παρ’ αὐτοῦ

his family (an idiom, literally: those beside him) the associates of a person, including family, neighbors, and friends In some languages the idiom οἱ παρ’ αὐτοῦ is matched by other idiomatic expressions such as ‘those who share the same fire’ or ‘those who eat from the same pot.’

[MRK 3:21](https://ref.ly/Matt3:21)

1 ACT 16:13 Rather than set up a special meaning of παρά as ‘to a point beside,’ it seems far better to understand παρά in [ACT 16:13](https://ref.ly/John16:13) as meaning simply ‘beside’ and to assume an ellipsis of some verb of movement which could be readily omitted in view of the preceding ἐξήλθομεν. 2 There are no doubt certain subtle distinctions in the meanings of the terms in [83\.9](#83.9) (μέσος\[a], ἀνὰ μέσον, ἐντός\[a], ἐν\[b], μετά\[h], εἰς\[e], ἐπί\[c], πρός\[d], παρά\[b]). For example, πρός\[d] may suggest some kind of interaction or response and παρά\[b] may focus upon association, but it is difficult, if not impossible, on the basis of existing contexts to define precisely the differences in meaning. 3 The occurrence of a preposition with a particular case relationship may be described as an instance of a noncontiguous semantic unit in which both features join in signaling the meaning. But it is also possible to analyze such a relationship as one in which a case suffix marks the particular meaning which may be assigned to a preposition. 4 There are undoubtedly certain subtle distinctions of meaning associated with the various markers in [90\.20](#90.20), but it is impossible to determine those differences on the basis of existing contexts. 5 No doubt the meanings in this series (ἀπό\[i], ἐκ\[b], ὅθεν\[b], παρά\[j]) differ somewhat, but it is not possible on the basis of the contexts now available to determine precisely what designative or associative features of meaning may be involved.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 4:18; Matthew 13:1; Matthew 13:4; Matthew 13:19; Matthew 15:29; Matthew 15:30; Matthew 20:30; Mark 1:16; Mark 2:13; Mark 4:1; Mark 4:4; Mark 4:15; Mark 5:21; Mark 10:46; ## παραβαίνω (id: 003747000000000)

παραβαίνω
=========

παραβαίνω (G3845)

aor. παρέβην \| βαίνω (go, walk)

36\.28 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Obey, Disobey

to disobey, to break the law, to transgress, disobedience, transgression to act contrary to established custom or law, with the implication of intent Languages differ appreciably in the way in which they speak of disobeying or transgressing a law. It is rarely possible to speak of ‘breaking a law,’ since nothing is actually done to the law in the process of transgression, but languages do employ such expressions as ‘to tramp on a law,’ ‘to ridicule a law,’ and ‘to laugh at a law.’ See also discussion at [36\.29](#36.29).

[MAT 15:2](https://ref.ly/Matt15:2) · [MAT 15:3](https://ref.ly/Matt15:3)

68\.39 · Aspect · Cease, Stop

to cease, to stop, to give up (a figurative extension of meaning of παραβαίνω ‘to go aside,’ not occurring in the NT) to cease by removing oneself from some activity or position In [ACT 1:25](https://ref.ly/John1:25) διακονίας and ἀποστολῆς are the events to which παρέβη is related as aspect.

[ACT 1:25](https://ref.ly/John1:25)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 15:2; Matthew 15:3; Acts 1:25

παραβάλλω
=========

παραβάλλω (G3846)

aor. παρέβαλον \| βάλλω

to approach, to arrive at, to sail to (a technical, nautical term) to sail up to or near

[ACT 20:15](https://ref.ly/John20:15)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 20:15


## παράβασις (id: 003749000000000)

παράβασις
=========

παράβασις (G3847)

noun \| \-εως \| βαίνω (go, walk)

36\.28 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Obey, Disobey

to disobey, to break the law, to transgress, disobedience, transgression to act contrary to established custom or law, with the implication of intent Languages differ appreciably in the way in which they speak of disobeying or transgressing a law. It is rarely possible to speak of ‘breaking a law,’ since nothing is actually done to the law in the process of transgression, but languages do employ such expressions as ‘to tramp on a law,’ ‘to ridicule a law,’ and ‘to laugh at a law.’ See also discussion at [36\.29](#36.29).

[ROM 2:23](https://ref.ly/Acts2:23) · [ROM 4:15](https://ref.ly/Acts4:15) · [ROM 5:14](https://ref.ly/Acts5:14) · [GAL 3:19](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:19) · [1TI 2:14](https://ref.ly/2Thess2:14) · [HEB 2:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:2) · [HEB 9:15](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:15)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 2:23; Romans 4:15; Romans 5:14; Galatians 3:19; 1 Timothy 2:14; Hebrews 2:2; Hebrews 9:15


## παραβάτης (id: 003750000000000)

παραβάτης
=========

παραβάτης (G3848)

noun \| \-ου \| βαίνω (go, walk)

36\.29 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Obey, Disobey

transgressor (derivative of παραβαίνω\[a] ‘to disobey, to break the law,’ [36\.28](#36.28)) a person who customarily breaks or disobeys the law For terms involving ‘disobedience’ or ‘transgression,’ there are often a number of subtle distinctions reflecting several different types of contrasts. For example, there may be important distinctions between disobeying a person and disobeying a law or custom. A number of languages also make a clear distinction between intentional and unintentional disobedience or transgression. Another distinction may involve repeated activity or consistency of attitude, so that the choice of terms may specify whether a person has consistently disobeyed or has only disobeyed once or twice. A further distinction is sometimes made between disobedience of children or those who are mentally incapacitated and the disobedience of those who should know better. Accordingly, in the choice of terms for various passages of Scripture it is extremely important to note the distinctive features of meaning; otherwise, much of the emphasis upon disobedience and transgression as stated in the Scriptures may be seriously distorted.

[ROM 2:25](https://ref.ly/Acts2:25) · [ROM 2:27](https://ref.ly/Acts2:27) · [GAL 2:18](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:18) · [JAS 2:9](https://ref.ly/Heb2:9) · [JAS 2:11](https://ref.ly/Heb2:11)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 2:25; Romans 2:27; Galatians 2:18; James 2:9; James 2:11

to urge to speak in such a way as to encourage a particular type of behavior or action

=============

βολεύομαι

21\.7 · Danger, Risk, Safe, Save · Expose Oneself to Danger

to risk, to risk one’s life to expose oneself willingly to a danger or risk In a number of languages the equivalent of ‘risking one’s life’ is ‘exposing oneself to death’ or ‘showing that one is willing to die’ or ‘walking into the danger of dying.’

## παραβολή (id: 003753000000000)

παραβολή
========

παραβολή (G3850)

noun \| \-ῆς \| βολεύομαι

33\.15 · Communication · Discourse Types

parable, figure, allegory, figure of speech a relatively short narrative with symbolic meaning In almost all languages there is some way of speaking about parables or allegories. The equivalent in some languages may be ‘a likeness story’ or ‘a story that teaches’ or ‘a story that points the way’ or ‘words that have another meaning’ or ‘words that are saying something else important.’  
Some scholars have insisted that in the NT παραβολή is used only in the sense of a story with one level of symbolic meaning, but in some contexts παραβολή is also used to identify allegories in which each element of the account has a symbolic significance. It is therefore extremely difficult to make a rigid distinction between parables and allegories.

[MAT 13:3](https://ref.ly/Matt13:3) · [MAT 13:10](https://ref.ly/Matt13:10) · [MAT 13:13](https://ref.ly/Matt13:13) · [MAT 13:18](https://ref.ly/Matt13:18) · [MAT 13:31](https://ref.ly/Matt13:31) · [MAT 13:33](https://ref.ly/Matt13:33) · [MAT 13:34](https://ref.ly/Matt13:34) · [MAT 13:34](https://ref.ly/Matt13:34) · [MAT 13:35](https://ref.ly/Matt13:35) · [MAT 13:36](https://ref.ly/Matt13:36) · [MAT 13:53](https://ref.ly/Matt13:53) · [MAT 15:15](https://ref.ly/Matt15:15) · [MAT 21:33](https://ref.ly/Matt21:33) · [MAT 21:45](https://ref.ly/Matt21:45) · [MAT 22:1](https://ref.ly/Matt22:1) · [MAT 24:32](https://ref.ly/Matt24:32) · [MRK 3:23](https://ref.ly/Matt3:23) · [MRK 4:2](https://ref.ly/Matt4:2) · [MRK 4:10](https://ref.ly/Matt4:10) · [MRK 4:11](https://ref.ly/Matt4:11) · [MRK 4:13](https://ref.ly/Matt4:13) · [MRK 4:13](https://ref.ly/Matt4:13) · [MRK 4:30](https://ref.ly/Matt4:30) · [MRK 4:33](https://ref.ly/Matt4:33) · [MRK 4:34](https://ref.ly/Matt4:34) · [MRK 7:17](https://ref.ly/Matt7:17) · [MRK 12:1](https://ref.ly/Matt12:1) · [MRK 12:12](https://ref.ly/Matt12:12) · [MRK 13:28](https://ref.ly/Matt13:28) · [LUK 4:23](https://ref.ly/Mark4:23) · [LUK 5:36](https://ref.ly/Mark5:36) · [LUK 6:39](https://ref.ly/Mark6:39) · [LUK 8:4](https://ref.ly/Mark8:4) · [LUK 8:9](https://ref.ly/Mark8:9) · [LUK 8:10](https://ref.ly/Mark8:10) · [LUK 8:11](https://ref.ly/Mark8:11) · [LUK 12:16](https://ref.ly/Mark12:16) · [LUK 12:41](https://ref.ly/Mark12:41) · [LUK 13:6](https://ref.ly/Mark13:6) · [LUK 14:7](https://ref.ly/Mark14:7) · [LUK 15:3](https://ref.ly/Mark15:3) · [LUK 18:1](https://ref.ly/Mark18:1) · [LUK 18:9](https://ref.ly/Mark18:9) · [LUK 19:11](https://ref.ly/Mark19:11) · [LUK 20:9](https://ref.ly/Mark20:9) · [LUK 20:19](https://ref.ly/Mark20:19) · [LUK 21:29](https://ref.ly/Mark21:29) · [HEB 9:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:9)

58\.63 · Nature, Class, Example · Archetype, Corresponding Type \[Antitype]

archetype, figure, foreshadow, symbol a model or example which anticipates or precedes a later realization In a number of languages it may be difficult to find a lexical item equivalent to ‘symbol,’ but one can often employ a relatively close paraphrase. For example, in referring to certain aspects of the tabernacle as being a παραβολή, one may render the first clause of [HEB 9:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:9) as ‘this says something to us about the present time.’ In other languages one may sometimes use a term which means ‘picture,’ for example, ‘this is a picture for the present time’ or ‘this contains some meaning for the present time.’

[HEB 9:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:9) · [HEB 11:19](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:19)

33\.16 · Communication · Discourse Types

**Collocation:** παρατίθημι τὴν παραβολήν

to tell a parable, to utter a parable an idiom, literally: to place alongside a parable

[MAT 13:24](https://ref.ly/Matt13:24)1

33\.17 · Communication · Discourse Types

**Collocation:** ἐν παραβολῇ

symbolically, allegorically, figuratively, so to speak pertaining to expressions which are not to be understood literally, but symbolically or figuratively In a number of languages it may be more satisfactory to translate ἐν παραβολῇ and πνευματικῶς\[c] by a negative expression, for example, ‘not literally’ or ‘not really.’ The expression in [REV 11:8](https://ref.ly/Jude11:8) might be translated as ‘that city might be called Sodom, but it isn’t really Sodom.’

[HEB 11:19](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:19)

1 MAT 13:24 It would be possible to interpret παρατίθημι τὴν παραβολήν as meaning ‘to put a parable to,’ in other words, ‘to address a parable to someone.’ This interpretation is possible in view of the following participle λέγων, but the occurrence of such a participle to introduce direct discourse is frequent with expressions of speaking.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 13:3; Matthew 13:10; Matthew 13:13; Matthew 13:18; Matthew 13:31; Matthew 13:33; Matthew 13:34; Matthew 13:35; Matthew 13:36; Matthew 13:53; Matthew 15:15; Matthew 21:33; Matthew 21:45; Matthew 22:1; Matthew 24:32; Mark 3:23; Mark 4:2; Mark 4:10; Mark 4:11; Mark 4:13; Mark 4:30; Mark 4:33; Mark 4:34; Mark 7:17; Mark 12:1; Mark 12:12; Mark 13:28; Luke 4:23; Luke 5:36; Luke 6:39; Luke 8:4; Luke 8:9; Luke 8:10; Luke 8:11; Luke 12:16; Luke 12:41; Luke 13:6; Luke 14:7; Luke 15:3; Luke 18:1; Luke 18:9; Luke 19:11; Luke 20:9; Luke 20:19; Luke 21:29; Hebrews 9:9; Hebrews 11:19; Matthew 13:24

## παράγω (id: 003757000000000)

παράγω
======

παράγω (G3855)

1 It is possible that there are slight distinctions in meaning between παρέρχομαι\[a], παραπορεύομαι, and παράγω\[b] in view of certain distinctions of meaning in ἔρχομαι, πορεύομαι, and ἄγω (especially in the sense of ὑπάγω). παρέρχομαι\[a] may therefore be judged to be more generic in meaning, while παραπορεύομαι may refer specifically to walking along, and παράγω\[b] may involve primarily the act of passing beyond a particular point.

## παράδεισος (id: 003759000000000)

παράδεισος
==========

παράδεισος (G3857)

noun \| \-ου

1\.14 · Geographical Objects and Features · Regions Above the Earth

paradise a dwelling place of the righteous dead in a state of blessedness (generally equated with οὐρανός\[b] ‘heaven,’ [1\.11](#1.11)) In some languages ‘paradise’ has been translated by a borrowed term, but more often than not it is rendered by an expression which is roughly equivalent to ‘that wonderful place’ or even ‘that wonderful place in heaven.’  
In [REV 2:7](https://ref.ly/Jude2:7) ἐν τῷ παραδείσῳ τοῦ θεοῦ ‘in the paradise of God,’ the reference may reflect somewhat more closely the historical background of this term, which is derived from an Old Persian word meaning ‘enclosure,’ and thus was applied to a ‘garden’ or ‘park.’ For that reason, a number of commentators have believed that in [REV 2:7](https://ref.ly/Jude2:7), it is appropriate to translate ‘the garden of God,’ especially since in the context the reference is to the fruit of the tree of life.

[LUK 23:43](https://ref.ly/Mark23:43) · [2CO 12:4](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:4) · [REV 2:7](https://ref.ly/Jude2:7)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 23:43; 2 Corinthians 12:4; Revelation 2:7


## παραδέχομαι (id: 003760000000000)

παραδέχομαι
===========

παραδέχομαι (G3858)

δέχομαι

31\.52 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Accept As True

to accept, to receive, acceptance, reception to come to believe something to be true and to respond accordingly, with some emphasis upon the source

[MRK 4:20](https://ref.ly/Matt4:20) · [ACT 16:21](https://ref.ly/John16:21) · [ACT 22:18](https://ref.ly/John22:18) · [1TI 5:19](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:19) · [HEB 12:6](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:6)

34\.53 · Association · Welcome, Receive

to welcome, to receive, to accept, to have as a guest to accept the presence of a person with friendliness1

[ACT 15:4](https://ref.ly/John15:4)

1 There are no doubt certain contextual constraints involved in the choice of ἀναδέχομαι\[a], ἀποδέχομαι\[a], ἐπιδέχομαι\[a], εἰσδέχομαι, ὑποδέχομαι, προσδέχομαι\[b], and παραδέχομαι\[b] in the sense that the spacial relationships involved in the welcoming of a guest can be reflected in the prepositional prefixes. For example, in [LUK 8:40](https://ref.ly/Mark8:40) the use of ἀποδέχομαι may focus upon the particular source, while ἐπιδέχομαι in [3JN 1:10](https://ref.ly/2John1:10) may be relevant in view of the fact that the believers are coming to a particular point. προσδέχομαι in [LUK 15:2](https://ref.ly/Mark15:2) may suggest a kind of reciprocal relationship which is established with outcasts, and παραδέχομαι in [ACT 15:4](https://ref.ly/John15:4) may simply point to spacial proximity. However, it would be wrong to insist upon clear distinctions in meaning based on the very limited number of contexts involved. Some of these same distinctions in spacial relations may exist for the set προσλαμβάνομαι\[e], παραλαμβάνω\[d], and ἀπολαμβάνω\[d].

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 4:20; Acts 16:21; Acts 22:18; 1 Timothy 5:19; Hebrews 12:6; Acts 15:4

παραδίδωμι
==========

παραδίδωμι (G3860)

pres.2sg. παραδίδως · subj.3sg. παραδιδῷ, παραδιδοῖ · inf. παραδιδόναι · ptc. παραδιδούς · impf.3sg. παρεδίδου · 3pl. παρεδίδουν, παρεδίδοσαν · fut. παραδώσω · aor. παρέδωκα · 3pl. παρέδωκαν, παρέδοσαν · subj.3sg. παραδῷ, παραδοῖ · inf. παραδοῦναι · ptc. παραδούς · pf.ptc. παραδεδωκώς · plpf.3pl. παραδεδώκεισαν · pf.midd. παραδέδομαι · aor.pass. παρεδόθην · subj. παραδοθῶ · inf. παραδοθῆναι · ptc. παραδοθείς · fut.pass. παραδοθήσομαι \| δίδωμι

57\.77 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Give

to give over, to hand over to hand over to or to convey something to someone, particularly a right or an authority In some languages, however, it is impossible to speak of ‘handing over authority.’ In some instances one may use a causative expression, for example, ‘to cause someone to have.’

[MAT 11:27](https://ref.ly/Matt11:27) · [MAT 25:14](https://ref.ly/Matt25:14) · [MAT 25:20](https://ref.ly/Matt25:20) · [MAT 25:22](https://ref.ly/Matt25:22) · [LUK 4:6](https://ref.ly/Mark4:6) · [LUK 10:22](https://ref.ly/Mark10:22) · [ACT 14:26](https://ref.ly/John14:26) · [ACT 15:40](https://ref.ly/John15:40) · [1CO 13:3](https://ref.ly/Rom13:3) · [1CO 15:24](https://ref.ly/Rom15:24) · [2CO 4:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:11) · [GAL 2:20](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:20) · [EPH 4:19](https://ref.ly/Gal4:19) · [1PE 2:23](https://ref.ly/Jas2:23) · [JUD 1:3](https://ref.ly/3John1:3)

37\.111 · Control, Rule · Hand Over, Betray

to hand over, to turn over to, to betray to deliver a person into the control of someone else, involving either the handing over of a presumably guilty person for punishment by authorities or the handing over of an individual to an enemy who will presumably take undue advantage of the victim As is the case in English, a number of languages make a clear distinction between legitimate handing over of a presumably guilty person to a civil authority and the betrayal of a person in the in\-group to someone in the out\-group.

[MRK 4:29](https://ref.ly/Matt4:29)

37\.12 · Control, Rule · Control, Restrain

**Collocation:** παραδίδωμι εἰς χεῖρας

to deliver to the control of, to hand over to (an idiom, literally: to give into the hands) to hand someone over into the control of others In a context such as [MAT 26:45](https://ref.ly/Matt26:45), παραδίδοται εἰς χεῖρας may be rendered as ‘will hand him over to be arrested’ or ‘will cause him to be taken into custody.’

[MAT 17:22](https://ref.ly/Matt17:22) · [MAT 26:45](https://ref.ly/Matt26:45) · [MRK 9:31](https://ref.ly/Matt9:31) · [MRK 14:41](https://ref.ly/Matt14:41) · [LUK 9:44](https://ref.ly/Mark9:44) · [LUK 24:7](https://ref.ly/Mark24:7) · [ACT 21:11](https://ref.ly/John21:11) · [ACT 28:17](https://ref.ly/John28:17)

21\.7 · Danger, Risk, Safe, Save · Expose Oneself to Danger

**Collocation:** παραδίδωμι τὴν ψυχήν

to risk, to risk one’s life (an idiom, literally: to hand over life) to expose oneself willingly to a danger or risk In a number of languages the equivalent of ‘risking one’s life’ is ‘exposing oneself to death’ or ‘showing that one is willing to die’ or ‘walking into the danger of dying.’

[ACT 15:26](https://ref.ly/John15:26)

23\.110 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

**Collocation:** παραδίδωμι τὸ πνεῦμα

to die (an idiom, literally: to give over the spirit) to die, with the possible implication of a willing or voluntary act

[JHN 19:30](https://ref.ly/Luke19:30)

## παράδοξος (id: 003762000000000)

παράδοξος
=========

παράδοξος (G3861)

adj \| \-ον \| δοκέω

31\.44 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Believe To Be True

difficult to believe, unusual, incredible pertaining to being difficult to be believed It would also be possible to translate εἴδομεν παράδοξα σήμερον as ‘we have seen today things we can hardly believe’ or even ‘... things which people would scarcely believe.’ For another interpretation of παράδοξος in [LUK 5:26](https://ref.ly/Mark5:26), see [58\.56](#58.56).

[LUK 5:26](https://ref.ly/Mark5:26)

58\.56 · Nature, Class, Example · Unusual, Different From the Ordinary

unusual, remarkable pertaining to that which is unusual in the sense of contrary to expectations For another interpretation of παράδοξος in [LUK 5:26](https://ref.ly/Mark5:26), see [31\.44](#31.44).

[LUK 5:26](https://ref.ly/Mark5:26)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 5:26

## παραιτέομαι (id: 003769000000000)

παραιτέομαι
===========

impf. παρῃτούμην · pf.ptc. παρῃτημένος \| αἰτέω

33\.163 · Communication · Ask For, Request

to ask for, to demand, to plead for to ask for with urgency, even to the point of demanding1

[MRK 15:6](https://ref.ly/Matt15:6) · [LUK 14:18](https://ref.ly/Mark14:18) · [LUK 14:18](https://ref.ly/Mark14:18) · [LUK 14:19](https://ref.ly/Mark14:19) · [HEB 12:19](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:19)

27\.60 · Learn · Be Ready To Learn, Pay Attention

to refuse to pay attention to, to avoid, to pay no attention to to not pay attention to

[1TI 4:7](https://ref.ly/2Thess4:7) · [2TI 2:23](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:23)

34\.35 · Association · Belong To, Be Included in the Membership of, Be Excluded From

to refuse to accept, to reject to refuse to accept one into a particular association A strictly literal translation of παραιτέομαι in this context might be misunderstood, since it could suggest that younger widows were to be rejected from membership in the church. It may therefore be necessary to specify the particular relationships by translating ‘but do not accept younger widows in the list of those to receive support from the church.’

[1TI 5:11](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:11)

34\.41 · Association · Limit or Avoid Association

to shun, to avoid, to keep away from, to have nothing to do with purposely to avoid association with someone2

[ACT 25:11](https://ref.ly/John25:11) · [TIT 3:10](https://ref.ly/2Tim3:10)

36\.27 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Obey, Disobey

to refuse to listen, to refuse to obey, disobedience to refuse to listen to and hence to disobey It would be a mistake to translate παρακούω\[a], παρακοή, and παραιτέομαι\[e] as meaning nothing more than ‘not to listen to.’ The meaning is clearly ‘to refuse to listen to’ or ‘to refuse to pay attention to,’ and hence ‘to disobey.’ It may therefore be best in a number of instances to translate ‘to refuse to obey.’

[HEB 12:25](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:25) · [HEB 12:25](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:25)

1 It is possible that παραιτέομαι\[a] differs somewhat from αἰτέω, both in intensity as well as in focusing upon the source from which the grant is to be made. 2 There are probably certain differences of meaning in the terms in [34\.41](#34.41), but without more distinctive contexts, it is difficult to determine the precise areas of meaning.

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 15:6; Luke 14:18; Luke 14:19; Hebrews 12:19; 1 Timothy 4:7; 2 Timothy 2:23; 1 Timothy 5:11; Acts 25:11; Titus 3:10; Hebrews 12:25

## παράκλητος (id: 003775000000000)

παράκλητος
==========

παράκλητος (G3875)

noun \| \-ου \| καλέω

12\.19 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

Helper, Encourager, Mediator (a title for the Holy Spirit) one who helps, by consoling, encouraging, or mediating on behalf of The principal difficulty encountered in rendering παράκλητος is the fact that this term covers potentially such a wide area of meaning. The traditional rendering of ‘Comforter’ is especially misleading because it suggests only one very limited aspect of what the Holy Spirit does. A term such as ‘Helper’ is highly generic and can be particularly useful in some languages. In certain instances, for example, the concept of ‘Helper’ is expressed idiomatically, for example, ‘the one who mothers us’ or, as in one language in Central Africa, ‘the one who falls down beside us,’ that is to say, an individual who upon finding a person collapsed along the road, kneels down beside the victim, cares for his needs, and carries him to safety.  
A rendering based upon the concept of legal advocate seems in most instances to be too restrictive. Furthermore, there may be quite unsatisfactory connotations associated with any word which suggests a lawyer, especially since in so many societies, a lawyer is thought of primarily as one who ‘bribes the judges’ or ‘can speak two truths’ or, as in one language, is ‘a professional liar.’ See also παράκλητος [35\.16](#35.16) and especially its footnote.

[JHN 14:16](https://ref.ly/Luke14:16) · [JHN 14:26](https://ref.ly/Luke14:26) · [JHN 15:26](https://ref.ly/Luke15:26) · [JHN 16:7](https://ref.ly/Luke16:7)

35\.16 · Help, Care For · Help

helper (derivative of παρακαλέω ‘to call upon to provide help,’ not occurring in this specific sense in the NT) one who may be called upon to provide help or assistance1

[1JN 2:1](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:1)

1 The meaning of παράκλητος overlaps considerably with other domains. It certainly involves psychological factors of encouragement and therefore may very well be treated in Domain 25 Attitudes and Emotions. There is also a sense in which παράκλητος inevitably involves some communication, and therefore it may fittingly be analyzed in Domain 33 Communication. The role of intercession has certain legal implications, and accordingly, παράκλητος is justifiably related to legal procedures in Domain 56 Courts and Legal Procedures. It is very rare indeed that any one term in a receptor language will have all of the distinctive features of meaning possessed by παράκλητος, especially in reference to the role of the Holy Spirit (see [12\.19](#12.19)).

* **Associated Passages:** John 14:16; John 14:26; John 15:26; John 16:7; 1 John 2:1


## παρακοή (id: 003776000000000)

παρακοή
=======

παρακοή (G3876)

noun \| \-ῆς \| ἀκούω

36\.27 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Obey, Disobey

to refuse to listen, to refuse to obey, disobedience to refuse to listen to and hence to disobey It would be a mistake to translate παρακούω\[a], παρακοή, and παραιτέομαι\[e] as meaning nothing more than ‘not to listen to.’ The meaning is clearly ‘to refuse to listen to’ or ‘to refuse to pay attention to,’ and hence ‘to disobey.’ It may therefore be best in a number of instances to translate ‘to refuse to obey.’

[ROM 5:19](https://ref.ly/Acts5:19) · [2CO 10:6](https://ref.ly/1Cor10:6) · [HEB 2:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:2)

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 5:19; 2 Corinthians 10:6; Hebrews 2:2

to refuse to listen, to refuse to obey, disobedience to refuse to listen to and hence to disobey It would be a mistake to translate παρακούω\[a], παρακοή, and παραιτέομαι\[e] as meaning nothing more than ‘not to listen to.’ The meaning is clearly ‘to refuse to listen to’ or ‘to refuse to pay attention to,’ and hence ‘to disobey.’ It may therefore be best in a number of instances to translate ‘to refuse to obey.’

## παρακύπτω (id: 003779000000000)

παρακύπτω
=========

παρακύπτω (G3879)

κύπτω

17\.31 · Stances and Events Related to Stances · Bend Over, Straighten Up

to bend over, to stoop down to bend over or stoop down, with the implication of looking into something1

[LUK 24:12](https://ref.ly/Mark24:12) · [JHN 20:5](https://ref.ly/Luke20:5) · [JHN 20:11](https://ref.ly/Luke20:11)

24\.13 · Sensory Events and States · See

to stoop and look into to look into something by stooping down2

[LUK 24:12](https://ref.ly/Mark24:12) · [JHN 20:11](https://ref.ly/Luke20:11) · [JAS 1:25](https://ref.ly/Heb1:25)

27\.39 · Learn · Try To Learn

to try to learn, to desire to learn (a figurative extension of meaning of παρακύπτω\[b] ‘to stoop and look into,’ [24\.13](#24.13)) to make considerable effort in order to try to find out something

[JAS 1:25](https://ref.ly/Heb1:25) · [1PE 1:12](https://ref.ly/Jas1:12)

1 In παρακύπτω\[a] there appear to be two coordinate semantic elements: ‘to stoop down’ and ‘to look into.’ It is possible, of course, to understand the second element as implying the purpose for the first, but in translation it is essential that both elements, especially in the context of [JHN 20:11](https://ref.ly/Luke20:11), be adequately noted. The fact that there are two significant elements of meaning in παρακύπτω\[a] means that this term is essentially a member of two different domains at the same time, namely, Domain 17 dealing with Stances and Domain 24 Sensory Events and States. 2 Since παρακύπτω, particularly in the context of [JHN 20:11](https://ref.ly/Luke20:11), involves two quite distinct semantic features, namely ‘to stoop down’ and ‘to look into,’ it may be classified either as a particular stance ([17\.31](#17.31)) with the added element of seeing, or one may classify παρακύπτω as here, in the sensory subdomain of ‘seeing,’ with an adjunct feature of ‘stooping down.’ See also [17\.31](#17.31) and footnote.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 24:12; John 20:5; John 20:11; James 1:25; 1 Peter 1:12

[MAT 1:20](https://ref.ly/Matt1:20) · [MAT 1:24](https://ref.ly/Matt1:24) · [JHN 1:11](https://ref.ly/Luke1:11) · [JHN 14:3](https://ref.ly/Luke14:3) · [COL 2:6](https://ref.ly/Phil2:6)

37\.99 · Control, Rule · Assign to a Role or Function

to receive an appointment, to receive a task, to be assigned a ministry to receive an appointment for a particular ministry

[COL 4:17](https://ref.ly/Phil4:17)

33\.238 · Communication · Teach

to receive instruction from, to be taught by to receive traditional instruction

[1CO 11:23](https://ref.ly/Rom11:23) · [1CO 15:1](https://ref.ly/Rom15:1) · [1TH 2:13](https://ref.ly/Col2:13) · [HEB 12:28](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:28)

1 There are no doubt certain contextual constraints involved in the choice of ἀναδέχομαι\[a], ἀποδέχομαι\[a], ἐπιδέχομαι\[a], εἰσδέχομαι, ὑποδέχομαι, προσδέχομαι\[b], and παραδέχομαι\[b] in the sense that the spacial relationships involved in the welcoming of a guest can be reflected in the prepositional prefixes. For example, in [LUK 8:40](https://ref.ly/Mark8:40) the use of ἀποδέχομαι may focus upon the particular source, while ἐπιδέχομαι in [3JN 1:10](https://ref.ly/2John1:10) may be relevant in view of the fact that the believers are coming to a particular point. προσδέχομαι in [LUK 15:2](https://ref.ly/Mark15:2) may suggest a kind of reciprocal relationship which is established with outcasts, and παραδέχομαι in [ACT 15:4](https://ref.ly/John15:4) may simply point to spacial proximity. However, it would be wrong to insist upon clear distinctions in meaning based on the very limited number of contexts involved. Some of these same distinctions in spacial relations may exist for the set προσλαμβάνω\[e], παραλαμβάνω\[d], ἀπολαμβάνω\[d].

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 2:13; Matthew 2:14; Matthew 2:20; Matthew 2:21; Matthew 4:5; Matthew 4:8; Matthew 12:45; Matthew 17:1; Matthew 18:16; Matthew 24:40; Matthew 24:41; Matthew 26:37; Matthew 27:27; Mark 4:36; Mark 5:40; Mark 9:2; Mark 14:33; Luke 9:10; Luke 9:28; Luke 11:26; Luke 17:34; Luke 17:35; John 19:16; Acts 15:39; Acts 16:33; Acts 21:24; Acts 21:26; Acts 21:32; Acts 23:18; Matthew 20:17; Mark 10:32; Luke 18:31; Mark 7:4; 1 Corinthians 15:3; Galatians 1:9; Galatians 1:12; Philippians 4:9; Colossians 2:6; 1 Thessalonians 2:13; 1 Thessalonians 4:1; 2 Thessalonians 3:6; Matthew 1:20; Matthew 1:24; John 1:11; John 14:3; Colossians 4:17; 1 Corinthians 11:23; 1 Corinthians 15:1; Hebrews 12:28

## παραλύω (id: 003786000000000)

παραλύω
=======

παραλύω (G3886) — παραλύομαι

λύω

23\.170 · (παραλύομαι\[a]) · Physiological Processes and States · Sickness, Disease, Weakness

to be paralyzed, to be lame to suffer paralysis in one or more limbs, especially in the leg or foot In [HEB 12:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:12) παραλύομαι occurs in a highly figurative context: τὰ παραλελυμένα γόνατα ἀνορθώσατε ‘straighten up the paralyzed knees.’ It is often better in this context to speak of ‘strengthening the weak knees,’ but in any event, the reference to the physical situation of the individual is merely a way of speaking about a psychological state (see [25\.152](#25.152)).

[LUK 5:18](https://ref.ly/Mark5:18) · [LUK 5:24](https://ref.ly/Mark5:24) · [ACT 8:7](https://ref.ly/John8:7) · [ACT 9:33](https://ref.ly/John9:33) · [HEB 12:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:12)

25\.152 · (παραλύομαι\[b]) · Attitudes and Emotions · Encouragement, Consolation

**Collocation:** τὰ παραλελυμένα γόνατα ἀνορθόω

to become encouraged, to encourage oneself by determination (an idiom, literally: straighten paralyzed knees) to gain encouragement by a strong resolve See discussion at [23\.170](#23.170).

[HEB 12:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:12)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 5:18; Luke 5:24; Acts 8:7; Acts 9:33; Hebrews 12:12


## παραμένω (id: 003787000000000)

παραμένω
========

παραμένω (G3887)

aor.ptc. παραμείνας \| μένω

85\.56 · Existence in Space · Remain, Stay

to stay with, to remain with to stay in a place together with someone In [PHP 1:25](https://ref.ly/Eph1:25) παραμενῶ contrasts to some extent with μενῶ. The implication, in this particular context at least, is that the purpose of remaining is to serve the interests or needs of the believers in Philippi. It is also possible that some of this implication of serving may be found in [1CO 16:6](https://ref.ly/Rom16:6).

[1CO 16:6](https://ref.ly/Rom16:6) · [PHP 1:25](https://ref.ly/Eph1:25)

to continue, to remain in, to keep on to continue in an activity or state

## παρανομέω (id: 003791000000000)

παρανομέω
=========

παρανομέω (G3891)

νομίζω

36\.28 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Obey, Disobey

to disobey, to break the law, to transgress, disobedience, transgression to act contrary to established custom or law, with the implication of intent Languages differ appreciably in the way in which they speak of disobeying or transgressing a law. It is rarely possible to speak of ‘breaking a law,’ since nothing is actually done to the law in the process of transgression, but languages do employ such expressions as ‘to tramp on a law,’ ‘to ridicule a law,’ and ‘to laugh at a law.’ See also discussion at [36\.29](#36.29).

[ACT 23:3](https://ref.ly/John23:3)

=============

πορεύομαι

15\.28 · Linear Movement · Pass, Cross Over, Go Through, Go Around

to pass by, to go by to move past a reference point1

[MAT 27:39](https://ref.ly/Matt27:39) · [MRK 2:23](https://ref.ly/Matt2:23) · [MRK 9:30](https://ref.ly/Matt9:30) · [MRK 11:20](https://ref.ly/Matt11:20) · [MRK 15:29](https://ref.ly/Matt15:29)

1 It is possible that there are slight distinctions in meaning between παρέρχομαι\[a], παραπορεύομαι, and παράγω\[b] in view of certain distinctions of meaning in ἔρχομαι, πορεύομαι, and ἄγω (especially in the sense of ὑπάγω). παρέρχομαι\[a] may therefore be judged to be more generic in meaning, while παραπορεύομαι may refer specifically to walking along, and παράγω\[b] may involve primarily the act of passing beyond a particular point.

transgression, sin what a person has done in transgressing the will and law of God by some false step or failure1

[MAT 6:14](https://ref.ly/Matt6:14) · [MAT 6:15](https://ref.ly/Matt6:15) · [MRK 11:25](https://ref.ly/Matt11:25) · [ROM 4:25](https://ref.ly/Acts4:25) · [ROM 5:15](https://ref.ly/Acts5:15) · [ROM 5:15](https://ref.ly/Acts5:15) · [ROM 5:16](https://ref.ly/Acts5:16) · [ROM 5:17](https://ref.ly/Acts5:17) · [ROM 5:18](https://ref.ly/Acts5:18) · [ROM 5:20](https://ref.ly/Acts5:20) · [ROM 11:11](https://ref.ly/Acts11:11) · [ROM 11:12](https://ref.ly/Acts11:12) · [2CO 5:19](https://ref.ly/1Cor5:19) · [GAL 6:1](https://ref.ly/2Cor6:1) · [EPH 1:7](https://ref.ly/Gal1:7) · [EPH 2:1](https://ref.ly/Gal2:1) · [EPH 2:5](https://ref.ly/Gal2:5) · [COL 2:13](https://ref.ly/Phil2:13) · [COL 2:13](https://ref.ly/Phil2:13)

1 παράπτωμα is used in a number of contexts in a way in which it appears to be equivalent in meaning to ἁμάρτημα ([88\.290](#88.290)) or ἁμαρτία\[a] ([88\.289](#88.289)), but it is possible that παράπτωμα focuses more upon unpremeditated violation of God’s will and law.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 6:14; Matthew 6:15; Mark 11:25; Romans 4:25; Romans 5:15; Romans 5:16; Romans 5:17; Romans 5:18; Romans 5:20; Romans 11:11; Romans 11:12; 2 Corinthians 5:19; Galatians 6:1; Ephesians 1:7; Ephesians 2:1; Ephesians 2:5; Colossians 2:13

παραφρονέω
==========

παραφρονέω (G3912)

φρονέω

30\.24 · Think · To Think, Thought

to not be in one’s right mind, to be insane, to be mad, to be out of one’s mind, insanity, madness to think or reason in a completely irrational manner1

[2CO 11:23](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:23)

1 It is possible that there are some significant differences in meaning between the set μαίνομαι / μανία and the set παραφρονέω / παραφρονία, but the difference is likely to be more connotative than denotative, as, for example, the distinction in English between crazy and insane.

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Corinthians 11:23


## παραφρονία (id: 003813000000000)

παραφρονία
==========

παραφρονία (G3913)

noun \| \-ας \| φρονέω

30\.24 · Think · To Think, Thought

to not be in one’s right mind, to be insane, to be mad, to be out of one’s mind, insanity, madness to think or reason in a completely irrational manner1 It may be necessary in some languages to restructure the expression ‘stopped the prophet’s insane action.’ This can often be done by saying ‘prevented the prophet from acting like a crazy person.’ In other languages παραφρονία is best rendered by an idiomatic expression such as ‘wandering thoughts’ or ‘twisted thoughts.’

[2PE 2:16](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:16)

1 It is possible that there are some significant differences in meaning between the set μαίνομαι / μανία and the set παραφρονέω / παραφρονία, but the difference is likely to be more connotative than denotative, as, for example, the distinction in English between crazy and insane.

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Peter 2:16

adv \| χράομαι

suddenly, at once, immediately pertaining to an extremely short period of time between a previous state or event and a subsequent state or event In a number of contexts there is the implication of unexpectedness, but this seems to be a derivative of the context as a whole and not a part of the meaning of the lexical items.

## πάρδαλις (id: 003817000000000)

πάρδαλις
========

πάρδαλις (G3917)

noun \| \-εως

4\.13 · Animals · Animals

leopard refers to two members of the large cat family: Panthera pardus \- leopard; about 2 m in length and yellowish brown with black spots arranged in rosettes all over the body and tail; hunts by stalking prey; and Acinonyx jubatus \- cheetah; spotted but are slightly smaller and have longer legs than leopards; extremely fast; symbol of violent danger or of speed (cheetah) As in the case of the term for ‘bear’ ([4\.12](#4.12)), this reference to a leopard occurs in the NT only in [REV 13:2](https://ref.ly/Jude13:2) and is likewise used as a means of describing the apocalyptic beast. A term referring to a local type of leopard or jaguar would be perfectly appropriate, and in some languages a term referring to a mountain lion has been employed. In other instances the equivalent expression is based upon a phrase meaning ‘fierce, large, cat\-like animal.’ A borrowed term may also be employed with a descriptive classifier, for example, ‘an animal called leopard.’

[REV 13:2](https://ref.ly/Jude13:2)

* **Associated Passages:** Revelation 13:2

=========

noun \| \-ῆς \| βάλλω

1\.94 · Geographical Objects and Features · Population Centers

camp a temporary population center consisting of an encampment1 The equivalent of ‘camp’ in the context of [HEB 13:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:11) is often ‘a city of tents’ or ‘where people lived only for a short time.’

[HEB 11:34](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:34) · [HEB 13:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:11) · [HEB 13:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:13) · [REV 20:9](https://ref.ly/Jude20:9)

7\.22 · Constructions · Buildings

soldiers quarters, barracks a camp or barracks for soldiers2 In the NT παρεμβολή\[b] is used to refer to the place where Roman troops were housed in Jerusalem and to the place in Rome where soldiers who accompanied Paul were quartered. Though many languages have a set phrase or technical expression for soldiers barracks, it may be necessary in some cases to use a descriptive phrase, for example, ‘where soldiers lived or where soldiers lived together.’

1 Compare the meaning of παρεμβολή\[b] ([7\.22](#7.22)) as a construction consisting of barracks for soldiers. 2 παρεμβολή\[b] ‘barracks’ differs from παρεμβολή\[a] ‘camp’ ([1\.94](#1.94)) in that the latter is a temporary population center and not the actual construction of barracks or soldiers quarters.

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 11:34; Hebrews 13:11; Hebrews 13:13; Revelation 20:9; Acts 21:34; Acts 21:37; Acts 22:24; Acts 23:10; Acts 23:16; Acts 23:32

36\.28 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Obey, Disobey

to disobey, to break the law, to transgress, disobedience, transgression to act contrary to established custom or law, with the implication of intent Languages differ appreciably in the way in which they speak of disobeying or transgressing a law. It is rarely possible to speak of ‘breaking a law,’ since nothing is actually done to the law in the process of transgression, but languages do employ such expressions as ‘to tramp on a law,’ ‘to ridicule a law,’ and ‘to laugh at a law.’ See also discussion at [36\.29](#36.29).

to cease to exist, to pass away, to cease to go out of existence

67\.85 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

to pass, to have passed, past to mark the passage of time, with focus upon completion In [MAT 24:34](https://ref.ly/Matt24:34) it may be important to distinguish carefully between γενεά\[a] meaning ‘people’ ([11\.4](#11.4)) and γενεά\[d] meaning ‘time’ ([67\.144](#67.144)), since this will undoubtedly influence the way in which the temporal reference of παρέρχομαι\[e] is represented. If one understands generation in the sense of ‘people,’ then one may translate ‘these people living now will not die until all these things happen.’

[MAT 14:15](https://ref.ly/Matt14:15) · [MAT 24:34](https://ref.ly/Matt24:34) · [MRK 13:30](https://ref.ly/Matt13:30) · [LUK 21:32](https://ref.ly/Mark21:32) · [ACT 27:9](https://ref.ly/John27:9) · [1PE 4:3](https://ref.ly/Jas4:3)

1 It is possible that there are slight distinctions in meaning between παρέρχομαι\[a], παραπορεύομαι, and παράγω\[b] in view of certain distinctions of meaning in ἔρχομαι, πορεύομαι, and ἄγω (especially in the sense of ὑπάγω). παρέρχομαι\[a] may therefore be judged to be more generic in meaning, while παραπορεύομαι may refer specifically to walking along, and παράγω\[b] may involve primarily the act of passing beyond a particular point.
