## παρθένος (id: 003835000000000)

παρθένος
========

παρθένος (G3933)

noun \| \-ου \| παρθενία

9\.39 · People · Females

virgin, young woman a female person beyond puberty but not yet married and a virgin (though in some contexts virginity is not a focal component of meaning) In [ACT 21:9](https://ref.ly/John21:9) (τούτῳ δὲ ἦσαν θυγατέρες τέσσαρες παρθένοι ‘he had four virgin daughters’) the emphasis seems to be upon the fact that the daughters were not as yet married. (See [34\.77](#34.77).) Similarly, in [MAT 25:1](https://ref.ly/Matt25:1) the ten παρθένοι are unmarried girls desirous of participating in a wedding party, and the emphasis would seem to be more upon their being unmarried rather than upon their being virgins.  
In obtaining a satisfactory term for ‘virgin,’ there are often a number of difficulties. For example, a term which designates a virgin may also imply participation in a particular set of ritual or cult practices consisting of puberty rites, in which ritual sexual intercourse is an integral element. In some languages a term which technically means ‘virgin’ is also employed with unacceptable connotations in that it may suggest that the woman in question has a queer personality or unattractive appearance and therefore is to be sexually avoided. In some instances a term which in some contexts may be equivalent to ‘virgin’ may also refer to a homosexual female who has not had relations with a man but who does engage in sexual relations with women.

[MAT 1:23](https://ref.ly/Matt1:23) · [MAT 25:1](https://ref.ly/Matt25:1) · [MAT 25:7](https://ref.ly/Matt25:7) · [MAT 25:11](https://ref.ly/Matt25:11) · [LUK 1:27](https://ref.ly/Mark1:27) · [LUK 1:27](https://ref.ly/Mark1:27) · [ACT 21:9](https://ref.ly/John21:9) · [1CO 7:34](https://ref.ly/Rom7:34) · [1CO 7:36](https://ref.ly/Rom7:36) · [1CO 7:37](https://ref.ly/Rom7:37) · [1CO 7:38](https://ref.ly/Rom7:38) · [2CO 11:2](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:2)

9\.33 · People · Males

virgin, chaste an adult male who has not engaged in sexual relations with a woman It is very rare that the same term can be applied to a man who has not engaged in sexual relations with a woman as is used in speaking of a woman who has not had sexual relations with a man. In fact, in English the use of ‘virgin’ as applied to a man seems strange. In many languages there is simply no term for a man who is a virgin, since such a state is regarded as being rather unthinkable. However, the first part of this statement in [REV 14:4](https://ref.ly/Jude14:4) indicates clearly the state of the persons in question. But if one does attempt to find a satisfactory term, it is important to determine whether such a word implies homosexuality, for this tends to be the case in some languages. See also comments at [9\.39](#9.39).  
In most languages terms for the age\-grading of females parallel quite closely those relating to males. The principal distinctions in age include (1\) girl babies before the age of weaning; (2\) from weaning to puberty; (3\) from puberty to marriage; (4\) from marriage to menopause; and (5\) after menopause.

[REV 14:4](https://ref.ly/Jude14:4)

34\.77 · Association · Marriage, Divorce

unmarried person a person who has not as yet married (and possibly implying virginity) Some scholars interpret παρθένος\[c] as referring not only to those who have never married, but also to widows and widowers who have not remarried. The meaning of unmarried persons who are not necessarily virgins is well attested in Greek from classical times.

[1CO 7:25](https://ref.ly/Rom7:25) · [1CO 7:28](https://ref.ly/Rom7:28)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 1:23; Matthew 25:1; Matthew 25:7; Matthew 25:11; Luke 1:27; Acts 21:9; 1 Corinthians 7:34; 1 Corinthians 7:36; 1 Corinthians 7:37; 1 Corinthians 7:38; 2 Corinthians 11:2; Revelation 14:4; 1 Corinthians 7:25; 1 Corinthians 7:28

37\.111 · Control, Rule · Hand Over, Betray

to hand over, to turn over to, to betray to deliver a person into the control of someone else, involving either the handing over of a presumably guilty person for punishment by authorities or the handing over of an individual to an enemy who will presumably take undue advantage of the victim As is the case in English, a number of languages make a clear distinction between legitimate handing over of a presumably guilty person to a civil authority and the betrayal of a person in the in\-group to someone in the out\-group.

72\.4 · True, False · True, False

17\.3 · (παρίσταμαι\[a]) · Stances and Events Related to Stances · Stand

to stand near to stand near or alongside of someone, either with friendly or hostile intent To indicate merely that the kings of the earth were present may not be sufficient to communicate satisfactorily the hostile intent implied by the context of [ACT 4:26](https://ref.ly/John4:26). It may therefore be important to translate ‘the kings of earth prepared themselves’ or even ‘the kings of earth armed themselves.’

35\.1 · (παρίσταμαι\[f]) · Help, Care For · Help

[ROM 16:2](https://ref.ly/Acts16:2)

1 MRK 14:70 It would also be possible to classify παρίσταμαι in [MRK 14:70](https://ref.ly/Matt14:70) as a matter involving primarily stance (compare παρίσταμαι\[a] ‘to stand near,’ [17\.3](#17.3)). 2 2TI 4:17 It is also valid to classify παρίσταμαι in [2TI 4:17](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:17) as meaning ‘to be on hand to help’ (see [35\.1](#35.1)). 3 The various terms noted in [35\.1](#35.1) (ἀντιλαμβάνω\[a], ἐπιλαμβάνομαι\[d], ὑπολαμβάνω\[c], συμβάλλω\[e], παρίστημι\[l], ἀντέχω\[c], ἐπαρκέω\[a], βοηθέω, βοήθεια\[a]) with a generic meaning of ‘help’ no doubt possess certain contrastive features of meaning, at least of a connotative type, but there is no specific evidence of this in NT usage.

## πάροδος (id: 003840000000000)

πάροδος
=======

πάροδος (G3938)

noun \| \-ου \| ὁδεύω

15\.29 · Linear Movement · Pass, Cross Over, Go Through, Go Around

to pass by, to travel on through, to travel past to pass by some point of reference as one travels A strictly literal translation of [1CO 16:7](https://ref.ly/Rom16:7) in the form ‘I do not want to see you just briefly as I travel past’ may be misleading, for it might imply that Paul simply did not want to have a glance at the people as he travelled past. In fact, the negative in this expression may result in serious misunderstanding, and therefore it may be better to translate ‘I want to have an opportunity to talk with you as I travel on through’ or ‘I wish to visit you at least for a while as I journey on.’

alien, stranger, temporary resident (derivative of παροικέω\[b] ‘to live as a foreigner,’ [85\.78](#85.78)) a person who for a period of time lives in a place which is not his normal residence In [ACT 7:6](https://ref.ly/John7:6) the expression ‘as strangers in a foreign country’ may be rendered ‘as strangers living in a country which was not their own.’ Similarly, ‘who live as aliens’ may also be rendered as ‘who live in countries not their own’ or ‘who live with people to whose nation they do not belong.’

## παροιμία (id: 003844000000000)

παροιμία
========

παροιμία (G3942)

noun \| \-ας \| παρομοιάζω

33\.14 · Communication · Discourse Types

proverb, saying a short saying in fixed form, emphasizing some general truth In a context such as [2PE 2:22](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:22) one may render ‘saying’ as ‘what people so often say’ or ‘what is often said.’

[2PE 2:22](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:22)

33\.15 · Communication · Discourse Types

parable, figure, allegory, figure of speech a relatively short narrative with symbolic meaning In almost all languages there is some way of speaking about parables or allegories. The equivalent in some languages may be ‘a likeness story’ or ‘a story that teaches’ or ‘a story that points the way’ or ‘words that have another meaning’ or ‘words that are saying something else important.’  
Some scholars have insisted that in the NT παραβολή is used only in the sense of a story with one level of symbolic meaning, but in some contexts παραβολή is also used to identify allegories in which each element of the account has a symbolic significance. It is therefore extremely difficult to make a rigid distinction between parables and allegories.

[JHN 10:6](https://ref.ly/Luke10:6) · [JHN 16:25](https://ref.ly/Luke16:25) · [JHN 16:25](https://ref.ly/Luke16:25) · [JHN 16:29](https://ref.ly/Luke16:29)

* **Associated Passages:** 2 Peter 2:22; John 10:6; John 16:25; John 16:29

1 HEB 10:24 In the case of [2CO 9:2](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:2) it is difficult to know precisely how much meaning to attach to ἐρεθίζω in the sense of ‘to encourage’ or ‘to motivate.’ It is possible that ἐρεθίζω retains in this context a considerable amount of its more common meaning (see [88\.168](#88.168)). But in [2CO 9:2](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:2) it may also represent an instance in which the original meaning of a term has been largely lost or at least reduced to primarily a marker of causative relation. It appears likely that παροξυσμός in [HEB 10:24](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:24) has also been largely reduced to a marker of a causative relation, in other words, ‘to cause people to love and to do good,’ but since in this instance there seems to be some focus upon the psychological factors involved, one may translate παροξυσμός as ‘to encourage.’

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 10:24; Acts 15:39

25\.158 · Attitudes and Emotions · Courage, Boldness

boldness, courage a state of boldness and confidence, sometimes implying intimidating circumstances

[MRK 8:32](https://ref.ly/Matt8:32) · [JHN 7:4](https://ref.ly/Luke7:4) · [JHN 7:13](https://ref.ly/Luke7:13) · [JHN 7:26](https://ref.ly/Luke7:26) · [JHN 10:24](https://ref.ly/Luke10:24) · [JHN 11:14](https://ref.ly/Luke11:14) · [JHN 11:54](https://ref.ly/Luke11:54) · [JHN 16:25](https://ref.ly/Luke16:25) · [JHN 16:29](https://ref.ly/Luke16:29) · [JHN 18:20](https://ref.ly/Luke18:20) · [ACT 2:29](https://ref.ly/John2:29) · [ACT 4:13](https://ref.ly/John4:13) · [ACT 4:29](https://ref.ly/John4:29) · [ACT 4:31](https://ref.ly/John4:31) · [ACT 28:31](https://ref.ly/John28:31) · [2CO 3:12](https://ref.ly/1Cor3:12) · [2CO 7:4](https://ref.ly/1Cor7:4) · [EPH 3:12](https://ref.ly/Gal3:12) · [EPH 6:19](https://ref.ly/Gal6:19) · [PHP 1:20](https://ref.ly/Eph1:20) · [COL 2:15](https://ref.ly/Phil2:15) · [1TI 3:13](https://ref.ly/2Thess3:13) · [PHM 1:8](https://ref.ly/Titus1:8) · [HEB 3:6](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:6) · [HEB 4:16](https://ref.ly/Phlm4:16) · [HEB 10:19](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:19) · [HEB 10:35](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:35) · [1JN 2:28](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:28) · [1JN 3:21](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:21) · [1JN 4:17](https://ref.ly/2Pet4:17) · [1JN 5:14](https://ref.ly/2Pet5:14)

## παρρησιάζομαι (id: 003857000000000)

παρρησιάζομαι
=============

παρρησιάζομαι (G3955)

πᾶς \+ ῥῆμα

33\.90 · Communication · Speak, Talk

to speak boldly, to speak openly (derivative of παρρησία ‘boldness,’ [25\.158](#25.158)) to speak openly about something and with complete confidence In some languages the equivalent of ‘to speak boldly’ is ‘to speak regardless of who is listening’ or ‘to speak without fearing’ or ‘to speak without worrying.’

[ACT 9:27](https://ref.ly/John9:27) · [ACT 9:28](https://ref.ly/John9:28) · [ACT 13:46](https://ref.ly/John13:46) · [ACT 14:3](https://ref.ly/John14:3) · [ACT 18:26](https://ref.ly/John18:26) · [ACT 19:8](https://ref.ly/John19:8) · [ACT 26:26](https://ref.ly/John26:26) · [EPH 6:20](https://ref.ly/Gal6:20) · [1TH 2:2](https://ref.ly/Col2:2)

25\.159 · Attitudes and Emotions · Courage, Boldness

to be bold, to have courage to have courage or boldness in the face of danger or opposition

[1TH 2:2](https://ref.ly/Col2:2)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 9:27; Acts 9:28; Acts 13:46; Acts 14:3; Acts 18:26; Acts 19:8; Acts 26:26; Ephesians 6:20; 1 Thessalonians 2:2

78\.44 · Degree · Completely, Enough \[Completive Degree]

complete, completely, totally, totality a degree of totality or completeness2

63\.2 · Whole, Unite, Part, Divide · Whole

entire, whole, total (with the definite article) pertaining to being entire or whole, with focus on the totality

every kind of, all sorts of a totality of kinds or sorts

67\.15 · Time · A Point of Time without Reference to Other Points of Time: Time, Occasion, Ever, Often

**Collocation:** διὰ παντός

regularly, periodically (an idiom, literally: through all) a number of related points of time, occurring at regular intervals

[LUK 24:53](https://ref.ly/Mark24:53) · [ACT 10:2](https://ref.ly/John10:2) · [2TH 3:16](https://ref.ly/1Thess3:16) · [HEB 9:6](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:6)

67\.86 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

**Collocation:** διὰ παντός

always, constantly, continually (an idiom, literally: through all) duration of time, either continuous or episodic, but without limits In some languages there may be problems involved in rendering ἀεί or διὰ παντός\[b], since there may be a basic distinction between (a) continuous activity and (b) activity which may be defined as episodic in that it regularly recurs in related episodes. For example, in [2CO 6:10](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:10) the fact of being glad may be related specifically to the occasions of being saddened, while in [MAT 18:10](https://ref.ly/Matt18:10) the focus of meaning is probably upon the continuous nature of the relation.

[MAT 18:10](https://ref.ly/Matt18:10) · [MRK 5:5](https://ref.ly/Matt5:5) · [LUK 24:53](https://ref.ly/Mark24:53) · [ACT 2:25](https://ref.ly/John2:25) · [ACT 24:16](https://ref.ly/John24:16) · [ROM 11:10](https://ref.ly/Acts11:10) · [HEB 2:15](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:15) · [HEB 13:15](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:15)

1 In Attic Greek ἅπας occurs after consonants and πᾶς after vowels, but this distinction is not maintained in the NT, and there is insufficient evidence to indicate any significant distinction in meaning, though some persons have believed that ἅπας is somewhat more emphatic. 2 There may very well be certain significant differences of meaning in this series of degree expressions, but this is difficult, if not impossible, to determine from existing contexts.

## πάσχα (id: 003859000000000)

πάσχα
=====

πάσχα (G3957)

noun

51\.6 · Festivals

Passover festival, Passover the Jewish festival commemorating the deliverance of Jews from Egypt In some languages the term πάσχα has been borrowed in one form or another, but frequently it is necessary to have some qualifying statement to identify this festival, for example, ‘a festival to celebrate the passing over of the angel’ or ‘a festival to celebrate deliverance from Egypt.’

[MAT 26:2](https://ref.ly/Matt26:2) · [MRK 14:1](https://ref.ly/Matt14:1) · [MRK 14:12](https://ref.ly/Matt14:12) · [LUK 2:41](https://ref.ly/Mark2:41) · [LUK 22:1](https://ref.ly/Mark22:1) · [JHN 2:13](https://ref.ly/Luke2:13) · [JHN 2:23](https://ref.ly/Luke2:23) · [JHN 6:4](https://ref.ly/Luke6:4) · [JHN 11:55](https://ref.ly/Luke11:55) · [JHN 11:55](https://ref.ly/Luke11:55) · [JHN 12:1](https://ref.ly/Luke12:1) · [JHN 13:1](https://ref.ly/Luke13:1) · [JHN 18:39](https://ref.ly/Luke18:39) · [JHN 19:14](https://ref.ly/Luke19:14) · [ACT 12:4](https://ref.ly/John12:4) · [HEB 11:28](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:28)

51\.7 · Festivals

Passover meal a Passover meal eaten in connection with the Passover festival (see [51\.6](#51.6))

[MAT 26:18](https://ref.ly/Matt26:18) · [MAT 26:19](https://ref.ly/Matt26:19) · [MRK 14:14](https://ref.ly/Matt14:14) · [MRK 14:16](https://ref.ly/Matt14:16) · [LUK 22:8](https://ref.ly/Mark22:8) · [LUK 22:13](https://ref.ly/Mark22:13) · [JHN 18:28](https://ref.ly/Luke18:28)

4\.27 · Animals · Animals

Passover lamb a specially selected lamb (or a collective for all such lambs) killed and eaten during the festival commemorating the departure of Israel from Egypt The term πάσχα (a borrowing from Hebrew) has three different meanings which refer to three different aspects of the Passover. In a context which speaks of ‘the Passover taking place’ ([MAT 26:2](https://ref.ly/Matt26:2)), the meaning is the festival ([51\.6](#51.6)). With a term such as ἑτοιμάζειν ‘to prepare’ ([MAT 26:19](https://ref.ly/Matt26:19)), the term πάσχα means ‘the Passover meal,’ that is to say, ‘to prepare the Passover meal’ ([51\.7](#51.7)). But with a term meaning ‘to kill’ or ‘to sacrifice’ ([LUK 22:7](https://ref.ly/Mark22:7)), the meaning is ‘the Passover lamb.’ These different meanings in Greek reflect similar uses in Hebrew.  
It is often impossible, however, to use a phrase such as ‘Passover lamb,’ since a literal rendering may suggest ‘a lamb that passes over’ or ‘a lamb that someone has passed over,’ either in the sense of ‘ridden over’ or ‘neglected.’ It is therefore necessary in many languages to expand the phrase ‘the Passover lamb’ to read ‘the lamb that is eaten at the Passover Festival’ or ‘the lamb associated with the Festival that celebrates the passing over.’ It may even be necessary to expand the phrase ‘passing over’ to refer specifically to the passing over of the angel of death, and in certain instances one cannot even employ a literal rendering such as ‘passing over,’ since this might mean merely ‘flying above.’ Therefore, it may be necessary to use an expression roughly equivalent to ‘passing by.’ It may, however, be better to use a short, though somewhat obscure, expression in the text and provide a full explanation in a marginal note or glossary.

[MAT 26:17](https://ref.ly/Matt26:17) · [MRK 14:12](https://ref.ly/Matt14:12) · [LUK 22:7](https://ref.ly/Mark22:7) · [LUK 22:11](https://ref.ly/Mark22:11) · [LUK 22:15](https://ref.ly/Mark22:15) · [1CO 5:7](https://ref.ly/Rom5:7)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 26:2; Mark 14:1; Mark 14:12; Luke 2:41; Luke 22:1; John 2:13; John 2:23; John 6:4; John 11:55; John 12:1; John 13:1; John 18:39; John 19:14; Acts 12:4; Hebrews 11:28; Matthew 26:18; Matthew 26:19; Mark 14:14; Mark 14:16; Luke 22:8; Luke 22:13; John 18:28; Matthew 26:17; Luke 22:7; Luke 22:11; Luke 22:15; 1 Corinthians 5:7


## πάσχω (id: 003860000000000)

πάσχω
=====

πάσχω (G3958)

aor. ἔπαθον · inf. παθεῖν · ptc. παθών · fem.ptc. παθοῦσα · pf. πέπονθα \| κακοπάθεια · μετριοπαθέω · ὁμοιοπαθής · πάθημα · παθητός · πάθος · πραϋπάθεια · προπάσχω · συγκακοπαθέω · συμπαθέω · συμπαθής · συμπάσχω

24\.78 · Sensory Events and States · Pain, Suffering

pain, suffering, to suffer, to be in pain to suffer pain

[MAT 16:21](https://ref.ly/Matt16:21) · [MAT 17:12](https://ref.ly/Matt17:12) · [MRK 5:26](https://ref.ly/Matt5:26) · [MRK 8:31](https://ref.ly/Matt8:31) · [MRK 9:12](https://ref.ly/Matt9:12) · [LUK 9:22](https://ref.ly/Mark9:22) · [LUK 17:25](https://ref.ly/Mark17:25) · [LUK 22:15](https://ref.ly/Mark22:15) · [LUK 24:26](https://ref.ly/Mark24:26) · [LUK 24:46](https://ref.ly/Mark24:46) · [ACT 1:3](https://ref.ly/John1:3) · [ACT 3:18](https://ref.ly/John3:18) · [ACT 17:3](https://ref.ly/John17:3) · [1CO 12:26](https://ref.ly/Rom12:26) · [2CO 1:6](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:6)1 · [PHP 1:29](https://ref.ly/Eph1:29) · [1TH 2:14](https://ref.ly/Col2:14) · [2TH 1:5](https://ref.ly/1Thess1:5) · [2TI 1:12](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:12) · [HEB 2:18](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:18) · [HEB 5:8](https://ref.ly/Phlm5:8) · [HEB 9:26](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:26) · [HEB 13:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:12) · [1PE 2:19](https://ref.ly/Jas2:19) · [1PE 2:20](https://ref.ly/Jas2:20) · [1PE 2:21](https://ref.ly/Jas2:21) · [1PE 2:23](https://ref.ly/Jas2:23) · [1PE 3:14](https://ref.ly/Jas3:14) · [1PE 3:17](https://ref.ly/Jas3:17) · [1PE 3:18](https://ref.ly/Jas3:18) · [1PE 4:1](https://ref.ly/Jas4:1) · [1PE 4:1](https://ref.ly/Jas4:1) · [1PE 4:15](https://ref.ly/Jas4:15) · [1PE 4:19](https://ref.ly/Jas4:19) · [1PE 5:10](https://ref.ly/Jas5:10)

90\.66 · Case · Experiencer

to experience, to suffer to undergo an experience, usually difficult, and normally with the implication of physical or psychological suffering The first statement in [GAL 3:4](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:4) is generally interpreted as referring to the valuable experiences which the Galatians had in receiving the Spirit on the basis of hearing and believing. Some scholars, however, understand πάσχω in [GAL 3:4](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:4) as referring to difficult experiences resulting from those who opposed this new faith in Jesus Christ.

[MAT 17:15](https://ref.ly/Matt17:15) · [MAT 27:19](https://ref.ly/Matt27:19) · [LUK 13:2](https://ref.ly/Mark13:2) · [ACT 9:16](https://ref.ly/John9:16) · [ACT 28:5](https://ref.ly/John28:5) · [GAL 3:4](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:4) · [HEB 5:8](https://ref.ly/Phlm5:8) · [REV 2:10](https://ref.ly/Jude2:10)

1 2CO 1:6 It is also possible to interpret πάσχομεν in [2CO 1:6](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:6) as being essentially a marker of a case relationship meaning to experience (see [90\.66](#90.66)).

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 16:21; Matthew 17:12; Mark 5:26; Mark 8:31; Mark 9:12; Luke 9:22; Luke 17:25; Luke 22:15; Luke 24:26; Luke 24:46; Acts 1:3; Acts 3:18; Acts 17:3; 1 Corinthians 12:26; 2 Corinthians 1:6; Philippians 1:29; 1 Thessalonians 2:14; 2 Thessalonians 1:5; 2 Timothy 1:12; Hebrews 2:18; Hebrews 5:8; Hebrews 9:26; Hebrews 13:12; 1 Peter 2:19; 1 Peter 2:20; 1 Peter 2:21; 1 Peter 2:23; 1 Peter 3:14; 1 Peter 3:17; 1 Peter 3:18; 1 Peter 4:1; 1 Peter 4:15; 1 Peter 4:19; 1 Peter 5:10; Matthew 17:15; Matthew 27:19; Luke 13:2; Acts 9:16; Acts 28:5; Galatians 3:4; Revelation 2:10

## πατάσσω (id: 003862000000000)

πατάσσω
=======

πατάσσω (G3960)

19\.3 · Physical Impact · Hit, Strike

to strike a blow to strike a heavy blow, implying severe damage The context of [LUK 22:50](https://ref.ly/Mark22:50) indicates that a sword was used in striking the blow.

to slay, to strike down to slay by means of a mortal blow or disease

## πατέω (id: 003863000000000)

πατέω
=====

πατέω (G3961)

ἐμπεριπατέω · καταπατέω · περιπατέω

15\.226 · Linear Movement · Walk, Step

to step on, to tread on to step on something It is possible to interpret πατέω in [LUK 10:19](https://ref.ly/Mark10:19) as meaning ‘to trample,’ with emphasis upon the impact of the feet upon the snakes (see [19\.51](#19.51)). It is important in rendering [LUK 10:19](https://ref.ly/Mark10:19) not to give the impression that someone is to merely walk on a mass of snakes; this is simply a matter of accidentally stepping on a poisonous snake or scorpion.

[LUK 10:19](https://ref.ly/Mark10:19)

19\.51 · Physical Impact · Press

to trample to step down on, with the possible implication of continuous or repeated action The occurrence of πατέω in the figurative context of [REV 19:15](https://ref.ly/Jude19:15) is based upon the practice of persons squeezing out the juice from grapes by trampling on the grapes in a large vat or container which had an opening at the bottom through which the grape juice could flow.

[REV 14:20](https://ref.ly/Jude14:20) · [REV 19:15](https://ref.ly/Jude19:15)

20\.22 · Violence, Harm, Destroy, Kill · Harm, Wound

to trample on, to subdue by force (a figurative extension of meaning of πατέω\[b] ‘to trample,’ [19\.51](#19.51)) to harm severely by subjugation For another interpretation of πατέω in [LUK 21:24](https://ref.ly/Mark21:24), see [39\.54](#39.54).

[LUK 10:19](https://ref.ly/Mark10:19) · [LUK 21:24](https://ref.ly/Mark21:24) · [REV 11:2](https://ref.ly/Jude11:2)

39\.54 · Hostility, Strife · Conquer

to conquer, to tread down (a figurative extension of meaning of πατέω\[b] ‘to trample,’ [19\.51](#19.51)) to conquer and keep under subjection For another interpretation of πατέω in [LUK 21:24](https://ref.ly/Mark21:24), see [20\.22](#20.22).

[LUK 21:24](https://ref.ly/Mark21:24)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 10:19; Revelation 14:20; Revelation 19:15; Luke 21:24; Revelation 11:2

ancestor, forefather a person several preceding generations removed from the reference person In a number of languages one’s forefathers may be referred to simply as ‘fathers who have long since died.’ In other languages they may be referred to as ‘fathers of other generations’ or ‘fathers of ancient times.’

3:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:9) · [HEB 7:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:10) · [HEB 8:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm8:9) · [JAS 2:21](https://ref.ly/Heb2:21) · [2PE 3:4](https://ref.ly/1Pet3:4)

12\.12 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

Father (title for God, literally: father) one who combines aspects of supernatural authority and care for his people2 In a number of languages it is necessary to distinguish clearly between ‘Father’ when referring to the heavenly Father and ‘father’ as a reference to a human father. The use of capitalization is quite satisfactory for the individual who is reading a text but not for one who is hearing it read, and since more people hear a text read than read it for themselves, it is essential that the reference of ‘Father’ be clear. In order to identify the use of ‘Father’ as a title for God, it is possible in many languages to use ‘Father in heaven’ or ‘Father above’ or ‘Father God.’  
In some languages it may even be necessary to identify the ‘Father’ as the creator and therefore employ a phrase such as ‘our Father who created us.’  
A particularly complicating factor involved in the use of ‘Father’ as a title for God is its occurrence without a pronominal reference as to whose father is involved. For example, in a number of languages one cannot speak of ‘father’ without indicating whose father, because a person does not become a father except by some relationship to another individual. Therefore, one must always speak of ‘my father’ or ‘his father’ and never simply ‘the father.’ In passages in which Jesus is speaking of ‘the Father’ in relationship to himself, it is necessary usually to employ a phrase such as ‘my Father.’ When, however, ‘Father’ is used as a title for ‘God’ in his relationship to people generally, then one may speak of ‘our Father’ (normally with an inclusive first person plural pronoun if the language in question makes a distinction between inclusive and exclusive first person plural pronoun referents).

87\.48 · Status · High Status or Rank \[including persons of high status]

father, the honorable, excellency a title for a person of high rank

[MAT 23:9](https://ref.ly/Matt23:9)

11\.26 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

father, elder a member of a well\-defined socio\-religious entity and representing an older age group than the so\-called reference person

[ACT 7:2](https://ref.ly/John7:2) · [ACT 22:1](https://ref.ly/John22:1) · [1JN 2:13](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:13) · [1JN 2:14](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:14)

58\.64 · Nature, Class, Example · Archetype, Corresponding Type \[Antitype]

archetype, father, spiritual father (figurative extension of meaning of πατήρ\[a] ‘father,’ [10\.14](#10.14)) an archetype anticipating a later reality and suggesting a derivative relationship4

[JHN 8:44](https://ref.ly/Luke8:44) · [ROM 4:11](https://ref.ly/Acts4:11) · [ROM 4:12](https://ref.ly/Acts4:12) · [ROM 4:12](https://ref.ly/Acts4:12) · [ROM 4:16](https://ref.ly/Acts4:16) · [ROM 4:17](https://ref.ly/Acts4:17) · [HEB 12:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:9)

36\.8 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Guide, Lead

spiritual father, leader in the faith (a figurative extension of meaning of πατήρ\[a] ‘father,’ [10\.14](#10.14)) one who is responsible for having guided another into faith or into a particular pattern of behavior

[JHN 8:38](https://ref.ly/Luke8:38) · [JHN 8:41](https://ref.ly/Luke8:41) · [JHN 8:44](https://ref.ly/Luke8:44) · [JHN 8:44](https://ref.ly/Luke8:44) · [1CO 4:15](https://ref.ly/Rom4:15)5

52\.7 · Funerals and Burial

**Collocation:** προστίθημι πρὸς τοὺς πατέρας αὐτοῦ

to bury (a Semitic idiom, literally: to place with his fathers) to carry out a burial procedure

[ACT 13:36](https://ref.ly/John13:36)

1 The Aramaic word αββα meaning ‘father’ occurs in the NT only as a title for God (see [12\.12](#12.12)). 2 Items [12\.4](#12.4)\-[12\.8](#12.8), ([12\.9](#12.9)) and [12\.12](#12.12) in this domain are titles for θεός\[a] God ([12\.1](#12.1)). As titles, they have precisely the same reference as θεός\[a], but they each contribute distinctive meanings which are legitimate qualifications of the referent in question. 3 ACT 22:1 In [ACT 22:1](https://ref.ly/John22:1), as noted in [11\.25](#11.25) and [11\.26](#11.26), the use of ἀδελφοί focuses more upon identification of the speaker with the persons addressed, while πατέρες implies respect accorded those representing an older generation. 4 πατήρ and μήτηρ in this figurative sense do not differ essentially in meaning. The choice of either πατήρ or μήτηρ depends entirely upon the contextual referent which serves as the original example or archetype. 5 1CO 4:15 In Greek the expression οὐ πολλοὺς πατέρας clearly seems to be an instance of understatement, and it may therefore be translated as ‘only one spiritual father.’ The phrase ‘in Christ’ probably refers to matters concerning the Christian life.

## πατριά (id: 003866000000000)

πατριά
======

πατριά (G3965)

noun \| \-ᾶς \| πατήρ

10\.24 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations Involving Successive Generations

lineage, family line persons of successive generations who are related by birth1 In biblical times the reckoning of a lineage was somewhat complex in view of certain distinctions between legal and biological relations. The focus of meaning in γένεσις\[b], οἶκος\[c], and πατριά\[a] is not, however, the list of ancestors, but the lineage of successively related persons to which the reference person belongs. Some scholars, however, interpret γένεσις\[c] history, [33\.19](#33.19)).  
In some languages a successive series of ancestors is called ‘the forefathers’ or ‘those to whom one looks back’ or ‘those who have gone ahead.’ In other languages a lineage is referred to as ‘a person’s root’ or ‘a man’s trunk’ (referring to the trunk of a tree).  
In [LUK 2:4](https://ref.ly/Mark2:4) there appears to be no significant difference in meaning between οἶκος and πατριά, and in some receptor languages it would be sufficient to use a single term essentially equivalent to ‘lineage.’ The two terms appear to simply reinforce their shared meaning.

[LUK 2:4](https://ref.ly/Mark2:4)

11\.56 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Political

nation, people a relatively large unit of people who constitute a sociopolitical group, sharing a presumed biological descent (in many contexts πατριά\[b] and φυλή\[b] are very similar in meaning to ἔθνος and λαός\[a], [11\.55](#11.55))

[ACT 3:25](https://ref.ly/John3:25) · [EPH 3:15](https://ref.ly/Gal3:15)

1 πατριά\[a] and φυλή\[b] nation, people ([11\.56](#11.56)) focus attention upon a particular people as a socially functioning unit, while the feature of biological descent is largely secondary. But in the case of γένος\[b] and σάρξ\[e], the feature of biological descent is focal, while group functioning is largely secondary.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 2:4; Acts 3:25; Ephesians 3:15


## πατριάρχης (id: 003867000000000)

πατριάρχης
==========

πατριάρχης (G3966)

noun \| \-ου \| πατήρ \+ ἄρχω

10\.22 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations Involving Successive Generations

patriarch a particularly noted male ancestor (either because of his role in initiating an ethnic group or groups, for example, Abraham, Isaac, Jacob and the twelve sons of Jacob, or because of important exploits and accomplishments, for example, David) The equivalent expression for patriarch in a number of languages is merely ‘important forefather’ or ‘famous forefather’ or ‘great ancestor.’

[ACT 2:29](https://ref.ly/John2:29) · [ACT 7:8](https://ref.ly/John7:8) · [ACT 7:9](https://ref.ly/John7:9) · [HEB 7:4](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:4)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 2:29; Acts 7:8; Acts 7:9; Hebrews 7:4


## πατρικός (id: 003868000000000)

πατρικός
========

πατρικός (G3967)

adj \| \-ή, \-όν \| πατήρ

10\.21 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations Involving Successive Generations

of the ancestors, of the forefathers (derivative of πατήρ\[c] ‘ancestor, forefather,’ [10\.20](#10.20)) pertaining to one’s ancestors

[GAL 1:14](https://ref.ly/2Cor1:14)

* **Associated Passages:** Galatians 1:14


## πατρίς (id: 003869000000000)

πατρίς
======

πατρίς (G3968)

noun \| \-ίδος \| πατήρ

1\.81 · Geographical Objects and Features · Sociopolitical Areas

homeland, hometown the region or population center from which a person comes, that is to say, the place of one’s birth or childhood or the place from which one’s family has come In some languages a very clear distinction is made between three different references of a term such as πατρίς: (1\) the place from which one’s family has come, (2\) the place where one is born, and (3\) the place where one normally resides. Special care, therefore, must be exercised in translating a passage such as [MRK 6:1](https://ref.ly/Matt6:1).

[MAT 13:54](https://ref.ly/Matt13:54) · [MAT 13:57](https://ref.ly/Matt13:57) · [MRK 6:1](https://ref.ly/Matt6:1) · [MRK 6:4](https://ref.ly/Matt6:4) · [LUK 4:23](https://ref.ly/Mark4:23) · [LUK 4:24](https://ref.ly/Mark4:24) · [JHN 4:44](https://ref.ly/Luke4:44) · [HEB 11:14](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:14)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 13:54; Matthew 13:57; Mark 6:1; Mark 6:4; Luke 4:23; Luke 4:24; John 4:44; Hebrews 11:14

## παύω (id: 003875000000000)

παύω
====

παύω (G3973) — παύομαι

ἀκατάπαυστος · ἀναπαύω · ἐπαναπαύομαι · κατάπαυσις · καταπαύω · συναναπαύομαι

68\.46 · Aspect · Cease, Stop

to cause to cease, to make stop to cause something or someone to cease from some activity or state1

[ACT 5:42](https://ref.ly/John5:42) · [1PE 3:10](https://ref.ly/Jas3:10)

68\.34 · (παύομαι) · Aspect · Cease, Stop

to cease, to stop to cease from an activity in which one is engaged A literal rendering of [LUK 5:4](https://ref.ly/Mark5:4), however, may suggest a contradiction in some languages, since the initial clause implies a cessation of speech, while the principal clause indicates that Jesus spoke to Simon. It may be possible in some languages to use two entirely different words for speaking, one referring to speaking to a large group, and the second indicating a direct communication with a single person.

[LUK 5:4](https://ref.ly/Mark5:4) · [LUK 8:24](https://ref.ly/Mark8:24) · [LUK 11:1](https://ref.ly/Mark11:1) · [ACT 6:13](https://ref.ly/John6:13) · [ACT 13:10](https://ref.ly/John13:10) · [ACT 20:1](https://ref.ly/John20:1) · [ACT 20:31](https://ref.ly/John20:31) · [ACT 21:32](https://ref.ly/John21:32) · [1CO 13:8](https://ref.ly/Rom13:8) · [EPH 1:16](https://ref.ly/Gal1:16) · [COL 1:9](https://ref.ly/Phil1:9) · [HEB 10:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:2) · [1PE 4:1](https://ref.ly/Jas4:1)

1 It is possible that καταπαύω\[c] should be understood as being somewhat more emphatic in meaning than παύω.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 5:42; 1 Peter 3:10; Luke 5:4; Luke 8:24; Luke 11:1; Acts 6:13; Acts 13:10; Acts 20:1; Acts 20:31; Acts 21:32; 1 Corinthians 13:8; Ephesians 1:16; Colossians 1:9; Hebrews 10:2; 1 Peter 4:1

πέδη
====

πέδη (G3976)

noun \| \-ης \| πεζεύω

6\.17 · Artifacts · Instruments Used in Binding and Fastening

fetter, shackle metal chains with special links prepared to go around the ankles; used to restrict the movement of a prisoner These shackles would normally have consisted of chains with special links prepared to go around the ankles. The equivalent in some languages is simply ‘chains on the feet.’

## πεζεύω (id: 003880000000000)

πεζεύω
======

πεζεύω (G3978)

πέδη · πεδινός · πεζῇ · τράπεζα · τραπεζίτης

15\.224 · Linear Movement · Walk, Step

to go by foot, to go by land to travel by foot

[ACT 20:13](https://ref.ly/John20:13)1

1 ACT 20:13 It is possible that πεζεύω in [ACT 20:13](https://ref.ly/John20:13) can mean merely to travel by land and not necessarily by foot.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 20:13

adv \| πεζεύω

on foot, by land (derivative of πεζός ‘on foot,’ not occurring in the NT) pertaining to travel by foot overland1 In some languages a more appropriate might be ‘followed him by walking.’

## πειθώ (id: 003884000000000)

=====

adj \| \-οῦς · dat.sg. πειθοῖ \| πείθω

33\.301 · Communication · Urge, Persuade

to persuade, to convince to convince someone to believe something and to act on the basis of what is recommended

[MAT 27:20](https://ref.ly/Matt27:20) · [MAT 28:14](https://ref.ly/Matt28:14) · [LUK 16:31](https://ref.ly/Mark16:31) · [LUK 20:6](https://ref.ly/Mark20:6) · [ACT 5:37](https://ref.ly/John5:37) · [ACT 12:20](https://ref.ly/John12:20) · [ACT 13:43](https://ref.ly/John13:43) · [ACT 14:19](https://ref.ly/John14:19) · [ACT 17:4](https://ref.ly/John17:4) · [ACT 18:4](https://ref.ly/John18:4) · [ACT 19:8](https://ref.ly/John19:8) · [ACT 19:26](https://ref.ly/John19:26) · [ACT 21:14](https://ref.ly/John21:14) · [ACT 23:21](https://ref.ly/John23:21) · [ACT 26:26](https://ref.ly/John26:26) · [ACT 26:28](https://ref.ly/John26:28) · [ACT 27:11](https://ref.ly/John27:11) · [ACT 28:23](https://ref.ly/John28:23) · [ACT 28:24](https://ref.ly/John28:24) · [2CO 5:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor5:11) · [GAL 1:10](https://ref.ly/2Cor1:10) · [GAL 5:7](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:7) · [PHP 1:6](https://ref.ly/Eph1:6) · [PHP 1:25](https://ref.ly/Eph1:25) · [2TI 1:5](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:5) · [2TI 1:12](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:12) · [PHM 1:21](https://ref.ly/Titus1:21) · [HEB 6:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:9) · [HEB 13:18](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:18) · [JAS 3:3](https://ref.ly/Heb3:3)

31\.82 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Trust, Rely

to rely on, to trust in, to depend on, to have (complete) confidence in, confidence, trust to believe in something or someone to the extent of placing reliance or trust in or on In a number of languages trust or reliance is expressed idiomatically, for example, ‘to lean one’s weight on’ or ‘to hang upon’ or ‘to place oneself in the hands of.’

[MAT 27:43](https://ref.ly/Matt27:43) · [LUK 11:22](https://ref.ly/Mark11:22) · [LUK 18:9](https://ref.ly/Mark18:9) · [ACT 5:36](https://ref.ly/John5:36)1 · [ACT 5:39](https://ref.ly/John5:39) · [ROM 2:8](https://ref.ly/Acts2:8) · [ROM 2:19](https://ref.ly/Acts2:19) · [ROM 8:38](https://ref.ly/Acts8:38) · [ROM 14:14](https://ref.ly/Acts14:14) · [ROM 15:14](https://ref.ly/Acts15:14) · [2CO 1:9](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:9) · [2CO 2:3](https://ref.ly/1Cor2:3) · [2CO 10:7](https://ref.ly/1Cor10:7) · [GAL 5:10](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:10) · [PHP 1:14](https://ref.ly/Eph1:14) · [PHP 2:24](https://ref.ly/Eph2:24) · [PHP 3:3](https://ref.ly/Eph3:3) · [PHP 3:4](https://ref.ly/Eph3:4) · [2TH 3:4](https://ref.ly/1Thess3:4) · [PHM 1:21](https://ref.ly/Titus1:21) · [HEB 2:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:13) · [1JN 3:19](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:19)

36\.12 · (πείθομαι\[a]) · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Obey, Disobey

to obey to submit to authority or reason by obeying

[ROM 2:8](https://ref.ly/Acts2:8) · [HEB 13:17](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:17)

36\.34 · (πείθομαι\[b]) · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Follow, Be a Disciple

to be a follower of, to be a disciple of to be a disciple or follower of someone, in the sense of having put one’s confidence in a leader

[ACT 5:36](https://ref.ly/John5:36)1

31\.46 · (πείθομαι\[c]) · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Believe To Be True

to be certain, to be sure, to be convinced to come to believe the certainty of something on the basis of being convinced

[ROM 8:38](https://ref.ly/Acts8:38) · [ROM 14:14](https://ref.ly/Acts14:14) · [ROM 15:14](https://ref.ly/Acts15:14) · [HEB 13:18](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:18)

25\.166 · Attitudes and Emotions · Courage, Boldness

**Collocation:** πείθω τὴν καρδίαν

to be confident, to be assured (an idiom, literally: to convince the heart) to exhibit confidence and assurance in a situation which might otherwise cause dismay or fear

[1JN 3:19](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:19)

1 ACT 5:36 It would be possible to understand πείθομαι in [ACT 5:36](https://ref.ly/John5:36) as meaning ‘those who trust in’ or ‘… rely on,’ and as such πείθομαι would then be classified along with πείθω\[b] ‘to trust in,’ ‘to have complete confidence in’ ([31\.82](#31.82)). However, in [ACT 5:36](https://ref.ly/John5:36) the meaning seems to be much closer to that of μαθητεύω\[a] and ἀκολουθέω\[c] ([36\.31](#36.31)), since in [ACT 5:36](https://ref.ly/John5:36) the emphasis is upon the relationship of followers to Theudas rather than upon their confidence in or reliance upon him.

## πεινάω (id: 003886000000000)

πεινάω
======

πεινάω (G3983)

πρόσπεινος

23\.29 · Physiological Processes and States · Eat, Drink

to be hungry, to have hunger to be in a state of hunger, without any implications of particular contributing circumstances Hunger is often spoken of in an idiomatic manner, for example, ‘my stomach is crying,’ ‘my throat has been cut’ (implying that one has not been able to swallow food for a long time), or ‘my stomach is a deep hole.’

25\.17 · Attitudes and Emotions · Desire Strongly

to desire strongly (figurative extension of meaning of πεινάω ‘to hunger,’ [23\.29](#23.29)) to have a strong desire to attain some goal, with the implication of an existing lack In [MAT 5:6](https://ref.ly/Matt5:6) the two terms διψάω and πεινάω mutually reinforce the meaning of great desire.

## πεῖρα (id: 003887000000000)

πεῖρα
=====

πεῖρα (G3984)

noun \| \-ας \| πειράζω

68\.58 · Aspect · Try, Attempt

**Collocation:** πεῖραν λαμβάνω

to try, to attempt (an idiom, literally: to take an attempt1) to attempt to do something, with the implication of not succeeding In a number of languages expressions such as πειράζω\[d], πειράομαι, and πεῖραν λαμβάνω may be expressed as ‘to start to do something, but not be able to’ or ‘to think that one can do something, but discover it is impossible.’

[HEB 11:29](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:29) · [HEB 11:36](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:36)

1 The phrase πεῖραν λαμβάνω may be regarded as a so\-called low\-grade idiom, or λαμβάνω may be regarded as a relatively empty term which serves primarily to mark case relations. See Domain 90, footnote 17\.

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 11:29; Hebrews 11:36


## πειράζω (id: 003888000000000)

πειράζω
=======

πειράζω (G3985)

aor.pass. ἐπειράσθην \| ἀπείραστος · ἄπειρος · ἐκπειράζω · πεῖρα · πειράομαι · πειρασμός

27\.46 · Learn · Try To Learn

to test, to examine, to put to the test, examination, testing to try to learn the nature or character of someone or something by submitting such to thorough and extensive testing It is also possible to understand πειράζω and ἐκπειράζω in [MAT 16:1](https://ref.ly/Matt16:1) and [LUK 10:25](https://ref.ly/Mark10:25) as meaning ‘to try to trap’ (see [27\.31](#27.31)). See also [88\.308](#88.308).

[MAT 16:1](https://ref.ly/Matt16:1) · [JHN 6:6](https://ref.ly/Luke6:6) · [ACT 5:9](https://ref.ly/John5:9) · [1CO 10:9](https://ref.ly/Rom10:9) · [1CO 10:13](https://ref.ly/Rom10:13) · [2CO 13:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor13:5) · [HEB 3:9](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:9) · [HEB 11:17](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:17) · [REV 2:2](https://ref.ly/Jude2:2) · [REV 2:10](https://ref.ly/Jude2:10) · [REV 3:10](https://ref.ly/Jude3:10)

27\.31 · Learn · Learn Something Against Someone

to try to trap, to attempt to catch in a mistake to obtain information to be used against a person by trying to cause someone to make a mistake It is also possible to interpret πειράζω and ἐκπειράζω in [MAT 16:1](https://ref.ly/Matt16:1) and [LUK 10:25](https://ref.ly/Mark10:25) as ‘the process of testing or examining’ (see [27\.46](#27.46)). See also [88\.308](#88.308).

[MAT 16:1](https://ref.ly/Matt16:1) · [MAT 19:3](https://ref.ly/Matt19:3) · [MAT 22:18](https://ref.ly/Matt22:18) · [MAT 22:35](https://ref.ly/Matt22:35) · [MRK 8:11](https://ref.ly/Matt8:11) · [MRK 10:2](https://ref.ly/Matt10:2) · [MRK 12:15](https://ref.ly/Matt12:15) · [LUK 11:16](https://ref.ly/Mark11:16) · [JHN 8:6](https://ref.ly/Luke8:6)

88\.308 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Sin, Wrongdoing, Guilt

to tempt, to trap, to lead into temptation, temptation to endeavor or attempt to cause someone to sin In translating expressions involving tempting or trying, it is necessary in a number of languages to indicate clearly whether or not the temptations succeeded. Therefore, it may not be sufficient in [MRK 1:13](https://ref.ly/Matt1:13) to simply say ‘Satan tempted him’; in fact, in some instances it may be necessary to make the failure of the temptation quite specific, for example, ‘Satan tried to make Jesus sin, but was not successful.’

[MAT 4:1](https://ref.ly/Matt4:1) · [MRK 1:13](https://ref.ly/Matt1:13) · [LUK 4:2](https://ref.ly/Mark4:2) · [ACT 15:10](https://ref.ly/John15:10) · [1CO 7:5](https://ref.ly/Rom7:5) · [1CO 10:13](https://ref.ly/Rom10:13) · [GAL 6:1](https://ref.ly/2Cor6:1) · [1TH 3:5](https://ref.ly/Col3:5) · [1TH 3:5](https://ref.ly/Col3:5) · [HEB 2:18](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:18) · [HEB 2:18](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:18) · [HEB 4:15](https://ref.ly/Phlm4:15) · [JAS 1:13](https://ref.ly/Heb1:13) · [JAS 1:13](https://ref.ly/Heb1:13) · [JAS 1:13](https://ref.ly/Heb1:13) · [JAS 1:14](https://ref.ly/Heb1:14)

68\.58 · Aspect · Try, Attempt

to try, to attempt to attempt to do something, with the implication of not succeeding In a number of languages expressions such as πειράζω\[d], πειράομαι, and πεῖραν λαμβάνω may be expressed as to start to do something, but not be able to or to think that one can do something, but discover it is impossible.

[ACT 9:26](https://ref.ly/John9:26) · [ACT 16:7](https://ref.ly/John16:7) · [ACT 24:6](https://ref.ly/John24:6)

12\.36 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

**Collocation:** ὁ πειράζων

Tempter (a title for the Devil, literally: one who tempts) one who tempts or tries people with the intent of making them sin A common equivalent of ὁ πειράζων is ‘one who tries to make people sin’ or ‘one who tries to cause people to sin.’

[MAT 4:3](https://ref.ly/Matt4:3) · [1TH 3:5](https://ref.ly/Col3:5)

## πεισμονή (id: 003891000000000)

πεισμονή
========

πεισμονή (G3988)

noun \| \-ῆς \| πείθω

33\.303 · Communication · Urge, Persuade

that which persuades, the means of convincing (derivative of πείθω\[a] ‘to persuade,’ [33\.301](#33.301)) the means by which someone is caused to believe It is also possible to interpret πεισμονή in [GAL 5:8](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:8) as the actual process of persuasion, in which case πεισμονή would be classified as a nominal form corresponding to πείθω\[a] ([33\.301](#33.301)). Such an interpretation would require one to render [GAL 5:8](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:8) as he who called you was not the one who persuaded you.

[GAL 5:8](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:8)

* **Associated Passages:** Galatians 5:8


## πέλαγος (id: 003892000000000)

πέλαγος
=======

πέλαγος (G3989)

noun \| \-ους

1\.73 · Geographical Objects and Features · Bodies of Water

open sea, high sea, the deep, ocean the relatively deep area of the sea or ocean sufficiently far from land as to be beyond the range of any protection from the seacoast In order to designate the high sea or the ocean, it may be necessary in some instances to speak of ‘the sea far from land’ or ‘the sea out of sight of land.’ People who live near the ocean and are seafaring frequently have quite distinct terms for different conditions of the sea, whether, for example, it is stormy, moderately rough, or relatively calm. Languages may also use different terms in speaking of different areas of the sea or ocean. Note, for example, in English the use of China Sea, North Sea, Bering Sea, in contrast with Indian Ocean, Pacific Ocean, Atlantic Ocean. In English this contrast is relatively fixed, but it does reflect a gradation of size, since sea is used normally in speaking of smaller areas than is the case with ocean.

[MAT 18:6](https://ref.ly/Matt18:6) · [ACT 27:5](https://ref.ly/John27:5)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 18:6; Acts 27:5

15\.193 · Linear Movement · Carry, Bear

to send something, to send by someone to cause someone to carry something to some destination

[MAT 11:2](https://ref.ly/Matt11:2) · [ACT 11:29](https://ref.ly/John11:29)1 · [PHP 4:16](https://ref.ly/Eph4:16) · [REV 1:11](https://ref.ly/Jude1:11) · [REV 11:10](https://ref.ly/Jude11:10)

[ACT 10:32](https://ref.ly/John10:32) · [ACT 10:33](https://ref.ly/John10:33) · [ACT 19:31](https://ref.ly/John19:31) · [ACT 20:17](https://ref.ly/John20:17)

90\.88 · Case · To Cause To Experience

to cause to experience, to send upon to cause someone to experience an event or state from outside the area of normal influences

[2TH 2:11](https://ref.ly/1Thess2:11)

43\.17 · Agriculture

**Collocation:** πέμπω τὸ δρέπανον

to use a sickle, to swing a sickle, to begin to harvest (an idiom, literally: to send a sickle) to begin to harvest a crop by cutting ripe grain with a sickle In [REV 14:15](https://ref.ly/Jude14:15) and [REV 14:18](https://ref.ly/Jude14:18) this idiom occurs in a figurative context, and therefore it may be necessary in some languages to speak of ‘harvesting’ rather than using a phrase which mentions the literal use of a sickle.

[REV 14:15](https://ref.ly/Jude14:15) · [REV 14:18](https://ref.ly/Jude14:18)

1 ACT 11:29 The participant who actually carries the substance in question may be merely implicit. 2 πέμπω, ἀποστέλλω, and ἐξαποστέλλω in the contexts in [15\.67](#15.67) could be classified as a type of communication in Domain 33\.

=====

adj \| \-ητος \| πένομαι (work for one's living, labor) · πόνος

## πενθερά (id: 003897000000000)

πενθερά
=======

πενθερά (G3994)

noun \| \-ᾶς \| πενθερός

10\.59 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations Based upon Marriage

mother\-in\-law the mother of one’s spouse In a number of languages the semantic components of ‘mother\-in\-law’ must be made explicit. For example, in [MRK 1:30](https://ref.ly/Matt1:30) one may translate ‘the mother\-in\-law of Simon’ as ‘the mother of Simon’s wife.’

[MAT 8:14](https://ref.ly/Matt8:14) · [MAT 10:35](https://ref.ly/Matt10:35) · [MRK 1:30](https://ref.ly/Matt1:30) · [LUK 4:38](https://ref.ly/Mark4:38) · [LUK 12:53](https://ref.ly/Mark12:53) · [LUK 12:53](https://ref.ly/Mark12:53)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 8:14; Matthew 10:35; Mark 1:30; Luke 4:38; Luke 12:53


## πενθερός (id: 003898000000000)

πενθερός
========

πενθερός (G3995)

noun \| \-οῦ \| πενθερά

10\.58 · Kinship Terms · Kinship Relations Based upon Marriage

father\-in\-law the father of one’s spouse In a number of languages one must indicate explicitly the various components of meaning in ‘father\-in\-law.’ For example, πενθερός in [JHN 18:13](https://ref.ly/Luke18:13) may be rendered as ‘Caiaphas had married the daughter of Annas’ or ‘the daughter of Annas had become the wife of Caiaphas.’

[JHN 18:13](https://ref.ly/Luke18:13)

* **Associated Passages:** John 18:13

## πεντηκοστή (id: 003908000000000)

πεντηκοστή
==========

πεντηκοστή (G4005)

noun \| \-ῆς \| πέντε

51\.8 · Festivals

Pentecost a Jewish harvest festival celebrated on the fiftieth day after Passover In most languages the festival of Pentecost is identified simply by a borrowed term based on the Greek expression πεντηκοστή. Some languages, however, use a phrase such as ‘the festival of the fiftieth day,’ and then in a footnote explain the fact that this was a harvest or thanksgiving festival occurring fifty days after Passover.

[ACT 2:1](https://ref.ly/John2:1) · [ACT 20:16](https://ref.ly/John20:16) · [1CO 16:8](https://ref.ly/Rom16:8)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 2:1; Acts 20:16; 1 Corinthians 16:8

31\.82 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Trust, Rely

to rely on, to trust in, to depend on, to have (complete) confidence in, confidence, trust to believe in something or someone to the extent of placing reliance or trust in or on In a number of languages trust or reliance is expressed idiomatically, for example, ‘to lean one’s weight on’ or ‘to hang upon’ or ‘to place oneself in the hands of.’

[2CO 1:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:15) · [2CO 3:4](https://ref.ly/1Cor3:4) · [2CO 8:22](https://ref.ly/1Cor8:22) · [2CO 10:2](https://ref.ly/1Cor10:2) · [EPH 3:12](https://ref.ly/Gal3:12) · [PHP 3:4](https://ref.ly/Eph3:4)

67\.35 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

about a marker of a point of time which is approximately simultaneous to another point of time

[MAT 20:3](https://ref.ly/Matt20:3) · [MAT 20:5](https://ref.ly/Matt20:5) · [MAT 20:6](https://ref.ly/Matt20:6) · [MAT 20:9](https://ref.ly/Matt20:9) · [MAT 27:46](https://ref.ly/Matt27:46) · [MRK 6:48](https://ref.ly/Matt6:48) · [ACT 10:3](https://ref.ly/John10:3) · [ACT 10:9](https://ref.ly/John10:9) · [ACT 22:6](https://ref.ly/John22:6)

89\.36 · Relations · Cause and/or Reason

for, on account of, because a marker of cause or reason as an implied content of speaking One may also interpret περί in [LUK 19:37](https://ref.ly/Mark19:37) as being primarily a matter of specification (see [89\.6](#89.6)). As in a number of instances in this domain, markers may have more than one semantic function.

[LUK 3:19](https://ref.ly/Mark3:19) · [LUK 3:19](https://ref.ly/Mark3:19) · [LUK 19:37](https://ref.ly/Mark19:37) · [LUK 24:4](https://ref.ly/Mark24:4) · [JHN 8:26](https://ref.ly/Luke8:26) · [JHN 10:33](https://ref.ly/Luke10:33) · [JHN 10:33](https://ref.ly/Luke10:33) · [1CO 1:4](https://ref.ly/Rom1:4) · [1TH 3:9](https://ref.ly/Col3:9) · [1JN 2:26](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:26)

90\.39 · Case · Benefaction

on behalf of (with the genitive) a marker of events which are indirectly involved in a beneficial activity A strictly literal translation of this expression in [1PE 3:18](https://ref.ly/Jas3:18) could be extremely misleading in some languages, since it would imply that Christ’s suffering in some way benefited or enhanced sins. Accordingly, it is necessary in a number of languages to translate as ‘because Christ once for all suffered for the sake of people who had sinned.’ Note a similar use of ὑπέρ in [GAL 1:4](https://ref.ly/2Cor1:4) (see [90\.36](#90.36)).

[JHN 12:6](https://ref.ly/Luke12:6) · [JHN 16:26](https://ref.ly/Luke16:26) · [JHN 17:9](https://ref.ly/Luke17:9) · [JHN 17:9](https://ref.ly/Luke17:9) · [JHN 17:9](https://ref.ly/Luke17:9) · [ACT 8:15](https://ref.ly/John8:15) · [ROM 8:3](https://ref.ly/Acts8:3) · [1TH 5:25](https://ref.ly/Col5:25) · [2TH 3:1](https://ref.ly/1Thess3:1) · [PHM 1:10](https://ref.ly/Titus1:10) · [HEB 5:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm5:3) · [HEB 10:26](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:26) · [HEB 11:40](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:40) · [HEB 13:18](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:18) · [1PE 3:18](https://ref.ly/Jas3:18) · [1JN 2:2](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:2) · [1JN 4:10](https://ref.ly/2Pet4:10)

89\.110 · Relations · Association

with, in the company of a marker of association, involving accompaniment Note that οἱ περὶ Παῦλον does not mean ‘those who were with Paul,’ but it includes Paul and those with him.

[MRK 16:8](https://ref.ly/Matt16:8) · [ACT 13:13](https://ref.ly/John13:13)

89\.6 · Relations · Specification

in relation to, with regard to, concerning a marker of a relation, usually involving content or topic

1 The general equivalence in meaning of περί and ὑπέρ in contexts introducing content can be readily seen from the tendency for scribes to interchange these terms in manuscripts. However, there may be certain subtle distinctions in meaning, but these cannot be determined from existing contexts.

## περιάγω (id: 003916000000000)

περιάγω
=======

περιάγω (G4013)

ἄγω

15\.23 · Linear Movement · Travel, Journey

to travel about, to wander about to move about from place to place, with significant changes in direction In order to communicate the meaning of ‘to wander about’ or ‘to travel about,’ it may be necessary to be rather specific, for example, ‘they first go in one direction and then in another’ or ‘they first go to one place and then off to another.’

[MAT 4:23](https://ref.ly/Matt4:23) · [MAT 9:35](https://ref.ly/Matt9:35) · [MAT 23:15](https://ref.ly/Matt23:15) · [MRK 6:6](https://ref.ly/Matt6:6) · [ACT 13:11](https://ref.ly/John13:11)

15\.170 · Linear Movement · Lead, Bring, Take

to take along, to bring along on a trip to take along or to bring along on a journey or as one travels about

[1CO 9:5](https://ref.ly/Rom9:5)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 4:23; Matthew 9:35; Matthew 23:15; Mark 6:6; Acts 13:11; 1 Corinthians 9:5


## περιαιρέω (id: 003917000000000)

περιαιρέω
=========

περιαιρέω (G4014)

aor.inf. περιελεῖν · ptc. περιελών · impf.pass.3sg. περιῃρεῖτο \| αἱρέομαι

15\.204 · Linear Movement · Carry, Bear

to take from around, to remove to remove something which is around something else

[ACT 27:40](https://ref.ly/John27:40) · [2CO 3:16](https://ref.ly/1Cor3:16)

54\.24 · Maritime Activities

to lift anchor, to sail off (a technical, nautical term) to raise the anchor in preparation for departing

[ACT 28:13](https://ref.ly/John28:13)

13\.38 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · State

to do away with, to remove, to eliminate to cause a state to cease1

[HEB 10:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:11)

68\.43 · Aspect · Cease, Stop

to give up, to stop, to quit to stop doing something, with the implication of complete cessation

[ACT 27:20](https://ref.ly/John27:20)

1 There are undoubtedly some significant differences of meaning in this series in [13\.38](#13.38), but it is not possible to determine the distinctive differences on the basis of existing contexts.

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 27:40; 2 Corinthians 3:16; Acts 28:13; Hebrews 10:11; Acts 27:20


## περιάπτω (id: 003918000000000)

περιάπτω
========

περιάπτω (G0681a)

aor.ptc. περιάψας \| ἅπτω

14\.65 · Physical Events and States · Burning

to ignite, to kindle, to set ablaze, to start a fire, to light a lamp to cause the process of burning to begin As already noted in the series of terms listed in [14\.63](#14.63), languages differ appreciably in terms employed for different phases and results of burning. For example, to set something on fire for the sake of providing warmth may be expressed by quite a different term from that which is used in speaking of the process of setting something on fire in order to destroy it.  
It is possible that περιάπτω, in view of the prefix περι\-, adds a component of ‘round about,’ suggesting that the blaze or fire was for the benefit of those who encircled the fire.

[LUK 22:55](https://ref.ly/Mark22:55)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 22:55

ἀστράπτω

to shine brightly around, to flash around to shine very brightly on an area surrounding an object See the discussion in [14\.44](#14.44).

[ACT 9:3](https://ref.ly/John9:3) · [ACT 22:6](https://ref.ly/John22:6)

to clothe to put on clothes, implying the clothing being completely around; see also [49\.5](#49.5)

[MAT 6:29](https://ref.ly/Matt6:29) · [MAT 6:31](https://ref.ly/Matt6:31) · [MAT 25:36](https://ref.ly/Matt25:36) · [MAT 25:38](https://ref.ly/Matt25:38) · [MAT 25:43](https://ref.ly/Matt25:43) · [MRK 14:51](https://ref.ly/Matt14:51) · [MRK 16:5](https://ref.ly/Matt16:5) · [LUK 12:27](https://ref.ly/Mark12:27) · [LUK 23:11](https://ref.ly/Mark23:11) · [JHN 19:2](https://ref.ly/Luke19:2) · [ACT 12:8](https://ref.ly/John12:8) · [REV 3:18](https://ref.ly/Jude3:18) · [REV 10:1](https://ref.ly/Jude10:1) · [REV 11:3](https://ref.ly/Jude11:3) · [REV 12:1](https://ref.ly/Jude12:1) · [REV 19:13](https://ref.ly/Jude19:13)

49\.5 · Activities Involving Clothing and Adorning

to clothe, to adorn to put on clothing to adorn the outward form of something1

[MAT 6:29](https://ref.ly/Matt6:29) · [LUK 12:27](https://ref.ly/Mark12:27) · [LUK 23:11](https://ref.ly/Mark23:11) · [JHN 19:2](https://ref.ly/Luke19:2) · [REV 3:5](https://ref.ly/Jude3:5) · [REV 4:4](https://ref.ly/Jude4:4) · [REV 7:9](https://ref.ly/Jude7:9) · [REV 7:13](https://ref.ly/Jude7:13) · [REV 17:4](https://ref.ly/Jude17:4) · [REV 18:16](https://ref.ly/Jude18:16) · [REV 19:8](https://ref.ly/Jude19:8)

1 As is evident from the contexts cited in [49\.3](#49.3), ἀμφιέζω, ἀμφιέννυμι, and περιβάλλω occur not only in the sense of putting on clothes but in the more generic sense of adorning the outward appearance of something, for these verbs may occur not only with persons who are literally clothed but also with plants which are made attractive.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 6:29; Matthew 6:31; Matthew 25:36; Matthew 25:38; Matthew 25:43; Mark 14:51; Mark 16:5; Luke 12:27; Luke 23:11; John 19:2; Acts 12:8; Revelation 3:18; Revelation 10:1; Revelation 11:3; Revelation 12:1; Revelation 19:13; Revelation 3:5; Revelation 4:4; Revelation 7:9; Revelation 7:13; Revelation 17:4; Revelation 18:16; Revelation 19:8

## περιβόλαιον (id: 003922000000000)

περιβόλαιον
===========

περιβόλαιον (G4018)

noun \| \-ου \| βάλλω

6\.163 · Artifacts · Cloth, Leather, and Objects Made of Such Materials

clothing, apparel clothing as a covering1

[1CO 11:15](https://ref.ly/Rom11:15) · [HEB 1:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:12)

6\.172 · Artifacts · Cloth, Leather, and Objects Made of Such Materials

cloak, coat, robe any type of outer garment2 The choice of an equivalent for these terms relating to ‘outer garments’ will depend largely upon the specific context and the usage in each receptor language. The most common equivalent is a term meaning ‘coat.’

[HEB 1:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:12)

1 περιβόλαιον\[a] and σκέπασμα could also be treated as derivatives based on their respective corresponding events involving the covering of an object. The derivatives would then be merely instruments for covering. Since, however, in the NT these two terms are so closely associated with clothing as covering, they are here treated in this subdomain. 2 There are no doubt certain minor distinctions in meaning for the four terms in [6\.172](#6.172), but it is impossible to determine the distinctive features on the basis of existing contexts. There are considerable differences of opinion about φαιλόνης, regarded by many as a loan word from Latin paenula.

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Corinthians 11:15; Hebrews 1:12

1 LUK 5:9 The expression θάμβος γὰρ περιέσχεν αὐτόν in [LUK 5:9](https://ref.ly/Mark5:9) is a clear example of a syntactic structure being formally the converse of the semantic structure. One may say amazement seized him, but clearly it is the person who experiences the amazement.

## περισσεύω (id: 003958000000000)

περισσεύω
=========

περισσεύω (G4052)

περισσεία · περίσσευμα · περισσός · περισσότερος · περισσοτέρως · περισσῶς · ὑπερεκπερισσοῦ · ὑπερπερισσεύω · ὑπερπερισσῶς

59\.52 · Quantity · Abundance, Excess, Sparing

to abound, to be in abundance, to be a lot of, to exist in a large quantity, to be left over (derivative of περισσός\[c] ‘abundant,’ [59\.51](#59.51)) to be or exist in abundance, with the implication of being considerably more than what would be expected In a number of languages it is extremely difficult to find an equivalent of ‘to abound.’ Moreover, it is not at all easy to find an expression of quantity which seems to go satisfactorily with ‘the sufferings of Christ.’ In some languages the closest equivalent of this expression in [2CO 1:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:5) may be ‘for just as we suffer a great deal, even as Christ suffered.’ It would be wrong, however, to use an expression for ‘suffering’ which would suggest vicarious suffering.  
The rendering of περισσεύω in [ACT 16:5](https://ref.ly/John16:5) as ‘to grow’ results from the expression τῷ ἀριθμῷ. In a number of languages the equivalent would be ‘the number of people in the churches increased every day’ or ‘how many believers there were was more and more each day.’

[MRK 12:44](https://ref.ly/Matt12:44) · [LUK 12:15](https://ref.ly/Mark12:15) · [LUK 21:4](https://ref.ly/Mark21:4) · [JHN 6:12](https://ref.ly/Luke6:12) · [JHN 6:13](https://ref.ly/Luke6:13) · [ACT 16:5](https://ref.ly/John16:5) · [ROM 3:7](https://ref.ly/Acts3:7) · [ROM 5:15](https://ref.ly/Acts5:15) · [1CO 15:58](https://ref.ly/Rom15:58) · [2CO 1:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:5) · [2CO 1:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:5) · [2CO 3:9](https://ref.ly/1Cor3:9) · [2CO 4:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:15) · [2CO 9:8](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:8) · [2CO 9:12](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:12) · [PHP 1:26](https://ref.ly/Eph1:26) · [COL 2:7](https://ref.ly/Phil2:7) · [1TH 4:10](https://ref.ly/Col4:10)

59\.54 · Quantity · Abundance, Excess, Sparing

to provide in abundance, to provide a great deal of, to cause to be abundant to cause something to exist in an abundance In some languages, however, it may be very strange to speak of having or receiving an abundance of grace. The closest equivalent for [EPH 1:7](https://ref.ly/Gal1:7)\-[EPH 1:8](https://ref.ly/Gal1:8) may be ‘grace which he caused us to experience abundantly’ or ‘grace which he showed to us in an abundant way.’

[MAT 13:12](https://ref.ly/Matt13:12) · [MAT 25:29](https://ref.ly/Matt25:29) · [1CO 14:12](https://ref.ly/Rom14:12) · [2CO 9:8](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:8) · [EPH 1:8](https://ref.ly/Gal1:8)

57\.24 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Have Sufficient

to have (much) more than enough, to have an overabundance to have such an abundance as to be more than sufficient

[MAT 14:20](https://ref.ly/Matt14:20) · [MAT 15:37](https://ref.ly/Matt15:37) · [LUK 9:17](https://ref.ly/Mark9:17) · [LUK 15:17](https://ref.ly/Mark15:17) · [ROM 15:13](https://ref.ly/Acts15:13) · [1CO 14:12](https://ref.ly/Rom14:12) · [PHP 4:12](https://ref.ly/Eph4:12) · [PHP 4:12](https://ref.ly/Eph4:12) · [PHP 4:18](https://ref.ly/Eph4:18)

78\.31 · Degree · More Than, Less Than \[Comparative Degree]

very great, excessive, extremely, emphatic, surpassing, all the more, much greater a degree which is considerably in excess of some point on an implied or explicit scale of extent In [PHP 1:9](https://ref.ly/Eph1:9) it is clear that μᾶλλον καὶ μᾶλλον (see [78\.28](#78.28)) serves to emphasize the degree expressed in περισσεύω. The verb περισσεύητε in [1TH 4:1](https://ref.ly/Col4:1) may be regarded as a kind of substitute verb referring to περιπατεῖτε and indicating essentially a matter of degree. This is, of course, further reinforced by μᾶλλον ([78\.28](#78.28)).

[MAT 5:20](https://ref.ly/Matt5:20) · [2CO 8:2](https://ref.ly/1Cor8:2) · [2CO 8:7](https://ref.ly/1Cor8:7) · [2CO 8:7](https://ref.ly/1Cor8:7) · [PHP 1:9](https://ref.ly/Eph1:9) · [1TH 4:1](https://ref.ly/Col4:1) · [1TH 4:10](https://ref.ly/Col4:10)

78\.32 · Degree · More Than, Less Than \[Comparative Degree]

to cause to be intense, to cause to be more, to cause to grow to cause an increase in the degree of some experience or state1 It would seem that πλεονάσαι and περισσεύσαι in [1TH 3:12](https://ref.ly/Col3:12) simply reinforce the meaning of intense degree. It is assumed that these two expressions are not completely synonymous, but it is difficult to determine precisely how they may contrast. It may simply be that περισσεύσαι is somewhat more emphatic.

[1TH 3:12](https://ref.ly/Col3:12)

65\.47 · Value · Advantageous, Not Advantageous

to have a greater benefit, to experience a superior advantage to experience superior benefit or advantage, implying some type of comparison In indicating ‘a superior advantage’ it may be necessary to be quite specific about the basis for the comparison. For example, in [1CO 8:8](https://ref.ly/Rom8:8) it may be necessary to translate as ‘if we do eat, we will be no better off than if we did not eat,’ and in [ROM 3:9](https://ref.ly/Acts3:9) one may translate ‘what then; are we decidedly better off than the others are?’

[1CO 8:8](https://ref.ly/Rom8:8)

1 It is possible that περισσεύω\[e] differs somewhat from πλεονάζω\[b] in intensity, implying perhaps an overabundance of something, but it is difficult to confirm such a distinction on the basis of existing texts.

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 12:44; Luke 12:15; Luke 21:4; John 6:12; John 6:13; Acts 16:5; Romans 3:7; Romans 5:15; 1 Corinthians 15:58; 2 Corinthians 1:5; 2 Corinthians 3:9; 2 Corinthians 4:15; 2 Corinthians 9:8; 2 Corinthians 9:12; Philippians 1:26; Colossians 2:7; 1 Thessalonians 4:10; Matthew 13:12; Matthew 25:29; 1 Corinthians 14:12; Ephesians 1:8; Matthew 14:20; Matthew 15:37; Luke 9:17; Luke 15:17; Romans 15:13; Philippians 4:12; Philippians 4:18; Matthew 5:20; 2 Corinthians 8:2; 2 Corinthians 8:7; Philippians 1:9; 1 Thessalonians 4:1; 1 Thessalonians 3:12; 1 Corinthians 8:8

adj \| \-ή, όν \| περισσεύω

special advantage, of greater benefit pertaining to causing a decided or distinct advantage

[ROM 3:1](https://ref.ly/Acts3:1)

that which is more than, more than enough, beyond the norm, abundantly, superfluous pertaining to a quantity so abundant as to be considerably more than what one would expect or anticipate1 For another interpretation of περισσός in [2CO 9:1](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:1), see [71\.40](#71.40).

[MAT 5:37](https://ref.ly/Matt5:37) · [MRK 6:51](https://ref.ly/Matt6:51) · [JHN 10:10](https://ref.ly/Luke10:10) · [2CO 9:1](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:1)

71\.40 · Mode · Necessary, Unnecessary

unnecessary, not required pertaining to being unnecessary in view of being superfluous For another interpretation of περισσός in [2CO 9:1](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:1), see [59\.51](#59.51).

[2CO 9:1](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:1)

78\.31 · Degree · More Than, Less Than \[Comparative Degree]

**Collocation:** περισσότερος

very great, excessive, extremely, emphatic, surpassing, all the more, much greater a degree which is considerably in excess of some point on an implied or explicit scale of extent

[MAT 11:9](https://ref.ly/Matt11:9) · [MRK 7:36](https://ref.ly/Matt7:36) · [MRK 12:33](https://ref.ly/Matt12:33) · [MRK 12:40](https://ref.ly/Matt12:40) · [LUK 7:26](https://ref.ly/Mark7:26) · [LUK 12:4](https://ref.ly/Mark12:4) · [LUK 12:48](https://ref.ly/Mark12:48) · [LUK 20:47](https://ref.ly/Mark20:47) · [1CO 12:23](https://ref.ly/Rom12:23) · [1CO 12:24](https://ref.ly/Rom12:24) · [1CO 15:10](https://ref.ly/Rom15:10) · [2CO 2:7](https://ref.ly/1Cor2:7) · [HEB 6:17](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:17) · [HEB 7:15](https://ref.ly/Phlm7:15)

78\.20 · Degree · Much, Little \[Positive\-Negative Degree]

**Collocation:** ἐκ περισσοῦ

exceedingly, extremely, decidedly, excessively (an idiom, literally: from excess) an extremely high point on a scale of extent and implying excess In [MRK 6:51](https://ref.ly/Matt6:51) both λίαν ([78\.1](#78.1)) and ἐκ περισσοῦ are expressions of degree and they reinforce one another.

[MRK 6:51](https://ref.ly/Matt6:51)

1 A close comparison with Domain 78 Degree, Subdomain B More Than, Less Than will indicate how closely related are the meanings of degree and those of quantity. In fact, in a number of instances, the same expressions may be interpreted either as quantity or as degree.

## περιστερά (id: 003962000000000)

περιστερά
=========

περιστερά (G4058)

noun \| \-ᾶς

4\.44 · Animals · Birds

dove, pigeon Asiatic Rock dove: bird with a blue\-gray color with a pinkish sheen to the neck feathers and a black tip on its tail; lives in large colonies and eats mainly seeds; courts, mates, and has nests several times a year; nests on rock ledges; Columba via; used as food and for sacrifices; regarded as swift, docile, and innocent Though in English a relatively clear distinction is made between ‘doves’ (which have pointed tails) and ‘pigeons’ (which have square off tails), there seems to be no such distinction in the use of περιστερά and τρυγών in the Greek of the New Testament.  
There are, however, certain problems involved in the connotations of terms relating to pigeons or doves. According to ancient zoology, the dove was thought to have no bile, and consequently, therefore, to be a very peaceful and clean bird. It thus became a symbol of Christian virtue and of gentleness (as in [MAT 10:16](https://ref.ly/Matt10:16)). In reality, however, doves and pigeons are quite aggressive, and a group of pigeons are frequently known to peck an injured bird to death. Therefore, in some societies pigeons or doves are not regarded as gentle or peaceful, but rather as wicked and harmful. In fact, in some cultures doves are thought to be symbols of death and of evil. Under such circumstances it is important, therefore, to have some marginal note so as to indicate clearly the symbolic significance of such birds in biblical contexts.

[MAT 3:16](https://ref.ly/Matt3:16) · [MAT 10:16](https://ref.ly/Matt10:16) · [MAT 21:12](https://ref.ly/Matt21:12) · [MRK 1:10](https://ref.ly/Matt1:10) · [MRK 11:15](https://ref.ly/Matt11:15) · [LUK 2:24](https://ref.ly/Mark2:24) · [LUK 3:22](https://ref.ly/Mark3:22) · [JHN 1:32](https://ref.ly/Luke1:32) · [JHN 2:14](https://ref.ly/Luke2:14) · [JHN 2:16](https://ref.ly/Luke2:16)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 3:16; Matthew 10:16; Matthew 21:12; Mark 1:10; Mark 11:15; Luke 2:24; Luke 3:22; John 1:32; John 2:14; John 2:16


## περιτέμνω (id: 003963000000000)

περιτέμνω
=========

περιτέμνω (G4059)

aor. περιέτεμον · inf. περιτεμεῖν · pf.pass. περιτέτμημαι · aor.pass. περιετμήθην · inf. περιτμηθῆναι \| τομός

53\.51 · Religious Activities · Dedicate, Consecrate

to circumcise, circumcision to cut off the foreskin of the male genital organ as a religious rite involving consecration and ethnic identification In the case of societies which practice circumcision, there is normally a perfectly satisfactory term to designate this type of operation, though it may not have any religious significance but only be a part of puberty ritual. For societies which do not practice circumcision or are not well acquainted with such a practice, a term which specifically describes the operation may be both offensive and vulgar. It may therefore be necessary to use a borrowing or to employ a somewhat obscure term such as ‘to cut around’ and then to explain the specific meaning of the term or phrase in a glossary.  
Even in the case of societies which practice circumcision, there may be problems in comprehension of the significance of such an act, and these difficulties may require marginal notes. If, for example, a society practices circumcision only in connection with puberty rites, it may seem inexplicable that Jesus would have been circumcised on the eighth day after his birth ([LUK 2:21](https://ref.ly/Mark2:21)). In many societies such an act would seem to be either a deed of extreme cruelty to a baby or a sign of mature sexuality in an infant.

[LUK 1:59](https://ref.ly/Mark1:59) · [LUK 2:21](https://ref.ly/Mark2:21) · [JHN 7:22](https://ref.ly/Luke7:22) · [ACT 7:8](https://ref.ly/John7:8) · [ACT 15:1](https://ref.ly/John15:1) · [ACT 15:5](https://ref.ly/John15:5) · [ACT 16:3](https://ref.ly/John16:3) · [ACT 21:21](https://ref.ly/John21:21) · [1CO 7:18](https://ref.ly/Rom7:18) · [1CO 7:18](https://ref.ly/Rom7:18) · [GAL 2:3](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:3) · [GAL 5:2](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:2) · [GAL 5:3](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:3) · [GAL 6:12](https://ref.ly/2Cor6:12) · [GAL 6:13](https://ref.ly/2Cor6:13) · [GAL 6:13](https://ref.ly/2Cor6:13) · [COL 2:11](https://ref.ly/Phil2:11)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 1:59; Luke 2:21; John 7:22; Acts 7:8; Acts 15:1; Acts 15:5; Acts 16:3; Acts 21:21; 1 Corinthians 7:18; Galatians 2:3; Galatians 5:2; Galatians 5:3; Galatians 6:12; Galatians 6:13; Colossians 2:11

## περίχωρος (id: 003970000000000)

περίχωρος
=========

περίχωρος (G4066)

adj \| \-ον \| χῶρος (a place) · χωρέω

1\.80 · Geographical Objects and Features · Sociopolitical Areas

surrounding region an area or region around or near some central or focal point In certain contexts, however, περίχωρος may include not only the surrounding region but also the point of reference, for example, τῆς περιχώρου τῶν Γερασηνῶν ‘the Gerasenes and the people living around them’ [LUK 8:37](https://ref.ly/Mark8:37). In a context such as [MAT 3:5](https://ref.ly/Matt3:5), ἡ περίχωρος τοῦ Ἰορδάνου, it is difficult to speak of ‘the area surrounding the Jordan River,’ since a river is not like a city which would constitute a center. Accordingly, one must often translate ἡ περίχωρος τοῦ Ἰορδάνου as ‘the land on both sides of the River Jordan.’

## πετεινός (id: 003975000000000)

πετεινός
========

πετεινός (G4071) — πετεινόν

noun \| \-οῦ \| πέτομαι

4\.38 · Animals · Birds

bird any kind of bird, wild or domestic All languages have at least some terms for birds, but there may be no generic term which identifies birds while excluding all other creatures which fly. For example, in some languages a term which includes all kinds of birds also includes flying insects and bats. However, since in most instances such a term has birds as its primary reference, it can usually be employed in contexts in which birds are more or less readily identified by other expressions in the passage. In other languages generic terms for birds may identify only certain classes of birds, for example, birds of prey, water birds, and birds of the forest.

[MAT 13:4](https://ref.ly/Matt13:4) · [MRK 4:4](https://ref.ly/Matt4:4) · [LUK 12:24](https://ref.ly/Mark12:24) · [ROM 1:23](https://ref.ly/Acts1:23)1 · [JAS 3:7](https://ref.ly/Heb3:7)1

4\.41 · Animals · Birds

**Collocation:** πετεινὰ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ

birds, wild birds (an idiom, literally: birds of the sky) wild birds in contrast with domesticated birds such as chickens A literal rendering of πετεινὰ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ, either as ‘birds of the air’ or ‘birds of the sky,’ may be misleading, since ‘birds of the air’ might imply that these are birds made out of air, and ‘birds of the sky’ might suggest they are birds which remain always in the sky. In some languages the equivalent expression is ‘birds of the trees’ or ‘birds of the winds.’

[MAT 6:26](https://ref.ly/Matt6:26) · [MAT 8:20](https://ref.ly/Matt8:20) · [MAT 13:32](https://ref.ly/Matt13:32) · [MRK 4:32](https://ref.ly/Matt4:32) · [LUK 8:5](https://ref.ly/Mark8:5) · [LUK 9:58](https://ref.ly/Mark9:58) · [LUK 13:19](https://ref.ly/Mark13:19) · [ACT 10:12](https://ref.ly/John10:12) · [ACT 11:6](https://ref.ly/John11:6)1

1 ROM 1:23 · ACT 11:6 · JAS 3:7 In [ROM 1:23](https://ref.ly/Acts1:23), [ACT 11:6](https://ref.ly/John11:6), and [JAS 3:7](https://ref.ly/Heb3:7) πετεινός may possibly include insects, but this is not likely. The classifications in these contexts do not necessarily reflect exhaustive distinctions.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 13:4; Mark 4:4; Luke 12:24; Romans 1:23; James 3:7; Matthew 6:26; Matthew 8:20; Matthew 13:32; Mark 4:32; Luke 8:5; Luke 9:58; Luke 13:19; Acts 10:12; Acts 11:6
