## πέτρα (id: 003977000000000)

πέτρα
=====

πέτρα (G4073)

noun \| \-ας \| πετρῶδες

2\.21 · Natural Substances · Earth, Mud, Sand, Rock

rock, bedrock bedrock (possibly covered with a thin layer of soil), rocky crags, or mountain ledges, in contrast with separate pieces of rock normally referred to as λίθος\[a] (see [2\.23](#2.23))1 ὁμοιωθήσεται ἀνδρὶ φρονίμῳ, ὅστις ᾠκοδόμησεν αὐτοῦ τὴν οἰκίαν ἐπὶ τὴν πέτραν ‘he is like a wise man who built his house on bedrock’ [MAT 7:24](https://ref.ly/Matt7:24); ἔθηκεν αὐτὸν ἐν μνημείῳ ὅ ἦν λελατομημένον ἐκ πέτρας ‘they then put him in a grave which had been hewn from rock’ [MRK 15:46](https://ref.ly/Matt15:46); λέγουσιν τοῖς ὄρεσιν καὶ ταῖς πέτραις, Πέσετε ἐφ ̓ ἡμᾶς ‘they said to the mountains and to the rock cliffs, Fall on us’ [REV 6:16](https://ref.ly/Jude6:16). In these three contexts πέτρα must be translated in some languages by three quite different terms. Bedrock, that is to say, the rock which lies horizontally and often just below the surface, may be referred to by one term, while rock with an exposed face into which a tomb could be hewn would be quite different. Similarly, the rocks which would be called upon to fall upon people would be rendered by a term referring to ‘cliffs’ or ‘precipices.’  
In [LUK 8:6](https://ref.ly/Mark8:6) the term πέτρα in ἕτερον κατέπεσεν ἐπὶ τὴν πέτραν ‘other (seed) fell on rocky ground’ refers to bedrock which may be covered with a thin layer of soil. One may translate such an expression as ‘thin ground over rock’ or ‘just a little soil on top of rock.’  
In those passages which involve a play on words with the name Πέτρος ‘Peter,’ πέτρα refers to bedrock, that is to say, the rock on which a foundation may be placed.

[MAT 7:24](https://ref.ly/Matt7:24) · [MAT 7:25](https://ref.ly/Matt7:25) · [MAT 16:18](https://ref.ly/Matt16:18) · [MAT 27:51](https://ref.ly/Matt27:51) · [MAT 27:60](https://ref.ly/Matt27:60) · [MRK 15:46](https://ref.ly/Matt15:46) · [LUK 6:48](https://ref.ly/Mark6:48) · [LUK 8:6](https://ref.ly/Mark8:6) · [LUK 8:13](https://ref.ly/Mark8:13) · [ROM 9:33](https://ref.ly/Acts9:33)1 · [1CO 10:4](https://ref.ly/Rom10:4) · [1CO 10:4](https://ref.ly/Rom10:4) · [1PE 2:8](https://ref.ly/Jas2:8)1 · [REV 6:15](https://ref.ly/Jude6:15) · [REV 6:16](https://ref.ly/Jude6:16)

1 ROM 9:33 · 1PE 2:8 In [ROM 9:33](https://ref.ly/Acts9:33) and [1PE 2:8](https://ref.ly/Jas2:8) πέτρα occurs in parallelism with λίθος stone, as a quotation from [ISA 8:14](https://ref.ly/Isa8:14). On the basis of this usage one might argue that λίθος and πέτρα are completely synonymous, at least in this context. One would then have to describe πέτρα as having two different meanings, one rock and another stone. Parallelism, however, does not mean identity of meaning. In fact, parallelism (in contrast with tautology) normally implies slight differences of meaning in corresponding lexical items.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 7:24; Matthew 7:25; Matthew 16:18; Matthew 27:51; Matthew 27:60; Mark 15:46; Luke 6:48; Luke 8:6; Luke 8:13; Romans 9:33; 1 Corinthians 10:4; 1 Peter 2:8; Revelation 6:15; Revelation 6:16

========

rocky ground (derivative of πέτρα ‘bedrock,’ [2\.21](#2.21)) a rocky substance or bedrock covered by a thin layer of earth

[MAT 13:5](https://ref.ly/Matt13:5) · [MAT 13:20](https://ref.ly/Matt13:20) · [MRK 4:5](https://ref.ly/Matt4:5) · [MRK 4:16](https://ref.ly/Matt4:16)

## πήγανον (id: 003980000000000)

πήγανον
=======

πήγανον (G4076)

noun \| \-ου

3\.22 · Plants · Plants That Are Not Trees

rue an evergreen aromatic plant used for seasoning In the parallel passage ([MAT 23:23](https://ref.ly/Matt23:23)) ἄνηθον ‘dill’ and κύμινον ‘cumin’ occur (see [3\.24](#3.24)\-[3\.25](#3.25)).  
In designating ‘rue’ as well as ἡδύοσμον 'mint', ἄνηθον ‘dill,’ and κύμινον ‘cumin,’ it is probably wise to borrow a term and use a classifier, for example, ‘a seasoning herb called rue.’

[LUK 11:42](https://ref.ly/Mark11:42)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 11:42


## πηγή (id: 003981000000000)

πηγή
====

πηγή (G4077)

noun \| \-ῆς

1\.78 · Geographical Objects and Features · Bodies of Water

spring a source of water flowing onto the surface or into a pool somewhat below ground level πηγή\[a] spring differs from φρέαρ\[a] and πηγή\[b] well (see [7\.57](#7.57)) in that a πηγή\[a] is normally a natural source of water, in contrast with φρέαρ\[a] and πηγή\[b], which are dug and into which a πηγή\[a] may be said to flow.

[JHN 4:14](https://ref.ly/Luke4:14) · [JAS 3:11](https://ref.ly/Heb3:11) · [2PE 2:17](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:17) · [REV 7:17](https://ref.ly/Jude7:17) · [REV 8:10](https://ref.ly/Jude8:10) · [REV 14:7](https://ref.ly/Jude14:7) · [REV 16:4](https://ref.ly/Jude16:4) · [REV 21:6](https://ref.ly/Jude21:6)

7\.57 · Constructions · Constructions for Holding Water

well deep constructions, often walled with stone, at the bottom of which was a pool of water If there was a relatively ready flow of water, a well could very appropriately be called a πηγή\[a] 'spring,' [1\.78](#1.78) (and this may be the reason for πηγή\[a]. However, both terms are used interchangeably in certain contexts; compare, for example, πηγή in [JHN 4:6](https://ref.ly/Luke4:6) and φρέαρ in [JHN 4:11](https://ref.ly/Luke4:11), both referring to Jacob’s well at Sychar.

[JHN 4:6](https://ref.ly/Luke4:6) · [JHN 4:6](https://ref.ly/Luke4:6)

23\.182 · Physiological Processes and States · Sickness, Disease, Weakness

**Collocation:** πηγὴ αἵματος

loss of blood, menstrual flow, bleeding (a set phrase, literally: fountain of blood) the loss of blood through menstrual bleeding1

[MRK 5:29](https://ref.ly/Matt5:29)

1 The expressions in [23\.181](#23.181) and [23\.182](#23.182) all refer to a menstrual flow of blood, but the phrases πηγὴ αἵματος and ῥύσις αἵματος seem to be restricted primarily to this meaning, while αἱμορροέω in non\-biblical contexts means simply to suffer a loss of blood or to bleed, but it may, of course, refer to menstrual bleeding. Because of the wider range of reference in αἱμορροέω, this term has been listed separately.

* **Associated Passages:** John 4:14; James 3:11; 2 Peter 2:17; Revelation 7:17; Revelation 8:10; Revelation 14:7; Revelation 16:4; Revelation 21:6; John 4:6; Mark 5:29

πηδάλιον
========

πηδάλιον (G4079)

noun \| \-ου

6\.50 · Artifacts · Boats and Parts of Boats

rudder a large plank at the stern of a ship used to direct its course For some ships the rudder may have been simply a large steering paddle. For languages which have no technical term, a rudder may be described as ‘an instrument for steering the ship’ or ‘an instrument for making the ship go the way one wants it to go.’

how large, what large an interrogative of large size

## πηλός (id: 003985000000000)

πηλός
=====

πηλός (G4081)

noun \| \-οῦ

2\.18 · Natural Substances · Earth, Mud, Sand, Rock

mud, clay moistened earth of a clay consistency πηλός contrasts with βόρβορος ‘watery mud’ ([2\.17](#2.17)) and refers to moist earth with a consistency of clay. It may be translated in some languages as ‘sticky soil’ or ‘clay\-like mud’ or ‘moist dirt.’  
In [ROM 9:21](https://ref.ly/Acts9:21) (ἢ οὐκ ἔχει ἐξουσίαν ὁ κεραμεὺς τοῦ πηλοῦ ‘or doesn’t the man who makes the pots have the right to use the clay as he wishes’) the reference is specifically to potter’s clay. The meaning of πηλός, therefore, may be defined as any kind of moist earth with clay consistency. πηλός includes the area of meaning of κέραμος ‘potter’s clay’ (a meaning not occurring in the NT).  
Since pottery is so widely known throughout the world, there is normally no difficulty in obtaining a term which refers to potter’s clay. Even when the making of pottery is not known, it is usually possible to obtain a satisfactory term for such clay, for example, ‘mud that can be molded’ or ‘mud for shaping pots.’

[JHN 9:6](https://ref.ly/Luke9:6) · [JHN 9:6](https://ref.ly/Luke9:6) · [JHN 9:11](https://ref.ly/Luke9:11) · [JHN 9:14](https://ref.ly/Luke9:14) · [JHN 9:15](https://ref.ly/Luke9:15) · [ROM 9:21](https://ref.ly/Acts9:21)

* **Associated Passages:** John 9:6; John 9:11; John 9:14; John 9:15; Romans 9:21

πηρόω

**Collocation:** πηρόω τὴν καρδίαν

to close someone’s mind, to make someone unable to learn (an idiom, literally: to maim the heart) to cause someone to be unwilling to learn This word is found as a variant reading only: πεπήρωκεν αὐτῶν τὴν καρδίαν ‘he has made their hearts unable to learn’ [JHN 12:40](https://ref.ly/Luke12:40) (apparatus).

## πῆχυς (id: 003988000000000)

πῆχυς
=====

πῆχυς (G4083)

noun \| \-εως

81\.25 · Spacial Dimensions · Specific Measures of Length

cubit, eighteen inches, half meter traditionally the distance from the elbow to the end of the fingers, about eighteen inches or one\-half meter The interpretation of πῆχυς in [MAT 6:27](https://ref.ly/Matt6:27) as a measurement of stature rather than length of life may be justified as an instance of literary hyperbole or exaggeration. Most modern translations, however, interpret πῆχυς in this context as a reference to length of life rather than as a measurement of height (see discussion under ἡλικία\[a] in [67\.151](#67.151)).

## πικρία (id: 003993000000000)

πικρία
======

πικρία (G4088)

noun \| \-ας \| πικραίνω

79\.40 · Features of Objects · Sweet, Bitter, Tasteless

**Collocation:** εἰς χολὴν πικρίας εἰμί

bitterness, bitter with a bitter taste (occurring only in the idiom χολὴ πικρίας) As a part of the idiom εἰς χολὴν πικρίας εἰμί, πικρίας is to be understood in a literal sense but the idiom as such means to be particularly envious or resentful of someone (see [88\.166](#88.166)).

[ACT 8:23](https://ref.ly/John8:23) · [HEB 12:15](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:15)

88\.201 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Hate, Hateful

bitter resentment, spite, bitterness (a figurative extension of meaning of πικρία\[a] ‘bitter taste,’ [79\.40](#79.40)) a state of sharp, intense resentment or hate

[MAT 22:10](https://ref.ly/Matt22:10) · [MAT 27:48](https://ref.ly/Matt27:48) · [LUK 1:15](https://ref.ly/Mark1:15) · [LUK 1:41](https://ref.ly/Mark1:41) · [LUK 1:67](https://ref.ly/Mark1:67) · [LUK 5:7](https://ref.ly/Mark5:7) · [ACT 2:4](https://ref.ly/John2:4) · [ACT 4:8](https://ref.ly/John4:8) · [ACT 4:31](https://ref.ly/John4:31) · [ACT 5:17](https://ref.ly/John5:17) · [ACT 9:17](https://ref.ly/John9:17) · [ACT 13:9](https://ref.ly/John13:9) · [ACT 13:45](https://ref.ly/John13:45) · [ACT 19:29](https://ref.ly/John19:29)

13\.106 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Happen

to cause to happen, to make happen, to fulfill to cause to happen, with the implication of fulfilling some purpose1

[LUK 21:22](https://ref.ly/Mark21:22)

67\.70 · (πίμπλαμαι\[a]) · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Duration of Time: Beginning, End

to complete, to come to an end to come to the end of a period of time, with the implication of the completion of an implied purpose or plan2 In a number of languages it may be difficult to speak of a period of time being ‘complete.’ In [LUK 1:23](https://ref.ly/Mark1:23) one may often translate as ‘when he had finished his period of service’ or even ‘when he had done his work.’ In the case of [ACT 7:30](https://ref.ly/John7:30), the expression ‘after forty years were completed’ may be rendered in some languages as ‘after forty years were passed’ or ‘when forty years had happened.’

[LUK 1:23](https://ref.ly/Mark1:23) · [LUK 1:57](https://ref.ly/Mark1:57) · [LUK 2:6](https://ref.ly/Mark2:6) · [LUK 2:21](https://ref.ly/Mark2:21) · [LUK 2:22](https://ref.ly/Mark2:22)

78\.46 · (πίμπλαμαι\[b]) · Degree · Completely, Enough \[Completive Degree]

to be completely, to be entirely, to be full of, to be totally to experience a complete degree of involvement in some event or state

[LUK 4:28](https://ref.ly/Mark4:28) · [LUK 5:26](https://ref.ly/Mark5:26) · [LUK 6:11](https://ref.ly/Mark6:11) · [ACT 3:10](https://ref.ly/John3:10)

1 There are undoubtedly certain subtle differences of meaning in this set of terms in [13\.106](#13.106), but the distinctive features cannot be determined from existing contexts. 2 In a number of contexts it is difficult to determine whether the meanings in [67\.69](#67.69) and [67\.70](#67.70) are primarily temporal or aspectual. If the meaning in any one context seems to focus upon the completion of an activity, then the meaning should be classified as aspectual (Domain 68 Aspect), but if the emphasis would seem to be primarily upon the end point of an activity, then one may legitimately classify these meanings as being temporal.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 22:10; Matthew 27:48; Luke 1:15; Luke 1:41; Luke 1:67; Luke 5:7; Acts 2:4; Acts 4:8; Acts 4:31; Acts 5:17; Acts 9:17; Acts 13:9; Acts 13:45; Acts 19:29; Luke 21:22; Luke 1:23; Luke 1:57; Luke 2:6; Luke 2:21; Luke 2:22; Luke 4:28; Luke 5:26; Luke 6:11; Acts 3:10


## πίμπρημι (id: 003998000000000)

πίμπρημι
========

πίμπρημι (G4092) — πίμπραμαι

ἐμπί\[μ]πρημι · καταπίμπρημι

23\.159 · (πίμπραμαι\[a]) · Physiological Processes and States · Sickness, Disease, Weakness

to have a fever, fever to be sick with a fever Though in a number of languages one can say ‘a person has a fever,’ in some languages the reverse expression is used, for example, ‘the fever has a person.’ Similarly, instead of saying ‘a person has a disease,’ it is ‘the disease which grabs a person.’ One may often speak of a fever as ‘to become hot’ or ‘to burn’ or ‘to be sick with burning.’  
It is also possible to understand πίμπρημι in [ACT 28:6](https://ref.ly/John28:6) as a process of swelling up (see [23\.163](#23.163)).

[ACT 28:6](https://ref.ly/John28:6)

23\.163 · (πίμπραμαι\[b]) · Physiological Processes and States · Sickness, Disease, Weakness

to swell up It is also possible to understand πίμπρημι in [ACT 28:6](https://ref.ly/John28:6) as being sick with a fever (see [23\.159](#23.159)).

[ACT 28:6](https://ref.ly/John28:6)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 28:6

14\.35 · Physical Events and States · Events Involving Liquids and Dry Masses

to absorb, to soak up (a figurative extension of meaning of πίνω\[a] ‘to drink,’ [23\.34](#23.34)) the action of a material which soaks up a liquid Instead of speaking about ‘the ground absorbing the rain’ or ‘... drinking up the rain,’ it is often more natural to describe the event as ‘the rain disappearing into the ground’ or ‘the rain flowing into the ground.’

[HEB 6:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:7)

24\.81 · Sensory Events and States · Pain, Suffering

**Collocation:** πίνω ποτήριον

to undergo a difficult experience, to drink the cup, to suffer (an idiom, literally: to drink a cup) to undergo a trying, difficult experience In a number of languages it is not possible to use the phrase ‘to drink the cup,’ since this will almost inevitably refer to the drinking of a poison cup as a means of proving innocence in an ordeal. Even if one uses a literal rendering of the idiom ‘to drink the cup,’ it is usually necessary to have some explanation as to the significance of the idiom.

## πιότης (id: 004002000000000)

πιότης
======

πιότης (G4096)

noun \| \-ητος \| πίων (fat, rich, fertile)

3\.59 · Plants · Non\-Fruit Parts of Plants

fatness, rich quality a nutritionally rich, fatty quality The text of [ROM 11:17](https://ref.ly/Acts11:17) is strange, for literally it reads ‘of the root of the richness.’ As may be easily understood, a number of scribal variations occur in different manuscripts.  
Since the reference in [ROM 11:17](https://ref.ly/Acts11:17) is to the olive tree, the fruit of which produces so much oil, one can readily understand the basis for the extension of meaning. Some translations render πιότης simply as ‘sap’ (New English Bible), while others abandon completely the figure of speech, for example, TEV which has “you share the strong spiritual life.”1  
For another treatment of πιότης in [ROM 11:17](https://ref.ly/Acts11:17), see [65\.7](#65.7).

[ROM 11:17](https://ref.ly/Acts11:17)2

65\.7 · Value · Valuable, Lacking in Value

richness, value (a figurative extension of meaning of πιότης\[a] ‘fatness,’ [3\.59](#3.59)) a rich, valuable substance For another interpretation of πιότης in [ROM 11:17](https://ref.ly/Acts11:17), see [3\.59](#3.59).

[ROM 11:17](https://ref.ly/Acts11:17)2

1 πιότης occurs in non\-biblical Greek in the figurative sense of ‘wealth and riches,’ and it may be that in [ROM 11:17](https://ref.ly/Acts11:17) there is a blend of the conventional figurative meaning of ‘wealth’ with the more specific quality of fatness as exemplified by the fruit of the olive tree. See also [65\.7](#65.7) and footnote. 2 ROM 11:17 It is difficult to determine whether the expression συγκοινωνὸς τῆς ῥίζης τῆς πιότητος τῆς ἐλαίας in [ROM 11:17](https://ref.ly/Acts11:17) should be interpreted merely as an instance of an extended figurative use of related terms, or whether πιότης should be understood in a metaphorical sense of ‘value’ or ‘wealth,’ which it had already acquired in secular literature. If πιότης is to be understood in a figurative sense, then one is likewise justified in regarding ῥίζα in the figurative sense of ‘tradition’ and ἐλαία in this context as being a direct reference to Judaism.

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 11:17


## πιπράσκω (id: 004003000000000)

πιπράσκω
========

πιπράσκω (G4097)

pf. πέπρακα · pf.pass. πέπραμαι · aor.pass. ἐπράθην · inf. πραθῆναι · ptc.neut. πραθέν

57\.186 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Sell, Buy, Price

to sell to dispose of property or provide services in exchange for money or other valuable considerations πιπράσκω and ἀποδίδομαι (ἀποδίδωμι\[e]) may also be used to refer to selling a person into slavery. Note, for example, πιπράσκω in [MAT 18:25](https://ref.ly/Matt18:25) (ἐκέλευσεν αὐτὸν ὁ κύριος πραθῆναι ‘his master ordered him to be sold as a slave’) and ἀποδίδομαι in [ACT 7:9](https://ref.ly/John7:9) (οἱ πατριάρχαι ζηλώσαντες τὸν Ἰωσὴφ ἀπέδοντο εἰς Αἴγυπτον ‘the patriarchs were jealous of Joseph and sold him to be a slave in Egypt’).  
In a number of languages, however, it may be important to use a distinctive term for selling a person into slavery. In fact, it may be necessary to describe the event rather explicitly, for example, ‘to receive money for handing a person over to someone else to be a slave.’  
In [ROM 7:14](https://ref.ly/Acts7:14) πιπράσκω is used figuratively in the phrase πεπραμένος ὑπὸ τὴν ἁμαρτίαν ‘sold as a slave to sin,’ in the sense that sin is personified as the master.

[MAT 13:46](https://ref.ly/Matt13:46) · [MAT 18:25](https://ref.ly/Matt18:25) · [MAT 26:9](https://ref.ly/Matt26:9) · [MRK 14:5](https://ref.ly/Matt14:5) · [JHN 12:5](https://ref.ly/Luke12:5) · [ACT 2:45](https://ref.ly/John2:45) · [ACT 4:34](https://ref.ly/John4:34) · [ACT 5:4](https://ref.ly/John5:4) · [ROM 7:14](https://ref.ly/Acts7:14)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 13:46; Matthew 18:25; Matthew 26:9; Mark 14:5; John 12:5; Acts 2:45; Acts 4:34; Acts 5:4; Romans 7:14


## πίπτω (id: 004004000000000)

πίπτω
=====

πίπτω (G4098)

fut. πεσοῦμαι · aor. ἔπεσα · inf. πεσεῖν · ptc. πεσών · pf. πέπτωκα · 2sg. πέπτωκες \| ἀναπίπτω · ἀντιπίπτω · ἀποπίπτω · γονυπετέω · διοπετής · ἐκπίπτω · ἐμπίπτω · ἐπιπίπτω · καταπίπτω · παραπίπτω · παράπτωμα · περιπίπτω · προπετής · προσπίπτω · πτῶμα · πτῶσις · συμπίπτω

15\.118 · Linear Movement · Fall

to fall to fall from one level to another1

[MAT 10:29](https://ref.ly/Matt10:29) · [MAT 13:4](https://ref.ly/Matt13:4) · [MAT 13:5](https://ref.ly/Matt13:5) · [MAT 13:7](https://ref.ly/Matt13:7) · [MAT 13:8](https://ref.ly/Matt13:8) · [MAT 15:14](https://ref.ly/Matt15:14) · [MAT 15:27](https://ref.ly/Matt15:27) · [MAT 17:15](https://ref.ly/Matt17:15) · [MAT 21:44](https://ref.ly/Matt21:44) · [MAT 21:44](https://ref.ly/Matt21:44) · [MAT 24:29](https://ref.ly/Matt24:29) · [MRK 4:4](https://ref.ly/Matt4:4) · [MRK 4:5](https://ref.ly/Matt4:5) · [MRK 4:7](https://ref.ly/Matt4:7) · [MRK 4:8](https://ref.ly/Matt4:8) · [MRK 13:25](https://ref.ly/Matt13:25) · [LUK 8:5](https://ref.ly/Mark8:5) · [LUK 8:7](https://ref.ly/Mark8:7) · [LUK 8:8](https://ref.ly/Mark8:8) · [LUK 8:14](https://ref.ly/Mark8:14) · [LUK 10:18](https://ref.ly/Mark10:18) · [LUK 14:5](https://ref.ly/Mark14:5) · [LUK 16:21](https://ref.ly/Mark16:21) · [LUK 20:18](https://ref.ly/Mark20:18) · [LUK 20:18](https://ref.ly/Mark20:18) · [LUK 23:30](https://ref.ly/Mark23:30) · [JHN 12:24](https://ref.ly/Luke12:24) · [ACT 20:9](https://ref.ly/John20:9) · [HEB 3:17](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:17) · [HEB 4:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm4:11) · [REV 6:13](https://ref.ly/Jude6:13) · [REV 6:16](https://ref.ly/Jude6:16) · [REV 8:10](https://ref.ly/Jude8:10) · [REV 8:10](https://ref.ly/Jude8:10) · [REV 9:1](https://ref.ly/Jude9:1)

15\.119 · Linear Movement · Fall

to fall, to fall down to fall from a standing or upright position down to the ground or surface1

[MAT 7:25](https://ref.ly/Matt7:25) · [MAT 7:27](https://ref.ly/Matt7:27) · [MRK 9:20](https://ref.ly/Matt9:20) · [LUK 11:17](https://ref.ly/Mark11:17) · [LUK 13:4](https://ref.ly/Mark13:4) · [JHN 18:6](https://ref.ly/Luke18:6) · [ACT 5:5](https://ref.ly/John5:5) · [ACT 5:10](https://ref.ly/John5:10) · [ACT 9:4](https://ref.ly/John9:4) · [ACT 15:16](https://ref.ly/John15:16) · [ACT 22:7](https://ref.ly/John22:7) · [HEB 11:30](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:30) · [REV 1:17](https://ref.ly/Jude1:17)

17\.22 · Stances and Events Related to Stances · Prostrate as an Act of Reverence or Supplication

to prostrate oneself before, to fall down before to prostrate oneself before someone, implying supplication2 A strictly literal translation of προσπίπτω\[a] or πίπτω\[c], namely, to fall down before, can be entirely misleading in that it may suggest an accident caused by stumbling or tripping. It may therefore be necessary in a number of languages to translate to bow down low before or to bow down to the ground before.

[MAT 2:11](https://ref.ly/Matt2:11) · [MAT 4:9](https://ref.ly/Matt4:9) · [MAT 17:6](https://ref.ly/Matt17:6) · [MAT 18:26](https://ref.ly/Matt18:26) · [MAT 18:29](https://ref.ly/Matt18:29) · [MAT 26:39](https://ref.ly/Matt26:39) · [MRK 5:22](https://ref.ly/Matt5:22) · [MRK 14:35](https://ref.ly/Matt14:35) · [LUK 5:12](https://ref.ly/Mark5:12) · [LUK 8:41](https://ref.ly/Mark8:41) · [LUK 17:16](https://ref.ly/Mark17:16) · [JHN 11:32](https://ref.ly/Luke11:32) · [ACT 10:25](https://ref.ly/John10:25) · [1CO 14:25](https://ref.ly/Rom14:25) · [REV 4:10](https://ref.ly/Jude4:10) · [REV 5:8](https://ref.ly/Jude5:8) · [REV 5:14](https://ref.ly/Jude5:14) · [REV 7:11](https://ref.ly/Jude7:11) · [REV 11:16](https://ref.ly/Jude11:16) · [REV 19:4](https://ref.ly/Jude19:4) · [REV 19:10](https://ref.ly/Jude19:10) · [REV 22:8](https://ref.ly/Jude22:8)

20\.60 · Violence, Harm, Destroy, Kill · Destroy

to experience destruction, to be destroyed to suffer or experience destruction

[HEB 4:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm4:11) · [REV 11:13](https://ref.ly/Jude11:13) · [REV 14:8](https://ref.ly/Jude14:8) · [REV 14:8](https://ref.ly/Jude14:8) · [REV 16:19](https://ref.ly/Jude16:19) · [REV 18:2](https://ref.ly/Jude18:2) · [REV 18:2](https://ref.ly/Jude18:2)

23\.105 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

to die (a figurative extension of meaning of πίπτω\[a] ‘to fall,’ [15\.118](#15.118)) to fall down, as a euphemistic expression for a violent death It is rare that one can employ a literal meaning of πίπτω as a euphemistic expression for ‘dying,’ since receptors are too likely to understand ‘to fall’ in a purely literal sense.

[LUK 21:24](https://ref.ly/Mark21:24) · [1CO 10:8](https://ref.ly/Rom10:8)

68\.49 · Aspect · Cease, Stop

to stop, to cease, to fail (a figurative extension of meaning of πίπτω\[a] ‘to fall,’ [15\.118](#15.118)) to cease, with the possible implication of failure For another interpretation of πίπτω in [1CO 13:8](https://ref.ly/Rom13:8), see [75\.7](#75.7).

[LUK 16:17](https://ref.ly/Mark16:17) · [1CO 13:8](https://ref.ly/Rom13:8)

13\.122 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Happen

to happen to, to fall upon to happen suddenly to, with the connotation of something bad and adverse In this particular context it may be necessary to be somewhat more specific concerning the blindness that Elymas experienced. For example, one may render this expression as ‘suddenly a dark mist covered his eyes’ or ‘suddenly the eyes of Elymas became dark and misty’ (see [24\.40](#24.40)).

[ACT 13:11](https://ref.ly/John13:11)

13\.59 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Change of State

to fall from, to worsen to change for the worse, with emphasis upon extent and suddenness

[ROM 11:11](https://ref.ly/Acts11:11) · [ROM 11:22](https://ref.ly/Acts11:22) · [1CO 10:12](https://ref.ly/Rom10:12) · [REV 2:5](https://ref.ly/Jude2:5)

13\.97 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Exist

to cease, to come to an end, to fall to cease to exist in a particular post or position

[REV 17:10](https://ref.ly/Jude17:10)

75\.7 · Adequate, Qualified

to become inadequate, to fail (figurative extension of meaning of πίπτω\[a] ‘to fall,’ [15\.118](#15.118)) to become inadequate for some function For another interpretation of πίπτω in [1CO 13:8](https://ref.ly/Rom13:8), see [68\.49](#68.49).

[1CO 13:8](https://ref.ly/Rom13:8)

90\.71 · Case · Experiencer

to come to experience, to experience, to encounter, to be beset by to experience somewhat suddenly that which is difficult or bad

[JAS 5:12](https://ref.ly/Heb5:12)

30\.107 · Think · To Choose, To Select, To Prefer

**Collocation:** ὁ κλῆρος πίπτει ἐπί τινα

to choose by lot, to select by lot (an idiom, literally: the lot falls upon someone) the process of choosing, with the probable implication of discerning God’s will in this manner In some societies the closest natural equivalents of deciding by lot are procedures involving ‘drawing straws’ or ‘throwing dice’ or ‘throwing down sticks’ or ‘dropping pebbles.’

[ACT 1:26](https://ref.ly/John1:26)

24\.93 · Sensory Events and States · Pain, Suffering

**Collocation:** πίπτω ἐπί τινα

to cause to suffer, to cause pain to (an idiom, literally: to fall upon someone) to cause someone suffering or harm It is rare indeed that this idiom can ever be produced literally in other languages, though it may be possible to preserve some of the figurative impact by translating ‘nor will the heat of the sun ever cause them to suffer.’

[REV 7:16](https://ref.ly/Jude7:16)

87\.56 · Status · High Status or Rank \[including persons of high status]

**Collocation:** τῷ ἰδίῳ κυρίῳ στήκει ἢ πίπτει

honor or disgrace depends on the person whom one serves (an idiom, literally: to his own master he stands or falls, probably a familiar adage) whether one maintains one’s status or relationship to a master depends on the masters judgment or evaluation

[ROM 14:4](https://ref.ly/Acts14:4)

56\.32 · Courts and Legal Procedures · Judge, Condemn, Acquit

**Collocation:** ὑπὸ κρίσιν πίπτω

to be condemned, to suffer condemnation, to be judged guilty (an idiom, literally: to fall under judgment) to be condemned for acting contrary to laws and regulations In the context of [JAS 5:12](https://ref.ly/Heb5:12) the condemnation refers to God’s judgment.

[JAS 5:12](https://ref.ly/Heb5:12)

1 In both instances ([15\.118](#15.118) and [15\.119](#15.119)) καταπίπτω may be somewhat more emphatic in meaning than πίπτω in view of the prefixial form κατα. 2 πίπτω\[c] and προσπίπτω\[a] ([17\.22](#17.22)) differ to some extent from προσκυνέω\[b] ([17\.21](#17.21)) in that they focus primarily upon the actual position rather than upon the intent of showing reverence or making supplication. προσκυνέω\[b] is the more formal term and focuses more upon the attitude of reverence or honor.

## πιστεύω (id: 004007000000000)

πιστεύω
=======

πιστεύω (G4100)

ἀπιστέω · ἀπιστία · ἄπιστος · ὀλιγοπιστία · ὀλιγόπιστος · πιστικός · πίστις · πιστόομαι · πιστός

31\.35 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Believe To Be True

to believe, to think to be true, to regard as trustworthy to believe something to be true and, hence, worthy of being trusted1 In a number of languages the equivalent of πιστεύω\[a] is simply to think to be true or to regard as true, but in some languages an idiomatic expression is employed, for example, to think in the heart or to think in the liver or to hold in the heart.

31\.85 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Trust, Rely

to believe in, to have confidence in, to have faith in, to trust, faith, trust to believe to the extent of complete trust and reliance In rendering πιστεύω\[b] and πίστις\[b] it would be wrong to select a term which would mean merely ‘reliance’ or ‘dependency’ or even ‘confidence,’ for there should also be a significant measure of ‘belief,’ since real trust, confidence, and reliance can only be placed in someone who is believed to have the qualities attributed to such a person.

3:8](https://ref.ly/2Tim3:8) · [HEB 4:3](https://ref.ly/Phlm4:3) · [1PE 2:7](https://ref.ly/Jas2:7) · [1JN 5:10](https://ref.ly/2Pet5:10)

35\.50 · Help, Care For · Entrust To the Care Of

to entrust to, to put into the care of to entrust something to the care of someone In some languages it may be possible to render this expression in [ROM 3:2](https://ref.ly/Acts3:2) as ‘because God gave his message to them to take care of’ or ‘... to keep,’ but one should avoid the implication that the message was simply to be kept by the Jews for themselves.

[LUK 16:11](https://ref.ly/Mark16:11) · [JHN 2:24](https://ref.ly/Luke2:24) · [ROM 3:2](https://ref.ly/Acts3:2) · [1CO 9:17](https://ref.ly/Rom9:17) · [GAL 2:7](https://ref.ly/2Cor2:7) · [1TH 2:4](https://ref.ly/Col2:4) · [1TI 1:11](https://ref.ly/2Thess1:11) · [TIT 1:3](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:3)

1 πιστεύω\[a] differs from φρονέω\[c], κρίνω\[d], λογίζομαι\[c], ἡγέομαι\[a], and ἔχω\[c] to hold a view ([31\.1](#31.1)) because of the added component of trustworthiness. There is also a greater certainty in πιστεύω\[a] than in οἶμαι, νομίζω, δοκὲω\[a] and ὑπολαμβάνω\[d] to suppose ([31\.29](#31.29)), and though for certain contexts there may be a somewhat less degree of certainty than in the case of γινώσκω\[a] and οἶδα\[a] to know ([28\.1](#28.1)), there is the component of confidence and trustworthiness which is a focal element in this subdomain. πιστεύω\[a] in the sense of believing information differs from πιστεύω\[b] and πίστις\[b] to trust or rely on ([31\.85](#31.85)). There is also a contrast with πιστεύω\[c] and πίστις\[d] ([31\.102](#31.102)), which are essentially equivalent in meaning to becoming a Christian, since the belief and trust is specifically in salvation through Jesus Christ with the resulting new relationship to God.

## πιστικός (id: 004008000000000)

πιστικός
========

πιστικός (G4101)

adj \| \-ή, \-όν \| πιστεύω

79\.97 · Features of Objects · Pure, Unadulterated, Undiluted

pure pertaining to being pure, with the possible implication of a quality which can be trusted1

[MRK 14:3](https://ref.ly/Matt14:3) · [JHN 12:3](https://ref.ly/Luke12:3)

1 Scholarly opinion is divided as to the derivation of πιστικός, but πιστικός was associated with πίστις by some later writers, and it was then interpreted to mean genuine, unadulterated.

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 14:3; John 12:3


## πίστις (id: 004009000000000)

πίστις
======

πίστις (G4102)

noun \| \-εως \| πιστεύω

31\.43 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Believe To Be True

what can be fully believed, that which is worthy of belief, believable evidence, proof that which is completely believable

[ACT 17:31](https://ref.ly/John17:31)

31\.85 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Trust, Rely

to believe in, to have confidence in, to have faith in, to trust, faith, trust to believe to the extent of complete trust and reliance In rendering πιστεύω\[b] and πίστις\[b] it would be wrong to select a term which would mean merely ‘reliance’ or ‘dependency’ or even ‘confidence,’ for there should also be a significant measure of ‘belief,’ since real trust, confidence, and reliance can only be placed in someone who is believed to have the qualities attributed to such a person.

31\.104 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Be a Believer, Christian Faith

the faith, beliefs, doctrine (semantic derivative of πίστις\[d] ‘to be a believer, Christian faith,’ [31\.102](#31.102)) the content of what Christians believe It is also possible to interpret πίστις in [JUD 1:3](https://ref.ly/3John1:3) and [GAL 1:23](https://ref.ly/2Cor1:23) as the act of believing and placing confidence in Jesus Christ (see [31\.102](#31.102)).

## πιστός (id: 004010000000000)

πιστός
======

πιστός (G4103) — πιστή

adj \| \-ή, \-όν \| πιστεύω

31\.86 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Trust, Rely

one who trusts in, trusting (derivative of πιστεύω\[b] ‘to trust,’ [31\.85](#31.85)) pertaining to trusting

[JHN 20:27](https://ref.ly/Luke20:27) · [ACT 10:45](https://ref.ly/John10:45) · [2CO 6:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:15) · [GAL 3:9](https://ref.ly/2Cor3:9) · [EPH 1:1](https://ref.ly/Gal1:1) · [1TI 4:3](https://ref.ly/2Thess4:3) · [1TI 4:10](https://ref.ly/2Thess4:10) · [1TI 4:12](https://ref.ly/2Thess4:12) · [1TI 6:2](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:2) · [1TI 6:2](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:2) · [TIT 1:6](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:6) · [1PE 1:21](https://ref.ly/Jas1:21)

31\.87 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Trust, Rely

faithful, trustworthy, dependable, reliable (derivative of πιστεύω\[b] ‘to trust,’ [31\.85](#31.85)) pertaining to being trusted Since πιστός\[b] is a type of passive derivative of πιστεύω\[b], it may be necessary in a number of languages to render this passive relationship in an active form. For example, the phrase δοῦλε ἀγαθὲ καὶ πιστέ in [MAT 25:21](https://ref.ly/Matt25:21) may be rendered as ‘good servant and one whom I can trust.’ In [2TI 2:11](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:11) the phrase πιστὸς ὁ λόγος may be rendered as ‘this is a saying we can have confidence in’ or ‘... that we can trust.’

[MAT 24:45](https://ref.ly/Matt24:45) · [MAT 25:21](https://ref.ly/Matt25:21) · [MAT 25:21](https://ref.ly/Matt25:21) · [MAT 25:23](https://ref.ly/Matt25:23) · [MAT 25:23](https://ref.ly/Matt25:23) · [LUK 12:42](https://ref.ly/Mark12:42) · [LUK 16:10](https://ref.ly/Mark16:10) · [LUK 16:10](https://ref.ly/Mark16:10) · [LUK 16:11](https://ref.ly/Mark16:11) · [LUK 16:12](https://ref.ly/Mark16:12) · [LUK 19:17](https://ref.ly/Mark19:17) · [ACT 16:15](https://ref.ly/John16:15) · [1CO 1:9](https://ref.ly/Rom1:9) · [1CO 4:2](https://ref.ly/Rom4:2) · [1CO 4:17](https://ref.ly/Rom4:17) · [1CO 7:25](https://ref.ly/Rom7:25) · [1CO 10:13](https://ref.ly/Rom10:13) · [2CO 1:18](https://ref.ly/1Cor1:18) · [EPH 1:1](https://ref.ly/Gal1:1) · [EPH 6:21](https://ref.ly/Gal6:21) · [COL 1:2](https://ref.ly/Phil1:2) · [COL 1:7](https://ref.ly/Phil1:7) · [COL 4:7](https://ref.ly/Phil4:7) · [COL 4:9](https://ref.ly/Phil4:9) · [1TH 5:24](https://ref.ly/Col5:24) · [2TH 3:3](https://ref.ly/1Thess3:3) · [1TI 1:12](https://ref.ly/2Thess1:12) · [1TI 3:11](https://ref.ly/2Thess3:11) · [2TI 2:2](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:2) · [2TI 2:11](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:11) · [2TI 2:13](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:13) · [TIT 1:6](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:6) · [TIT 1:9](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:9) · [TIT 3:8](https://ref.ly/2Tim3:8) · [HEB 2:17](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:17) · [HEB 3:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:2) · [HEB 3:5](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:5) · [HEB 10:23](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:23) · [HEB 11:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:11) · [1PE 4:19](https://ref.ly/Jas4:19) · [1PE 5:12](https://ref.ly/Jas5:12) · [1JN 1:9](https://ref.ly/2Pet1:9) · [3JN 1:5](https://ref.ly/2John1:5) · [REV 1:5](https://ref.ly/Jude1:5) · [REV 2:10](https://ref.ly/Jude2:10) · [REV 2:13](https://ref.ly/Jude2:13) · [REV 3:14](https://ref.ly/Jude3:14) · [REV 17:14](https://ref.ly/Jude17:14) · [REV 19:11](https://ref.ly/Jude19:11) · [REV 21:5](https://ref.ly/Jude21:5) · [REV 22:6](https://ref.ly/Jude22:6)

71\.17 · Mode · Certain, Uncertain

sure pertaining to being sure, with the implication of being fully trustworthy

[ACT 13:34](https://ref.ly/John13:34) · [1TI 1:15](https://ref.ly/2Thess1:15) · [1TI 3:1](https://ref.ly/2Thess3:1) · [1TI 4:9](https://ref.ly/2Thess4:9) · [TIT 1:9](https://ref.ly/2Tim1:9)

31\.103 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Be a Believer, Christian Faith

**Collocation:** πιστή

believer, Christian, one who has Christian faith (derivative of πιστεύω\[c] ‘to be a believer,’ [31\.102](#31.102)) one (female) who believes in Jesus Christ1

[ACT 16:1](https://ref.ly/John16:1)2 · [1TI 5:16](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:16)

11\.17 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

**Collocation:** πιστή

believer, Christian, follower one (female) who is included among the faithful followers of Christ

[ACT 16:1](https://ref.ly/John16:1)2 · [1TI 5:16](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:16)

1 πιστή has also been treated in [11\.17](#11.17) as a name for a member of the Christian constituency. This is a good instance of complete overlapping in that the same term may be classified in two quite distinct ways, one based on group membership and the other based upon the type of belief which results in such membership. It would be possible, therefore, to indicate that in the case of πιστή\[b] (\= πιστός\[e]) in [11\.17](#11.17) there is an added component of group identification, but one may also argue that such a feature of meaning is derived essentially from the context. 2 ACT 16:1 πιστή may also be regarded as a derivative of πιστεύω\[c] to be a believer ([31\.102](#31.102)), but NT usage in a passage such as [ACT 16:1](https://ref.ly/John16:1) seems to focus more upon group adherence than upon the actual state of believing. See also [31\.103](#31.103) and footnote.

* **Associated Passages:** John 20:27; Acts 10:45; 2 Corinthians 6:15; Galatians 3:9; Ephesians 1:1; 1 Timothy 4:3; 1 Timothy 4:10; 1 Timothy 4:12; 1 Timothy 6:2; Titus 1:6; 1 Peter 1:21; Matthew 24:45; Matthew 25:21; Matthew 25:23; Luke 12:42; Luke 16:10; Luke 16:11; Luke 16:12; Luke 19:17; Acts 16:15; 1 Corinthians 1:9; 1 Corinthians 4:2; 1 Corinthians 4:17; 1 Corinthians 7:25; 1 Corinthians 10:13; 2 Corinthians 1:18; Ephesians 6:21; Colossians 1:2; Colossians 1:7; Colossians 4:7; Colossians 4:9; 1 Thessalonians 5:24; 2 Thessalonians 3:3; 1 Timothy 1:12; 1 Timothy 3:11; 2 Timothy 2:2; 2 Timothy 2:11; 2 Timothy 2:13; Titus 1:9; Titus 3:8; Hebrews 2:17; Hebrews 3:2; Hebrews 3:5; Hebrews 10:23; Hebrews 11:11; 1 Peter 4:19; 1 Peter 5:12; 1 John 1:9; 3 John 1:5; Revelation 1:5; Revelation 2:10; Revelation 2:13; Revelation 3:14; Revelation 17:14; Revelation 19:11; Revelation 21:5; Revelation 22:6; Acts 13:34; 1 Timothy 1:15; 1 Timothy 3:1; 1 Timothy 4:9; Acts 16:1; 1 Timothy 5:16


## πιστόω (id: 004011000000000)

πιστόω
======

πιστόω (G4104) — πιστόομαι

πιστεύω

31\.36 · (πιστόομαι) · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Believe To Be True

to come to believe to come to believe something to be true

[2TI 3:14](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:14)

## πλανάω (id: 004012000000000)

πλανάω
======

πλανάω (G4105) — πλανάομαι

πλάνη · πλανήτης · πλάνος

31\.8 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Hold a Wrong View, Be Mistaken

to mislead, to deceive, deception, to cause to be mistaken (figurative extension of meaning of πλανάω ‘to cause to wander off the path,’ not occurring in the NT) to cause someone to hold a wrong view and thus be mistaken1 To mislead people as to proper views which they should have may often be expressed idiomatically, for example, ‘to twist people’s thoughts,’ ‘to cause what is false to seem like what is true,’ ‘to make a lie appear true,’ ‘to dig away the truth,’ or ‘to cover the eyes with lies.’

[MAT 24:4](https://ref.ly/Matt24:4) · [MAT 24:5](https://ref.ly/Matt24:5) · [MAT 24:11](https://ref.ly/Matt24:11) · [MAT 24:24](https://ref.ly/Matt24:24) · [MRK 13:5](https://ref.ly/Matt13:5) · [MRK 13:6](https://ref.ly/Matt13:6) · [JHN 7:12](https://ref.ly/Luke7:12) · [2TI 3:13](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:13) · [1JN 1:8](https://ref.ly/2Pet1:8) · [1JN 2:26](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:26) · [1JN 3:7](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:7) · [REV 2:20](https://ref.ly/Jude2:20) · [REV 12:9](https://ref.ly/Jude12:9) · [REV 13:14](https://ref.ly/Jude13:14) · [REV 19:20](https://ref.ly/Jude19:20) · [REV 20:3](https://ref.ly/Jude20:3) · [REV 20:8](https://ref.ly/Jude20:8) · [REV 20:10](https://ref.ly/Jude20:10)

15\.24 · (πλανάομαι\[a]) · Linear Movement · Travel, Journey

to wander about to move about, without definite destination or particular purpose

[MAT 18:12](https://ref.ly/Matt18:12) · [MAT 18:12](https://ref.ly/Matt18:12) · [MAT 18:13](https://ref.ly/Matt18:13) · [HEB 11:38](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:38) · [1PE 2:25](https://ref.ly/Jas2:25)

31\.67 · (πλανάομαι\[b]) · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Change an Opinion Concerning Truth

to stray from the truth, to wander from the truth, to go astray from to no longer believe what is true, but to start believing what is false2 Though in some languages one can preserve the figurative meaning of ‘to wander’ or ‘to go astray’ in connection with truth, it is not possible to do so in many languages, and therefore it may be necessary to spell out in some detail precisely what is involved, for example, ‘to stop believing what is true and to start believing what is false.’

[MAT 22:29](https://ref.ly/Matt22:29) · [MRK 12:24](https://ref.ly/Matt12:24) · [MRK 12:27](https://ref.ly/Matt12:27) · [LUK 21:8](https://ref.ly/Mark21:8) · [JHN 7:47](https://ref.ly/Luke7:47) · [1CO 6:9](https://ref.ly/Rom6:9) · [1CO 15:33](https://ref.ly/Rom15:33) · [GAL 6:7](https://ref.ly/2Cor6:7) · [2TI 3:13](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:13) · [TIT 3:3](https://ref.ly/2Tim3:3) · [HEB 3:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:10) · [HEB 5:2](https://ref.ly/Phlm5:2) · [JAS 1:16](https://ref.ly/Heb1:16) · [JAS 5:19](https://ref.ly/Heb5:19) · [2PE 2:15](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:15) · [REV 18:23](https://ref.ly/Jude18:23)

1 The meanings of πλανάω and πλάνη\[a] ([31\.8](#31.8)), πλάνος ([31\.9](#31.9)), πλάνη\[b] ([31\.10](#31.10)), and ἀποπλανάω ([31\.11](#31.11)) focus primarily upon the misleading or deceptive views which people are caused to have, but these same terms may refer to more than mere mistaken ideas or opinions. They may involve deception of a behavioral nature, and therefore these same meanings could be in Domain 88 Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior, Subdomain U Mislead, Lead Astray, Deceive. 2 It is possible that ἀποπλανάομαι differs slightly from πλανάομαι\[b] in emphasizing the departure from truth.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 24:4; Matthew 24:5; Matthew 24:11; Matthew 24:24; Mark 13:5; Mark 13:6; John 7:12; 2 Timothy 3:13; 1 John 1:8; 1 John 2:26; 1 John 3:7; Revelation 2:20; Revelation 12:9; Revelation 13:14; Revelation 19:20; Revelation 20:3; Revelation 20:8; Revelation 20:10; Matthew 18:12; Matthew 18:13; Hebrews 11:38; 1 Peter 2:25; Matthew 22:29; Mark 12:24; Mark 12:27; Luke 21:8; John 7:47; 1 Corinthians 6:9; 1 Corinthians 15:33; Galatians 6:7; Titus 3:3; Hebrews 3:10; Hebrews 5:2; James 1:16; James 5:19; 2 Peter 2:15; Revelation 18:23


## πλάνη (id: 004013000000000)

πλάνη
=====

πλάνη (G4106)

noun \| \-ης \| πλανάω

31\.8 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Hold a Wrong View, Be Mistaken

deception (figurative extension of meaning of πλανάω ‘to cause to wander off the path,’ not occurring in the NT) act of causing someone to hold a wrong view and thus be mistaken1 To mislead people as to proper views which they should have may often be expressed idiomatically, for example, ‘to twist people’s thoughts,’ ‘to cause what is false to seem like what is true,’ ‘to make a lie appear true,’ ‘to dig away the truth,’ or ‘to cover the eyes with lies.’

[EPH 4:14](https://ref.ly/Gal4:14) · [1TH 2:3](https://ref.ly/Col2:3) · [2TH 2:11](https://ref.ly/1Thess2:11) · [JAS 5:20](https://ref.ly/Heb5:20) · [1JN 4:6](https://ref.ly/2Pet4:6)

31\.10 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Hold a Wrong View, Be Mistaken

misleading belief, deceptive belief, error, mistaken view (semantic derivative of πλάνη\[a] ‘deception,’ [31\.8](#31.8)) the content of that which misleads or deceives1

[MAT 27:64](https://ref.ly/Matt27:64) · [1TH 2:3](https://ref.ly/Col2:3) · [JAS 5:20](https://ref.ly/Heb5:20) · [2PE 2:18](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:18) · [2PE 3:17](https://ref.ly/1Pet3:17) · [JUD 1:11](https://ref.ly/3John1:11)

88\.262 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Licentiousness, Perversion

perversion behavior which deviates seriously from that which is morally correct In a number of languages πλάνη in [ROM 1:27](https://ref.ly/Acts1:27) may be rendered as ‘what people do which is completely wrong’ or ‘... what is very wrong indeed.’

[ROM 1:27](https://ref.ly/Acts1:27)

1 The meanings of πλανάω and πλάνη\[a] ([31\.8](#31.8)), πλάνος ([31\.9](#31.9)), πλάνη\[b] ([31\.10](#31.10)), and ἀποπλανάω ([31\.11](#31.11)) focus primarily upon the misleading or deceptive views which people are caused to have, but these same terms may refer to more than mere mistaken ideas or opinions. They may involve deception of a behavioral nature, and therefore these same meanings could be in Domain 88 Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior, Subdomain U Mislead, Lead Astray, Deceive.

* **Associated Passages:** Ephesians 4:14; 1 Thessalonians 2:3; 2 Thessalonians 2:11; James 5:20; 1 John 4:6; Matthew 27:64; 2 Peter 2:18; 2 Peter 3:17; Jude 1:11; Romans 1:27


## πλανήτης (id: 004014000000000)

πλανήτης
========

πλανήτης (G4107)

adj \| \-ου \| πλανάω

15\.26 · Linear Movement · Travel, Journey

wanderer (derivative of πλανάω\[b] ‘to wander about,’ [15\.24](#15.24)) one who or that which wanders about or around In [JUD 1:13](https://ref.ly/3John1:13) there appears to be a double\-entendre in that πλανήτης may also suggest the meaning of πλανάω ‘to deceive, to cause to be mistaken’ (see [31\.8](#31.8)). Therefore, such persons may be described not only as ‘wanderers,’ but as ‘deceivers.’ For a further discussion of πλανήτης in [JUD 1:13](https://ref.ly/3John1:13), see [1\.30](#1.30).

[JUD 1:13](https://ref.ly/3John1:13)

* **Associated Passages:** Jude 1:13

πλάνος
======

πλάνος (G4108)

adj \| \-ον \| πλανάω

31\.9 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Hold a Wrong View, Be Mistaken

causing someone to be mistaken, deceitful, that which deceives pertaining to causing someone to be mistaken1

[MAT 27:63](https://ref.ly/Matt27:63) · [2CO 6:8](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:8) · [1TI 4:1](https://ref.ly/2Thess4:1) · [2JN 1:7](https://ref.ly/1John1:7) · [2JN 1:7](https://ref.ly/1John1:7)

1 The meanings of πλανάω and πλάνη\[a] ([31\.8](#31.8)), πλάνος ([31\.9](#31.9)), πλάνη\[b] ([31\.10](#31.10)), and ἀποπλανάω ([31\.11](#31.11)) focus primarily upon the misleading or deceptive views which people are caused to have, but these same terms may refer to more than mere mistaken ideas or opinions. They may involve deception of a behavioral nature, and therefore these same meanings could be in Domain 88 Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior, Subdomain U Mislead, Lead Astray, Deceive.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 27:63; 2 Corinthians 6:8; 1 Timothy 4:1; 2 John 1:7

πλάξ

## πλάσσω (id: 004018000000000)

πλάσσω

πλάσσω (G4111)

aor.ptc. πλάσας · aor.pass. ἐπλάσθην \| πλάσμα · πλαστός

to form, to mold to give a particular form to something

## πλεονάζω (id: 004025000000000)

πλεονάζω
========

πλεονάζω (G4121)

aor.opt.3sg. πλεονάσαι \| πλεονεκτέω · πλεονέκτης · πλεονεξία · πλείων · ὑπερπλεονάζω

59\.67 · Quantity · Increase, Decrease

to increase considerably, to become more and more, to multiply to increase considerably the extent of an activity or state, with the implication of the result being an abundance It may be difficult in some languages to speak of ‘sin increasing,’ but in [ROM 5:20](https://ref.ly/Acts5:20) one can say ‘where people sinned more and more.’  
In [2CO 4:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:15) the increase would be in the instances of God’s grace extending to more and more people.

[ROM 5:20](https://ref.ly/Acts5:20) · [ROM 5:20](https://ref.ly/Acts5:20) · [ROM 6:1](https://ref.ly/Acts6:1) · [2CO 4:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:15) · [PHP 4:17](https://ref.ly/Eph4:17)

78\.32 · Degree · More Than, Less Than \[Comparative Degree]

to cause to be intense, to cause to be more, to cause to grow to cause an increase in the degree of some experience or state1 It would seem that πλεονάσαι and περισσεύσαι in [1TH 3:12](https://ref.ly/Col3:12) simply reinforce the meaning of intense degree. It is assumed that these two expressions are not completely synonymous, but it is difficult to determine precisely how they may contrast. It may simply be that περισσεύσαι is somewhat more emphatic.

[1TH 3:12](https://ref.ly/Col3:12) · [2TH 1:3](https://ref.ly/1Thess1:3)

59\.48 · Quantity · Abundance, Excess, Sparing

to be in abundance, to have in abundance to exist or be in abundance The two participial forms, ὑπάρχοντα (see [57\.2](#57.2)) and πλεονάζοντα, are coalesced in the translation as ‘have ... in abundance.’  
In a number of languages the equivalent of this expression in [2PE 1:8](https://ref.ly/1Pet1:8) might be ‘if you have a great many of these’ or ‘for if these are big with you.’

[2PE 1:8](https://ref.ly/1Pet1:8)

57\.23 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Have Sufficient

to have more than enough, to have too much to have more than enough to meet one’s needs

[2CO 8:15](https://ref.ly/1Cor8:15)

1 It is possible that περισσεύω\[e] differs somewhat from πλεονάζω\[b] in intensity, implying perhaps an overabundance of something, but it is difficult to confirm such a distinction on the basis of existing texts.

* **Associated Passages:** Romans 5:20; Romans 6:1; 2 Corinthians 4:15; Philippians 4:17; 1 Thessalonians 3:12; 2 Thessalonians 1:3; 2 Peter 1:8; 2 Corinthians 8:15

## πλεονεξία (id: 004028000000000)

πλεονεξία
=========

πλεονεξία (G4124)

noun \| \-ας \| πλεονάζω \+ ἔχω

25\.22 · Attitudes and Emotions · Desire Strongly

greed, avarice, covetousness a strong desire to acquire more and more material possessions or to possess more things than other people have, all irrespective of need

[LUK 12:15](https://ref.ly/Mark12:15) · [ROM 1:29](https://ref.ly/Acts1:29) · [EPH 4:19](https://ref.ly/Gal4:19) · [EPH 5:3](https://ref.ly/Gal5:3) · [COL 3:5](https://ref.ly/Phil3:5) · [1TH 2:5](https://ref.ly/Col2:5) · [2PE 2:3](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:3) · [2PE 2:14](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:14)

88\.144 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Exploit

to take advantage of, to exploit, exploitation to take advantage of someone, usually as the result of a motivation of greed It is also possible to understand πλεονεξία in [2CO 9:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:5) as referring to ‘compulsion.’

[MRK 7:22](https://ref.ly/Matt7:22) · [2CO 9:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:5)1 · [1TH 2:5](https://ref.ly/Col2:5)

1 2CO 9:5 Though in [2CO 9:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:5) one may translate μὴ ὡς πλεονεξίαν as ‘not because you are being taken advantage of,’ the meaning of πλεονεξία is essentially active and may also be rendered as ‘not because someone is exploiting you.’

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 12:15; Romans 1:29; Ephesians 4:19; Ephesians 5:3; Colossians 3:5; 1 Thessalonians 2:5; 2 Peter 2:3; 2 Peter 2:14; Mark 7:22; 2 Corinthians 9:5


## πλευρά (id: 004029000000000)

πλευρά
======

πλευρά (G4125)

noun \| \-ᾶς

8\.41 · Body, Body Parts, and Body Products · Parts of the Body

side of the body either side of the trunk of the body In a number of languages it is necessary to be quite specific with regard to the side of the body, since terms differ depending on whether the area involves (1\) the ribs, (2\) the area between the ribs and the pelvic bone, or (3\) the area of the hip, from the fleshy mid\-part of the trunk to the leg.

[JHN 19:34](https://ref.ly/Luke19:34) · [JHN 20:20](https://ref.ly/Luke20:20) · [JHN 20:25](https://ref.ly/Luke20:25) · [JHN 20:27](https://ref.ly/Luke20:27) · [ACT 12:7](https://ref.ly/John12:7)

ἀποπλέω · βραδυπλοέω · διαπλέω · ἐκπλέω · καταπλέω · παραπλέω · πλοιάριον · πλοῖον · πλοῦς · ὑποπλέω

54\.1 · Maritime Activities

to sail, sailing the movement of a boat or ship through the water, either rowed or blown by the wind

[LUK 8:23](https://ref.ly/Mark8:23) · [ACT 21:3](https://ref.ly/John21:3) · [ACT 27:2](https://ref.ly/John27:2) · [ACT 27:6](https://ref.ly/John27:6) · [ACT 27:24](https://ref.ly/John27:24)

**Collocation:** ὁ ἐπὶ τόπον πλέων

sea traveller, sea merchant one who frequently travels by sea1

[REV 18:17](https://ref.ly/Jude18:17)

1 There are considerable differences of opinion as to the meaning of the expression ὁ ἐπὶ τόπον πλέων. It may denote merely passengers, but it probably has a more specialized meaning. Some prefer to interpret it as meaning one who sails along the coast.

## πληγή (id: 004031000000000)

πληγή
=====

πληγή (G4127)

noun \| \-ῆς \| πλήσσω

19\.1 · Physical Impact · Hit, Strike

to hit, to strike, to beat to strike or hit an object, one or more times One may also render this expression in [LUK 12:48](https://ref.ly/Mark12:48) as ‘does something for which he should be beaten.’

[LUK 10:30](https://ref.ly/Mark10:30) · [LUK 12:48](https://ref.ly/Mark12:48) · [ACT 16:23](https://ref.ly/John16:23) · [2CO 6:5](https://ref.ly/1Cor6:5) · [2CO 11:23](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:23)

20\.29 · Violence, Harm, Destroy, Kill · Harm, Wound

wound the condition resulting from being severely hurt or wounded1

[ACT 16:33](https://ref.ly/John16:33) · [REV 13:3](https://ref.ly/Jude13:3) · [REV 13:12](https://ref.ly/Jude13:12) · [REV 13:14](https://ref.ly/Jude13:14)

23\.158 · Physiological Processes and States · Sickness, Disease, Weakness

plague, pestilence a widespread contagious disease, often associated with divine retribution It is also possible to interpret πληγή in [REV 11:6](https://ref.ly/Jude11:6) as being somewhat more generic in meaning, that is to say, trouble or distress causing widespread suffering (see [22\.13](#22.13)).

[REV 9:18](https://ref.ly/Jude9:18) · [REV 9:20](https://ref.ly/Jude9:20) · [REV 11:6](https://ref.ly/Jude11:6)

22\.13 · Trouble, Hardship, Relief, Favorable Circumstances · Trouble, Hardship, Distress

plague, great suffering, distress (a figurative extension of meaning of πληγή\[c] ‘plague,’ [23\.158](#23.158)) a type of trouble or distress causing widespread and/or intense suffering One may also render [REV 18:4](https://ref.ly/Jude18:4) as ‘so that you will not suffer as she is going to suffer.’

[REV 9:18](https://ref.ly/Jude9:18) · [REV 9:20](https://ref.ly/Jude9:20) · [REV 11:6](https://ref.ly/Jude11:6) · [REV 15:1](https://ref.ly/Jude15:1) · [REV 15:6](https://ref.ly/Jude15:6) · [REV 15:8](https://ref.ly/Jude15:8) · [REV 16:9](https://ref.ly/Jude16:9) · [REV 16:21](https://ref.ly/Jude16:21) · [REV 16:21](https://ref.ly/Jude16:21) · [REV 18:4](https://ref.ly/Jude18:4) · [REV 18:8](https://ref.ly/Jude18:8) · [REV 21:9](https://ref.ly/Jude21:9) · [REV 22:18](https://ref.ly/Jude22:18)

1 These expressions for wound normally do not imply a mortal wound. It is possible, however, to speak of a mortal wound by the phrase ἡ πληγὴ τοῦ θανάτου as in [REV 13:3](https://ref.ly/Jude13:3), [REV 13:12](https://ref.ly/Jude13:12).

## πλῆθος (id: 004032000000000)

πλῆθος
======

πλῆθος (G4128)

noun \| \-ους \| πίμπλημι

59\.9 · Quantity · Many, Few \[Countables]

large number of, a multitude of a large number of countable objects or events, with the probable implication of some type of grouping

[LUK 5:6](https://ref.ly/Mark5:6) · [JHN 5:3](https://ref.ly/Luke5:3) · [JHN 21:6](https://ref.ly/Luke21:6) · [ACT 4:32](https://ref.ly/John4:32) · [ACT 5:14](https://ref.ly/John5:14) · [ACT 17:4](https://ref.ly/John17:4) · [ACT 21:36](https://ref.ly/John21:36) · [ACT 28:3](https://ref.ly/John28:3) · [HEB 11:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:12) · [JAS 5:20](https://ref.ly/Heb5:20) · [1PE 4:8](https://ref.ly/Jas4:8)

11\.1 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · General

crowd, multitude a casual non\-membership group of people, fairly large in size and assembled for whatever purpose1 In a number of languages there is no term corresponding to ‘crowd.’ One may, however, usually speak of such a group of people as ‘many people’ or ‘many men and women.’ Depending on the context, it may besary to indicate some relative difference in size, for example, ‘very large crowd’ or ‘many, many people.’ If the crowd is relatively small, one may sometimes speak of ‘just some people.’

[MRK 3:7](https://ref.ly/Matt3:7) · [MRK 3:8](https://ref.ly/Matt3:8) · [LUK 1:10](https://ref.ly/Mark1:10) · [LUK 2:13](https://ref.ly/Mark2:13) · [LUK 6:17](https://ref.ly/Mark6:17) · [LUK 8:37](https://ref.ly/Mark8:37) · [LUK 19:37](https://ref.ly/Mark19:37) · [LUK 23:1](https://ref.ly/Mark23:1) · [LUK 23:27](https://ref.ly/Mark23:27) · [ACT 2:6](https://ref.ly/John2:6) · [ACT 5:16](https://ref.ly/John5:16) · [ACT 6:2](https://ref.ly/John6:2) · [ACT 6:5](https://ref.ly/John6:5) · [ACT 14:1](https://ref.ly/John14:1) · [ACT 14:4](https://ref.ly/John14:4) · [ACT 15:12](https://ref.ly/John15:12) · [ACT 15:30](https://ref.ly/John15:30) · [ACT 19:9](https://ref.ly/John19:9) · [ACT 23:7](https://ref.ly/John23:7) · [ACT 25:24](https://ref.ly/John25:24)

1 In this set of terms referring to a crowd or a multitude, it is possible that πλῆθος\[b] denotes a somewhat larger group of persons than the other terms and that λαός\[c] suggests a crowd made up of common people, but one cannot be certain of these distinctions from existing contexts.

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 5:6; John 5:3; John 21:6; Acts 4:32; Acts 5:14; Acts 17:4; Acts 21:36; Acts 28:3; Hebrews 11:12; James 5:20; 1 Peter 4:8; Mark 3:7; Mark 3:8; Luke 1:10; Luke 2:13; Luke 6:17; Luke 8:37; Luke 19:37; Luke 23:1; Luke 23:27; Acts 2:6; Acts 5:16; Acts 6:2; Acts 6:5; Acts 14:1; Acts 14:4; Acts 15:12; Acts 15:30; Acts 19:9; Acts 23:7; Acts 25:24


## πληθύνω (id: 004033000000000)

πληθύνω
=======

πληθύνω (G4129)

πίμπλημι

59\.68 · Quantity · Increase, Decrease

to grow, to increase greatly, to multiply to increase greatly in number or extent It may be difficult to speak of ‘a number growing’ in some languages, so that one may need to translate this expression in [ACT 6:7](https://ref.ly/John6:7) as ‘the disciples in Jerusalem became more and more’ or ‘there were more and more disciples in Jerusalem.’  
It may be difficult, if not impossible, to speak of ‘evil increasing’ as in [MAT 24:12](https://ref.ly/Matt24:12), but one can usually speak of a greater number of instances of doing evil, and therefore one may translate ‘more and more people will be doing what is evil.’  
A literal rendering of [ACT 12:24](https://ref.ly/John12:24) may be impossible in many languages, for a strictly literal rendering would imply that somehow the message from God ‘got bigger,’ which may mean in some languages ‘resulted in lies.’ The meaning is, of course, that more and more people accepted God’s word as true.

[MAT 24:12](https://ref.ly/Matt24:12) · [ACT 6:1](https://ref.ly/John6:1) · [ACT 6:7](https://ref.ly/John6:7) · [ACT 7:17](https://ref.ly/John7:17) · [ACT 9:31](https://ref.ly/John9:31) · [ACT 12:24](https://ref.ly/John12:24) · [1PE 1:2](https://ref.ly/Jas1:2) · [2PE 1:2](https://ref.ly/1Pet1:2) · [JUD 1:2](https://ref.ly/3John1:2)

59\.69 · Quantity · Increase, Decrease

to increase, to cause an increase, to multiply to cause an increase in the number of objects In [HEB 6:14](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:14) it is essential in most languages to specify what is increased. A strictly literal translation such as ‘to cause you to increase’ would simply mean ‘to cause you to get big.’ The reference is clearly to Abraham’s descendants, and it is usually essential to make this reference clear.

[2CO 9:10](https://ref.ly/1Cor9:10) · [HEB 6:14](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:14) · [HEB 6:14](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:14)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 24:12; Acts 6:1; Acts 6:7; Acts 7:17; Acts 9:31; Acts 12:24; 1 Peter 1:2; 2 Peter 1:2; Jude 1:2; 2 Corinthians 9:10; Hebrews 6:14

flood, high water the overflowing of the banks by a river or stream One might very well render [LUK 6:48](https://ref.ly/Mark6:48) as ‘when the river overflowed its banks, it burst against the house.’ In some instances it may not be possible to use a term which would be more or less literally ‘to burst against,’ but one can say ‘tried to cause the house to collapse’ or ‘tried to destroy the house.’

full a quantity of space completely occupied by something

whole, complete, full a totality which has been brought to completion

πληρόω \+ φέρω

to cause to happen, to make happen, to fulfill to cause to happen, with the implication of fulfilling some purpose1

to proclaim, to tell fully, to proclaim completely to relate fully the content of a message

==========

to be absolutely sure, to be certain, complete certainty to be completely certain of the truth of something The phrase ‘with complete certainty’ may be expressed in some languages as ‘you may surely believe it’ or ‘there is no reason at all for you to doubt.’

to finish, to complete to finish an activity after having done everything involved

[LUK 7:1](https://ref.ly/Mark7:1) · [ACT 12:25](https://ref.ly/John12:25) · [ACT 13:25](https://ref.ly/John13:25) · [ACT 14:26](https://ref.ly/John14:26) · [ACT 19:21](https://ref.ly/John19:21) · [2TH 1:11](https://ref.ly/1Thess1:11)

35\.33 · Help, Care For · Provide For, Support

to provide for completely, to supply fully to provide for by supplying a complete amount ‘To be fully provided for’ may be expressed as ‘to have all that one needs.’

[PHP 4:18](https://ref.ly/Eph4:18) · [PHP 4:19](https://ref.ly/Eph4:19)

to proclaim, to tell fully, to proclaim completely to relate fully the content of a message

33\.144 · Communication · Interpret, Mean, Explain

to give the true meaning to, to provide the real significance of to give the true or complete meaning to something In speaking of ‘true meaning,’ it may be useful in some languages to use a phrase meaning ‘real intent’ or ‘real purpose.’

[MAT 5:17](https://ref.ly/Matt5:17) · [GAL 5:14](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:14)

13\.106 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Happen

to cause to happen, to make happen, to fulfill to cause to happen, with the implication of fulfilling some purpose1

[MAT 1:22](https://ref.ly/Matt1:22) · [MAT 2:15](https://ref.ly/Matt2:15) · [MAT 2:17](https://ref.ly/Matt2:17) · [MAT 2:23](https://ref.ly/Matt2:23) · [MAT 3:15](https://ref.ly/Matt3:15) · [MAT 4:14](https://ref.ly/Matt4:14) · [MAT 8:17](https://ref.ly/Matt8:17) · [MAT 12:17](https://ref.ly/Matt12:17) · [MAT 13:35](https://ref.ly/Matt13:35) · [MAT 21:4](https://ref.ly/Matt21:4) · [MAT 26:54](https://ref.ly/Matt26:54) · [MAT 26:56](https://ref.ly/Matt26:56) · [MAT 27:9](https://ref.ly/Matt27:9) · [MRK 14:49](https://ref.ly/Matt14:49) · [LUK 1:20](https://ref.ly/Mark1:20) · [LUK 4:21](https://ref.ly/Mark4:21) · [LUK 9:31](https://ref.ly/Mark9:31) · [LUK 22:16](https://ref.ly/Mark22:16) · [LUK 24:44](https://ref.ly/Mark24:44) · [JHN 7:8](https://ref.ly/Luke7:8) · [JHN 12:38](https://ref.ly/Luke12:38) · [JHN 13:18](https://ref.ly/Luke13:18) · [JHN 15:25](https://ref.ly/Luke15:25) · [JHN 17:12](https://ref.ly/Luke17:12) · [JHN 18:9](https://ref.ly/Luke18:9) · [JHN 18:32](https://ref.ly/Luke18:32) · [JHN 19:24](https://ref.ly/Luke19:24) · [JHN 19:36](https://ref.ly/Luke19:36) · [ACT 1:16](https://ref.ly/John1:16) · [ACT 3:18](https://ref.ly/John3:18) · [ACT 13:27](https://ref.ly/John13:27) · [COL 4:17](https://ref.ly/Phil4:17) · [2TH 1:11](https://ref.ly/1Thess1:11) · [JAS 2:23](https://ref.ly/Heb2:23)

30\.29 · Think · To Think, Thought

**Collocation:** πληρόω τὴν καρδίαν

to make think, to fill the heart, to cause to decide (an idiom, literally: ‘to fill the heart)to cause someone to think in a particular manner, often as a means of inducing some behavior

[JHN 16:6](https://ref.ly/Luke16:6) · [ACT 5:3](https://ref.ly/John5:3)

67\.70 · (πληρόομαι\[a]) · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Duration of Time: Beginning, End

to complete, to come to an end to come to the end of a period of time, with the implication of the completion of an implied purpose or plan2 In a number of languages it may be difficult to speak of a period of time being ‘complete.’ In [LUK 1:23](https://ref.ly/Mark1:23) one may often translate as ‘when he had finished his period of service’ or even ‘when he had done his work.’ In the case of [ACT 7:30](https://ref.ly/John7:30), the expression ‘after forty years were completed’ may be rendered in some languages as ‘after forty years were passed’ or ‘when forty years had happened.’

[LUK 21:24](https://ref.ly/Mark21:24) · [ACT 7:23](https://ref.ly/John7:23) · [ACT 7:30](https://ref.ly/John7:30) · [ACT 9:23](https://ref.ly/John9:23) · [ACT 24:27](https://ref.ly/John24:27) · [REV 3:2](https://ref.ly/Jude3:2)

78\.46 · (πληρόομαι\[b]) · Degree · Completely, Enough \[Completive Degree]

to be completely, to be entirely, to be full of, to be totally to experience a complete degree of involvement in some event or state

[ACT 13:52](https://ref.ly/John13:52) · [2JN 1:12](https://ref.ly/1John1:12)

1 There are undoubtedly certain subtle differences of meaning in this set of terms in [13\.106](#13.106), but the distinctive features cannot be determined from existing contexts. 2 In a number of contexts it is difficult to determine whether the meanings in [67\.69](#67.69) and [67\.70](#67.70) are primarily temporal or aspectual. If the meaning in any one context seems to focus upon the completion of an activity, then the meaning should be classified as aspectual (Domain 68 Aspect), but if the emphasis would seem to be primarily upon the end point of an activity, then one may legitimately classify these meanings as being temporal.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 13:48; Matthew 23:32; Luke 2:40; Luke 3:5; John 3:29; John 12:3; Acts 2:2; Acts 2:28; Acts 5:28; Romans 1:29; Romans 15:13; Romans 15:14; 2 Corinthians 7:4; Ephesians 3:19; Ephesians 4:10; Ephesians 5:18; Philippians 1:11; Colossians 1:9; 2 Timothy 1:4; Mark 1:15; John 15:11; John 16:24; John 17:13; Romans 8:4; Romans 13:8; 2 Corinthians 10:6; Ephesians 1:23; Philippians 2:2; Colossians 2:10; 1 John 1:4; Revelation 6:11; Luke 7:1; Acts 12:25; Acts 13:25; Acts 14:26; Acts 19:21; 2 Thessalonians 1:11; Philippians 4:18; Philippians 4:19; Romans 15:19; Colossians 1:25; Matthew 5:17; Galatians 5:14; Matthew 1:22; Matthew 2:15; Matthew 2:17; Matthew 2:23; Matthew 3:15; Matthew 4:14; Matthew 8:17; Matthew 12:17; Matthew 13:35; Matthew 21:4; Matthew 26:54; Matthew 26:56; Matthew 27:9; Mark 14:49; Luke 1:20; Luke 4:21; Luke 9:31; Luke 22:16; Luke 24:44; John 7:8; John 12:38; John 13:18; John 15:25; John 17:12; John 18:9; John 18:32; John 19:24; John 19:36; Acts 1:16; Acts 3:18; Acts 13:27; Colossians 4:17; James 2:23; John 16:6; Acts 5:3; Luke 21:24; Acts 7:23; Acts 7:30; Acts 9:23; Acts 24:27; Revelation 3:2; Acts 13:52; 2 John 1:12


## πλήρωμα (id: 004041000000000)

πλήρωμα
=======

πλήρωμα (G4138)

noun \| \-τος \| πληρόω

59\.36 · Quantity · Full, Empty

that which fills, contents a quantity which fills a space

[MAT 9:16](https://ref.ly/Matt9:16) · [MRK 2:21](https://ref.ly/Matt2:21) · [MRK 6:43](https://ref.ly/Matt6:43) · [MRK 8:20](https://ref.ly/Matt8:20) · [1CO 10:26](https://ref.ly/Rom10:26)

59\.32 · Quantity · All, Any, Each, Every \[Totality]

full number, full measure, fullness, completeness, totality a total quantity, with emphasis upon completeness In a number of languages it may be difficult to use a generic expression such as ‘totality.’ As a result, this clause in [COL 2:9](https://ref.ly/Phil2:9) must often be completely restructured, for example, ‘for Christ is completely like God’ or ‘for just what God is, that is exactly what Christ is.’

[JHN 1:16](https://ref.ly/Luke1:16) · [ROM 11:12](https://ref.ly/Acts11:12) · [ROM 11:25](https://ref.ly/Acts11:25) · [ROM 13:10](https://ref.ly/Acts13:10) · [ROM 15:29](https://ref.ly/Acts15:29) · [EPH 1:23](https://ref.ly/Gal1:23) · [EPH 3:19](https://ref.ly/Gal3:19) · [EPH 4:13](https://ref.ly/Gal4:13) · [COL 1:19](https://ref.ly/Phil1:19) · [COL 2:9](https://ref.ly/Phil2:9)

67\.69 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Duration of Time: Beginning, End

end, completion the totality of a period of time, with the implication of proper completion1

[GAL 4:4](https://ref.ly/2Cor4:4) · [EPH 1:10](https://ref.ly/Gal1:10)

1 In a number of contexts it is difficult to determine whether the meanings in [67\.69](#67.69) and [67\.70](#67.70) are primarily temporal or aspectual. If the meaning in any one context seems to focus upon the completion of an activity, then the meaning should be classified as aspectual (Domain 68 Aspect), but if the emphasis would seem to be primarily upon the end point of an activity, then one may legitimately classify these meanings as being temporal.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 9:16; Mark 2:21; Mark 6:43; Mark 8:20; 1 Corinthians 10:26; John 1:16; Romans 11:12; Romans 11:25; Romans 13:10; Romans 15:29; Ephesians 1:23; Ephesians 3:19; Ephesians 4:13; Colossians 1:19; Colossians 2:9; Galatians 4:4; Ephesians 1:10


## πλησίον (id: 004042000000000)

πλησίον
=======

πλησίον (G4139)

prep \| παραπλήσιον · παραπλησίως

83\.27 · Spacial Positions · At, Beside, Near, Far

quite near, nearby a position quite close to another position, with the possible implication of being contiguous

[JHN 4:5](https://ref.ly/Luke4:5)

11\.89 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Political

neighbor, brother a person who lives close beside others and who thus by implication is a part of a so\-called in\-group, that is, the group with which an individual identifies both ethnically and culturally It may be that of the three terms γείτων, περίοικος, and πλησίον\[b], the last is somewhat broader in meaning. At least πλησίον is used in [LUK 10:27](https://ref.ly/Mark10:27) in a somewhat extended sense of ‘fellow man,’ while ἀδελφός\[e] probably suggests a more intimate or close relationship.  
The translation of πλησίον\[b] as a singular in [LUK 10:27](https://ref.ly/Mark10:27) may pose a problem in understanding, since the singular may be understood in a specific rather than in a generic sense, that is to say, ‘love your neighbor as yourself’ might immediately give rise to the question ‘which neighbor?’ Therefore, one must often translate [LUK 10:27](https://ref.ly/Mark10:27) as ‘you must love your neighbors as you love yourselves’ or ‘you must love other people as you love yourselves.’

[MAT 5:43](https://ref.ly/Matt5:43) · [MAT 19:19](https://ref.ly/Matt19:19) · [MAT 22:39](https://ref.ly/Matt22:39) · [MRK 12:31](https://ref.ly/Matt12:31) · [MRK 12:33](https://ref.ly/Matt12:33) · [LUK 10:27](https://ref.ly/Mark10:27) · [LUK 10:29](https://ref.ly/Mark10:29) · [LUK 10:36](https://ref.ly/Mark10:36) · [ACT 7:27](https://ref.ly/John7:27) · [ROM 13:9](https://ref.ly/Acts13:9) · [ROM 13:10](https://ref.ly/Acts13:10) · [ROM 15:2](https://ref.ly/Acts15:2) · [GAL 5:14](https://ref.ly/2Cor5:14) · [EPH 4:25](https://ref.ly/Gal4:25) · [JAS 2:8](https://ref.ly/Heb2:8) · [JAS 4:12](https://ref.ly/Heb4:12)

* **Associated Passages:** John 4:5; Matthew 5:43; Matthew 19:19; Matthew 22:39; Mark 12:31; Mark 12:33; Luke 10:27; Luke 10:29; Luke 10:36; Acts 7:27; Romans 13:9; Romans 13:10; Romans 15:2; Galatians 5:14; Ephesians 4:25; James 2:8; James 4:12


## πλησμονή (id: 004043000000000)

πλησμονή
========

πλησμονή (G4140)

noun \| \-ῆς \| πίμπλημι

25\.14 · Attitudes and Emotions · Desire Strongly

gratification, indulgence the process of indulging in or procuring the satisfaction of certain desires or needs There have been extensive differences of opinion with regard to the precise meaning of πλησμονή and of σάρξ in [COL 2:23](https://ref.ly/Phil2:23). Some early Greek exegetes understood this passage to involve ‘gratification of physical needs,’ but since σάρξ seemingly needs to be understood in a bad sense, the meaning of ‘indulgence’ seems to be more appropriate. The meaning of ‘indulgence’ can be expressed in some languages as ‘to give in to’ or ‘to surrender to’ or ‘to permit oneself to enjoy.’

[COL 2:23](https://ref.ly/Phil2:23)

* **Associated Passages:** Colossians 2:23


## πλήσσω (id: 004044000000000)

πλήσσω
======

πλήσσω (G4141)

aor.pass. ἐπλήγην \| ἐκπλήσσομαι · ἐπιπλήσσω · πληγή

19\.1 · Physical Impact · Hit, Strike

to hit, to strike, to beat to strike or hit an object, one or more times In [REV 8:12](https://ref.ly/Jude8:12) the emphasis seems to be upon the damage caused to the sun rather than the actual fact of a blow, but the use of πλήσσω seems to indicate that the damage is the result of some type of blow.

[REV 8:12](https://ref.ly/Jude8:12)

* **Associated Passages:** Revelation 8:12


## πλοιάριον (id: 004045000000000)

πλοιάριον
=========

πλοιάριον (G4142)

noun \| \-ου \| πλέω

6\.42 · Artifacts · Boats and Parts of Boats

small boat (a diminutive form of πλοῖον ‘boat, ship,’ [6\.41](#6.41), but in some contexts it is possible that the diminutive aspect of πλοιάριον is not relevant) small fishing boat Receptor language terms to be employed for πλοιάριον, πλοῖον ([6\.41](#6.41)), and ναῦς ([6\.43](#6.43)) depend largely upon the contextual equivalents in each receptor language.

[MRK 3:9](https://ref.ly/Matt3:9) · [JHN 6:22](https://ref.ly/Luke6:22) · [JHN 6:23](https://ref.ly/Luke6:23) · [JHN 6:24](https://ref.ly/Luke6:24) · [JHN 21:8](https://ref.ly/Luke21:8)

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 3:9; John 6:22; John 6:23; John 6:24; John 21:8


## πλοῖον (id: 004046000000000)

πλοῖον
======

πλοῖον (G4143)

noun \| \-ου \| πλέω

6\.41 · Artifacts · Boats and Parts of Boats

boat, ship any kind of boat, from small fishing boats as on Lake Galilee to large seagoing vessels It may be very important to distinguish clearly between small fishing boats and larger ships or vessels. In a number of languages the distinction is based upon whether or not such vessels have decks. For the fishing boats on Lake Galilee there was probably no deck structure, while vessels going for long distances on the Mediterranean would certainly have had decks. See [6\.42](#6.42).

πλουτέω

to be rich, to be wealthy, well\-todo to have considerably more than what would be regarded as the norm in a society

to become rich, to become wealthy, to prosper to prosper to the point of being rich In some instances it may be useful to translate ‘to become wealthy’ as ‘he soon possessed much’ or even ‘he was no longer poor, but rich.’ By introducing ‘poor’ in contrast with ‘rich,’ one may identify a change of state.

to be rich in, to have a great deal of to have a large amount of something which has value

πλουτέω

59\.59 · Quantity · Abundance, Excess, Sparing

## πλοῦτος (id: 004052000000000)

πλοῦτος
=======

πλοῦτος (G4149)

noun \| \-ου \| πλουτέω

57\.30 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Be Rich, Be Wealthy

wealth, riches, abundance an abundance of possessions exceeding the norm of a particular society and often with a negative connotation ‘Your riches’ in [JAS 5:2](https://ref.ly/Heb5:2) may be expressed by means of a descriptive phrase, for example, ‘all the many things you possess.’

[MAT 13:22](https://ref.ly/Matt13:22) · [MRK 4:19](https://ref.ly/Matt4:19) · [LUK 8:14](https://ref.ly/Mark8:14) · [ROM 9:23](https://ref.ly/Acts9:23) · [ROM 11:12](https://ref.ly/Acts11:12) · [ROM 11:12](https://ref.ly/Acts11:12) · [ROM 11:33](https://ref.ly/Acts11:33) · [PHP 4:19](https://ref.ly/Eph4:19) · [1TI 6:17](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:17) · [HEB 11:26](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:26) · [JAS 5:2](https://ref.ly/Heb5:2) · [REV 5:12](https://ref.ly/Jude5:12) · [REV 18:17](https://ref.ly/Jude18:17)

78\.15 · Degree · Much, Little \[Positive\-Negative Degree]

great, abundant, abundantly, greatly, extremely a high point on any scale and having the implication of value as well as abundance In a number of languages, the expression τὸ πλοῦτος τῆς χάριτος αὐτοῦ in [EPH 1:7](https://ref.ly/Gal1:7) would need to be translated as ‘he is very, very kind.’ Though syntactically ὁ πλοῦτος is the nominal head of the phrase in [EPH 1:18](https://ref.ly/Gal1:18), semantically ὁ πλοῦτος indicates the extent of the δόξα, and δόξα, in turn, clarifies the nature of the κληρονομία.

[ROM 2:4](https://ref.ly/Acts2:4) · [2CO 8:2](https://ref.ly/1Cor8:2) · [EPH 1:7](https://ref.ly/Gal1:7) · [EPH 1:18](https://ref.ly/Gal1:18) · [EPH 2:7](https://ref.ly/Gal2:7) · [EPH 3:8](https://ref.ly/Gal3:8) · [EPH 3:16](https://ref.ly/Gal3:16) · [COL 1:27](https://ref.ly/Phil1:27) · [COL 2:2](https://ref.ly/Phil2:2)
