## πνεῦμα (id: 004054000000000)

πνεῦμα
======

πνεῦμα (G4151)

noun \| \-τος \| πνέω

12\.18 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

Spirit, Spirit of God, Holy Spirit a title for the third person of the Trinity, literally: spirit In many religious systems the significant difference between the gods and the spirits is that the gods are regarded as supernatural beings which control certain aspects of natural phenomena, while the spirits are supernatural beings, often impersonal, which indwell or inhabit certain places, including rivers, streams, mountains, caves, animals, and people. Spirits are often regarded as being primarily evil, though it may be possible to induce them to be favorable to people.  
It is extremely difficult to find in some languages a fully satisfactory term to speak of the Spirit of God. If one uses a term which normally identifies local supernatural beings, there is a tendency to read into the term the meaning of evil or mischievous character. If, however, one uses a term which may identify the spirit of a person, the problems may even be greater, since according to many systems of religious belief, the spirit of an individual does not become active until the individual dies. Therefore, the activity of the Spirit of God would presumably suggest that God himself had died. However, if one uses a term which means ‘heart’ or ‘soul’ (and thus the Spirit of God would be literally equivalent to ‘the heart of God’), there may be complications since this aspect of human personality is often regarded as not being able to act on its own.  
The solutions to the problem of ‘Spirit’ have been varied. In some languages the term for Spirit is essentially equivalent to ‘the unseen one,’ and therefore the Spirit of God is essentially equivalent to ‘the invisibleness of God.’  
In a number of languages the closest equivalent for Spirit is ‘breath,’ and in a number of indigenous religious systems, the ‘breath’ is regarded as having a kind of independent existence. In other languages the term for Spirit is equivalent to what is often translated as ‘the soul,’ that is to say, the immaterial part of a person. There is, of course, always the difficulty of employing a term meaning ‘soul’ or ‘life,’ since it often proves to be impersonal and thus provides no basis for speaking of the Spirit of God as being a person or a personal manifestation of God.  
In quite a few languages the equivalent of Spirit is literally ‘shadow,’ since the ‘shadow’ of a person is regarded as the immaterial part of the individual. Moreover, in many systems of religious thought the shadow is regarded as having some significant measure of independent existence.  
In a few cases the term for Spirit is literally ‘wind,’ but there are frequently difficulties involved in this type of terminology since a term for wind often suggests calamity or evil intent. One meaning of Spirit which must be clearly avoided is that of ‘apparition’ or ‘ghost.’  
Frequently it is not possible to find a fully satisfactory term for ‘Spirit,’ and therefore in all contexts some characterizing feature is added, for example, either ‘of God’ or ‘holy,’ in the sense of ‘divine.’ This may be particularly necessary in a passage such as [MRK 1:12](https://ref.ly/Matt1:12) where the average reader might assume that a spirit which would make Jesus go out into the desert would be an evil rather than a good spirit.  
Often the Holy Spirit is named by phrases which explain something of the nature and activity of the Spirit: πνεῦμα (ἐκ τοῦ) θεοῦ ‘Spirit of God,’ πνεῦμα ἅγιον ‘Holy Spirit,’ πνεῦμα αἰώνιον ‘the Eternal Spirit’ (often called ‘the unending spirit’ or ‘the spirit that never ceases’), πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας ‘the Spirit of Truth,’ that is, ‘the Spirit who communicates truth’ (sometimes rendered as ‘the Spirit that communicates the truth about God’); πνεῦμα τῆς δόξης ‘Spirit of glory,’ that is, ‘the glorious Spirit’ or ‘the wonderful Spirit’; πνεῦμα τῆς ζωῆς ‘the Spirit of life,’ that is, ‘the Spirit who brings life’ or ‘the Spirit that causes people to live’; πνεῦμα υἱοθεσίας ‘the Spirit of sonship,’ that is, ‘the Spirit who makes us sons of God’ or ‘the Spirit that causes us to become God’s sons;’ πνεῦμα τῆς χάριτος ‘Spirit of grace,’ that is, ‘the Spirit who bestows kindness’ or ‘the Spirit who shows kindness to us;’ πνεῦμα σοφίας καὶ ἀποκάλυπσεως ‘Spirit of wisdom and revelation,’ that is ‘the Spirit who gives wisdom and who reveals God’ or ‘the Spirit who causes us to become wise and who shows us what God is like’ (or ‘... who God truly is’). The phrase πνεῦμα κυρίου, ‘Spirit of the Lord,’ is ambiguous because κύριος may either refer to God the Father or to Jesus Christ, but the Spirit in either case is the same: τί ὅτι συνεφωνήθη ὑμῖν πειράσαι τὸ πνεῦμα κυρίου; ‘why did you decide to put the Lord’s Spirit to the test?’ [ACT 5:9](https://ref.ly/John5:9).

22:17](https://ref.ly/Jude22:17)

12\.33 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

spirit a supernatural non\-material being πνεῦμα in [JHN 4:24](https://ref.ly/Luke4:24) and [ACT 23:8](https://ref.ly/John23:8) is highly generic. There is no implication of such a spirit being either good or evil, nor is πνεῦμα in such contexts to be regarded as merely an aspect of some other being. The reference is simply to a supernatural and non\-material entity. In rendering [ACT 23:8](https://ref.ly/John23:8), one may speak of the Sadducees rejection of the existence of spirits as ‘they say ... there is nothing which isn’t physical’ or ‘... everything that exists is physical.’

[JHN 3:6](https://ref.ly/Luke3:6) · [JHN 4:24](https://ref.ly/Luke4:24) · [JHN 6:63](https://ref.ly/Luke6:63) · [ACT 23:8](https://ref.ly/John23:8) · [ACT 23:9](https://ref.ly/John23:9) · [ROM 11:8](https://ref.ly/Acts11:8) · [1CO 12:10](https://ref.ly/Rom12:10) · [1CO 15:45](https://ref.ly/Rom15:45) · [2CO 11:4](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:4) · [2TH 2:2](https://ref.ly/1Thess2:2) · [HEB 1:14](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:14) · [1JN 4:1](https://ref.ly/2Pet4:1) · [1JN 4:1](https://ref.ly/2Pet4:1) · [1JN 4:2](https://ref.ly/2Pet4:2) · [1JN 4:6](https://ref.ly/2Pet4:6) · [1JN 4:6](https://ref.ly/2Pet4:6) · [REV 1:4](https://ref.ly/Jude1:4) · [REV 3:1](https://ref.ly/Jude3:1) · [REV 4:5](https://ref.ly/Jude4:5) · [REV 5:6](https://ref.ly/Jude5:6)

12\.37 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

demon, evil spirit an evil supernatural being or spirit While in a number of languages the Devil may be spoken of as ‘the chief of the demons,’ sometimes the demons are simply called ‘the spirits of the Devil’ or ‘the servants of the Devil.’  
In a number of languages the difficulty in terminology for demons is not the absence of a term, but the abundance of different terms for different kinds of demons, each one of which may be responsible for particular kinds of human behavior, for example, insanity, depression, epilepsy, sex perversion, and violent assault. Sometimes demons are classified as ‘those of the home’ and ‘those of the forest,’ in which case the latter are normally regarded as more violent and virulent. When there is an abundance of different terms for demons and no generic term for all types of demons, one can usually select a class of demons which parallels most closely the descriptions of demon activity in the NT and use such a term with appropriate contextual qualifications so as to suggest that such a term is to be understood in a general sense.

[MAT 8:16](https://ref.ly/Matt8:16) · [MAT 12:45](https://ref.ly/Matt12:45) · [MRK 9:17](https://ref.ly/Matt9:17) · [MRK 9:20](https://ref.ly/Matt9:20) · [MRK 9:25](https://ref.ly/Matt9:25) · [LUK 4:33](https://ref.ly/Mark4:33) · [LUK 9:39](https://ref.ly/Mark9:39) · [LUK 10:20](https://ref.ly/Mark10:20) · [LUK 13:11](https://ref.ly/Mark13:11) · [ACT 16:16](https://ref.ly/John16:16) · [ACT 16:18](https://ref.ly/John16:18) · [ACT 19:12](https://ref.ly/John19:12) · [ACT 19:13](https://ref.ly/John19:13) · [ACT 19:15](https://ref.ly/John19:15) · [ACT 19:16](https://ref.ly/John19:16) · [EPH 2:2](https://ref.ly/Gal2:2) · [1TI 4:1](https://ref.ly/2Thess4:1) · [REV 16:14](https://ref.ly/Jude16:14)

12\.42 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

ghost an apparition Most languages have quite satisfactory terms for ‘ghosts’ or ‘apparitions,’ since the psychological phenomena associated with such appearances are apparently universal. In some languages the equivalent of ‘ghost’ is simply ‘shadow.’ In other instances it may be literally ‘breath.’ In some instances the meaning of ‘ghost’ is incorporated within a verb expression, for example, ‘they saw, as it were, through him.’  
The NT contains a number of lexical items ([12\.43](#12.43)\-[12\.47](#12.47)) referring to supernatural powers believed to be active as elemental spirits exercising control over man’s fate. Though these may have certain special features associated with them and though in extrabiblical literature they may represent various grades of supernatural power, there seems to be no way in which they can be clearly distinguished on the basis of their NT usage. Appropriate equivalent expressions in other languages are extremely difficult to find, primarily because other religious systems usually have completely different beliefs relative to supernatural powers. In many languages it is simply not possible to speak of such supernatural powers without in some way identifying them with various kinds of spirits.

[LUK 24:37](https://ref.ly/Mark24:37) · [LUK 24:39](https://ref.ly/Mark24:39)

26\.9 · Psychological Faculties

spirit, spiritual, spiritual nature, inner being the non\-material, psychological faculty which is potentially sensitive and responsive to God (πνεῦμα\[e] contrasts with σάρξ\[f], [26\.7](#26.7), as an expression of the divine in contrast with the purely human) A special problem is posed by the phrase πνεῦμα ἁγιωσύνης in [ROM 1:4](https://ref.ly/Acts1:4). Some persons have assumed that this phrase is merely a lexical alternative for πνεῦμα ἅγιον ‘Holy Spirit,’ but this hardly seems to be the case, especially since κατὰ πνεῦμα ἁγιωσύνης is in structural contrast with κατὰ σάρκα ([ROM 1:3](https://ref.ly/Acts1:3)). The phrase κατὰ σάρκα seems to be best interpreted as referring to the humanity of Jesus, and therefore in contrast κατὰ πνεῦμα ἁγιωσύνης may perhaps be best interpreted as his ‘divine holiness’ or rather his ‘holy spiritual being.’ Accordingly, the relevant elements in [ROM 1:3](https://ref.ly/Acts1:3)b\-[ROM 1:4](https://ref.ly/Acts1:4) may be rendered as ‘as to his humanity, he was born a descendant of David, but as to his divine nature, he was shown with great power to be the Son of God by being raised from death.’  
There are often a number of serious problems involved in obtaining a satisfactory term for translating πνεῦμα\[e]. In a few instances one can use a more or less literal equivalent, namely ‘breath,’ and in other instances the appropriate equivalent is a derived term meaning ‘that which doesn’t die.’ In some instances the equivalent is ‘the person who isn’t seen,’ meaning that part of the person which is never visible. In order to emphasize the non\-material aspects of πνεῦμα\[e], some persons have used terms which actually refer to ghosts, but this should be avoided.  
In a number of languages a clear distinction is made between (1\) the spirit that dwells within a person during one’s lifetime and (2\) that spirit which leaves a person and passes on into the next world. In a number of contexts one must make certain that the appropriate term is used; otherwise, there is not only extreme confusion but serious misunderstanding.

30\.6 · Think · To Think, Thought

disposition, attitude, way of thinking (compare πνεῦμα\[e] ‘inner being,’ [26\.9](#26.9)) an attitude or disposition reflecting the way in which a person thinks about or deals with some matter In some languages πνεῦμα\[f] may be regarded as implicit in the context itself, so that the last part of [GAL 6:1](https://ref.ly/2Cor6:1) may well be translated as ‘but do it with gentleness.’

[ROM 8:15](https://ref.ly/Acts8:15) · [1CO 2:12](https://ref.ly/Rom2:12) · [1CO 4:21](https://ref.ly/Rom4:21) · [1CO 14:12](https://ref.ly/Rom14:12) · [2CO 4:13](https://ref.ly/1Cor4:13) · [2CO 12:18](https://ref.ly/1Cor12:18) · [GAL 6:1](https://ref.ly/2Cor6:1) · [EPH 1:17](https://ref.ly/Gal1:17) · [EPH 4:23](https://ref.ly/Gal4:23) · [PHP 1:27](https://ref.ly/Eph1:27) · [2TH 2:13](https://ref.ly/1Thess2:13) · [1PE 3:4](https://ref.ly/Jas3:4)

14\.4 · Physical Events and States · Wind

wind, blowing, to blow air in relatively rapid movement, but without specification as to the force of the movement As in the case of English (which has, for example, such terms as breeze, zephyr, gust, storm, whirlwind, cyclone, tornado, etc.), other languages likewise normally have a number of different terms depending upon the strength of the wind and whether the wind may be carrying substances such as sand or dust. In some languages there may also be a problem involved in the use of terms for ‘wind,’ since some of these may have meanings of malicious or malevolent spirits, or they may imply a disease\-bringing event.  
In [PSA 104:4](https://ref.ly/Ps104:4) the Hebrew actually means ‘you use the winds as your messengers.’ The form cited here in [HEB 1:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:7) reflects the Septuagint translation. It may be relevant in some translations to call attention to the reason for this difference, since it may not be easy to make the Greek form of this expression fully meaningful, for literally ‘to turn his angels into winds’ may seem like purposeless magic. Some translators must employ a phrase such as ‘he makes his angels like winds.’

[JHN 3:8](https://ref.ly/Luke3:8) · [HEB 1:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:7)

23\.186 · Physiological Processes and States · Breathe, Breath

breath a breath of air coming from the lungs In this figurative context, πνεῦμα\[h] refers to a single breath of air. It does not refer to the process of breathing (see πνοή\[b] breath, [23\.187](#23.187)). The use of breath to kill a person may immediately suggest in some languages the power of sorcery. It would be difficult indeed to avoid this connotation in view of the total context, but it may be necessary to have some type of marginal note explaining the symbolic significance.

[2TH 2:8](https://ref.ly/1Thess2:8) · [JAS 2:26](https://ref.ly/Heb2:26) · [REV 11:11](https://ref.ly/Jude11:11) · [REV 13:15](https://ref.ly/Jude13:15)

12\.39 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

**Collocation:** πνεῦμα ἀκάθαρτον

unclean spirit (a fixed phrase) an evil supernatural spirit which is ritually unclean and which causes persons to be ritually unclean It is important in rendering a term such as ‘unclean’ to avoid the implication that one is merely speaking of ‘dirty demons.’ What is important about the term ‘unclean’ is that the possession of such a spirit makes the individual ritually or ceremonially unclean. Accordingly, ‘an unclean spirit’ is equivalent in a number of languages to ‘a contaminating spirit.’

[MAT 10:1](https://ref.ly/Matt10:1) · [MAT 12:43](https://ref.ly/Matt12:43) · [MRK 1:23](https://ref.ly/Matt1:23) · [MRK 1:26](https://ref.ly/Matt1:26) · [MRK 1:27](https://ref.ly/Matt1:27) · [MRK 3:11](https://ref.ly/Matt3:11) · [MRK 3:30](https://ref.ly/Matt3:30) · [MRK 5:2](https://ref.ly/Matt5:2) · [MRK 5:8](https://ref.ly/Matt5:8) · [MRK 5:13](https://ref.ly/Matt5:13) · [MRK 6:7](https://ref.ly/Matt6:7) · [MRK 7:25](https://ref.ly/Matt7:25) · [MRK 9:25](https://ref.ly/Matt9:25) · [LUK 4:36](https://ref.ly/Mark4:36) · [LUK 6:18](https://ref.ly/Mark6:18) · [LUK 8:29](https://ref.ly/Mark8:29) · [LUK 9:42](https://ref.ly/Mark9:42) · [LUK 11:24](https://ref.ly/Mark11:24) · [ACT 5:16](https://ref.ly/John5:16) · [ACT 8:7](https://ref.ly/John8:7) · [REV 16:13](https://ref.ly/Jude16:13) · [REV 18:2](https://ref.ly/Jude18:2)

12\.38 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

**Collocation:** πνεῦμα πονηρόν

demon, evil spirit (a fixed phrase equivalent in reference to πνεῦμα\[c] ‘demon,’ [12\.37](#12.37), but with specific emphasis upon evil) a supernatural evil being There are a number of translational problems involved in rendering ‘evil spirits.’ In the first place, one must make certain that any characterization of ‘spirits’ as ‘wicked’ or ‘evil’ or ‘unclean’ will not be misunderstood as unduly restrictive. For example, if the term for ‘spirits’ itself indicates an evil supernatural being, then ‘evil spirits’ might suggest that there were both ‘evil demons’ as well as ‘good demons.’ The same would be true of an expression such as ‘unclean spirits.’

[LUK 7:21](https://ref.ly/Mark7:21) · [LUK 8:2](https://ref.ly/Mark8:2) · [LUK 11:26](https://ref.ly/Mark11:26)

25\.73 · Attitudes and Emotions · Be Eager, Be Earnest, In a Devoted Manner

**Collocation:** ζέω τῷ πνεύματι

to show enthusiasm, to commit oneself completely to (an idiom, literally: to boil in the spirit) to show great eagerness toward something In some languages it may be difficult to find an appropriate equivalent of the phrase ‘with great enthusiasm,’ but in some instances an equivalent expression may be ‘he showed how much he liked to do’ or ‘he showed how he did so with all his heart.’

[ACT 18:25](https://ref.ly/John18:25) · [ROM 12:11](https://ref.ly/Acts12:11)

23\.110 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

**Collocation:** παραδίδωμι τὸ πνεῦμα

to die (an idiom, literally: to give over the spirit) to die, with the possible implication of a willing or voluntary act

[MAT 27:50](https://ref.ly/Matt27:50) · [JHN 19:30](https://ref.ly/Luke19:30)

88\.57 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Humility

**Collocation:** πτωχὸς τῷ πνεύματι

to be humble (an idiom, literally: poor in spirit) pertaining to one who is humble with regard to his own capacities (in the one NT occurrence, namely, [MAT 5:3](https://ref.ly/Matt5:3), this humility is in relationship to God) A literal translation of πτωχὸς τῷ πνεύματι may lead to serious misunderstanding, since ‘poor in spirit’ is likely to mean either ‘lacking in the Holy Spirit’ or ‘lacking in ambition or drive.’ In order to indicate clearly that this poverty or need is related in some way to spiritual realities, one may translate ‘happy are those who recognize their need of God.’

[MAT 5:3](https://ref.ly/Matt5:3)

30\.76 · Think · To Decide, To Conclude

**Collocation:** τίθεμαι ἐν τῷ πνεύματι

to make up one’s mind, to decide (an idiom, literally: to place in the mind) to engage in the process of deciding It is also possible to interpret the phrase ἐν τῷ πνεύματι in [ACT 19:21](https://ref.ly/John19:21) as being a reference to the Holy Spirit and accordingly, the passage may be translated as ‘Paul, led by the Spirit, decided to travel through Macedonia.’

[ACT 19:21](https://ref.ly/John19:21)

## πνευματικός (id: 004055000000000)

πνευματικός
===========

πνευματικός (G4152)

noun \| \-οῦ \| πνέω

12\.20 · ((οῦ m)) · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

one who is spiritual, one who has received the Spirit (derivative of πνεῦμα\[a] ‘Spirit,’ [12\.18](#12.18)) one who has received God’s Spirit and presumably lives in accordance with this relationship In a number of languages it is not possible to speak of ‘possessing the Spirit,’ but one can often say ‘to be possessed by the Spirit’ or ‘those whom the Spirit possesses’ or ‘... owns’ or ‘... controls.’

[1CO 2:13](https://ref.ly/Rom2:13) · [1CO 2:15](https://ref.ly/Rom2:15) · [1CO 14:37](https://ref.ly/Rom14:37) · [GAL 6:1](https://ref.ly/2Cor6:1)

adj \| \-ή, όν \| πνέω

12\.21 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

spiritual, from the Spirit (in reference to such matters as gifts, benefits, teachings, blessings, and religious songs) (derivative of πνεῦμα\[a] ‘Spirit,’ [12\.18](#12.18)) pertaining to being derived from or being about the Spirit, in reference to such matters as gifts, benefits, teachings, blessings, and religious songs In [1CO 2:13](https://ref.ly/Rom2:13) πνευματικά may perhaps be best rendered in some languages as ‘truths revealed by the Spirit’ or possibly ‘truths about the Spirit.’ Some persons would insist, however, that in [1CO 2:13](https://ref.ly/Rom2:13) πνευματικά refers to teachings which are of particular benefit or relevance to peoples spirits.  
The occurrence of πνευματικός in [EPH 1:3](https://ref.ly/Gal1:3) and [ROM 1:11](https://ref.ly/Acts1:11) could be understood as referring to the human spirit, but the contexts would seem to to point more clearly to the involvement of the Spirit of God. See [26\.10](#26.10).  
It is also possible that in [EPH 5:19](https://ref.ly/Gal5:19) πνευματικαῖς means merely ‘spiritual,’ and as such pertains to what is ‘sacred’ or ‘religious.’ One can, therefore, translate ᾠδαῖς πνευματικαῖς as ‘songs used in worship’ or ‘songs used in worship of God.’ For a discussion of certain related problems involving the rendering of πνευματικός, see [26\.10](#26.10).

[ROM 1:11](https://ref.ly/Acts1:11) · [ROM 15:27](https://ref.ly/Acts15:27) · [1CO 2:13](https://ref.ly/Rom2:13) · [1CO 12:1](https://ref.ly/Rom12:1) · [1CO 14:1](https://ref.ly/Rom14:1) · [EPH 1:3](https://ref.ly/Gal1:3) · [EPH 5:19](https://ref.ly/Gal5:19) · [COL 1:9](https://ref.ly/Phil1:9)

26\.10 · Psychological Faculties

spiritual, of the spirit, on a spiritual basis, in a spiritual manner pertaining to the spiritual nature or being of a person It is also possible to interpret πνευματικός in [EPH 1:3](https://ref.ly/Gal1:3) as referring to ‘the Holy Spirit,’ that is to say, these blessings would be those which come from the Spirit of God (see [12\.21](#12.21)).  
In [ROM 15:27](https://ref.ly/Acts15:27) (εἰ γὰρ τοῖς πνευματικοῖς αὐτῶν ἐκοινώνησαν τὰ ἔθνη, ὀφείλουσιν καὶ ἐν τοῖς σαρκικοῖς λειτουργῆσαι αὐτοῖς), πνευματικός stands in contrast to σαρκικός\[b] ‘material’ ([79\.1](#79.1)) and may therefore focus upon non\-material or spiritual aspects of human personality or life in contrast with the physical aspects. Accordingly, one may translate [ROM 15:27](https://ref.ly/Acts15:27) as ‘if the Gentiles shared in their spiritual benefits, they ought to help them with material things.’ It is also possible, however, to understand πνευματικός in [ROM 15:27](https://ref.ly/Acts15:27) as referring to those blessings which come from the Holy Spirit (see [12\.21](#12.21)) rather than those blessings which are for the human spirit. The terms πνευματικός, ψυχικός, σαρκικός, and σάρκινος reflect considerable overlapping in meaning; see also [26\.8](#26.8) and [79\.1](#79.1)\-[79\.6](#79.6).

[ROM 15:27](https://ref.ly/Acts15:27) · [1CO 9:11](https://ref.ly/Rom9:11) · [EPH 1:3](https://ref.ly/Gal1:3) · [EPH 5:19](https://ref.ly/Gal5:19) · [COL 3:16](https://ref.ly/Phil3:16)

41\.40 · Behavior and Related States · Particular Patterns of Behavior

spiritual, of spiritual conduct, guided by the Spirit (derivative of πνεῦμα\[a] ‘Spirit of God,’ [12\.18](#12.18)) pertaining to a pattern of life controlled or directed by God’s Spirit

[1CO 3:1](https://ref.ly/Rom3:1)1

79\.3 · Features of Objects · Physical \[Material], Spiritual

not physical, not material, spiritual pertaining to not being physical In some languages the concept of ‘spiritual body’ can only be expressed negatively as ‘the body will not have flesh and bones’ or ‘the body will not be a regular body.’

[1CO 15:44](https://ref.ly/Rom15:44) · [1CO 15:44](https://ref.ly/Rom15:44) · [1CO 15:46](https://ref.ly/Rom15:46) · [1CO 15:46](https://ref.ly/Rom15:46) · [1PE 2:5](https://ref.ly/Jas2:5) · [1PE 2:5](https://ref.ly/Jas2:5)

79\.6 · Features of Objects · Natural \[Human], Spiritual

spiritual, supernatural pertaining to being supernatural and having its ultimate source in God In a number of languages this meaning of πνευματικός can only be reflected by a phrase such as ‘which comes from God’ or ‘provided by God.’

[ROM 7:14](https://ref.ly/Acts7:14) · [1CO 10:3](https://ref.ly/Rom10:3) · [1CO 10:4](https://ref.ly/Rom10:4) · [1CO 10:4](https://ref.ly/Rom10:4)

12\.44 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Powers

**Collocation:** τὰ πνευματικὰ τῆς πονηρίας ἐν τοῖς ἐπουρανίοις

power, authority, lordship, ruler, wicked force (title for supernatural forces and powers) a supernatural power having some particular role in controlling the destiny and activities of human beings Some scholars have believed that it is possible to reconstruct at least in part some of the hierarchy represented by these various supernatural forces and powers, on the basis of the neoplatonic system of nine such powers arranged in three orders of three each. NT terminology and usage does not, however, lend itself to such a classification, and it is difficult, if not impossible, to determine what are the significant differences between these supernatural powers and forces.  
For the most part, translators have endeavored to render the various terms in somewhat different ways, for example, ἐξουσία as ‘authority’; δύναμις as ‘power’; κυριότης as ‘ruler’; and phrases such as τὰ πνευματικὰ τῆς πονηρίας ἐν τοῖς ἐπουρανίοις as ‘wicked spiritual forces in the heavenly world.’ In a number of languages, however, there are simply not enough terms to designate various kinds of powers, authorities, and forces which could be employed in speaking of such supernatural impersonal powers. Accordingly, some translators have simply used a phrase such as ‘any and all kinds of supernatural powers.’ Where it is possible to make certain lexical distinctions between terms and phrases, this, of course, should be done, but in a number of contexts this may be impossible, and accordingly, some explanation in a marginal note may be required if the reader is to understand satisfactorily the intent of the passages in question.

[EPH 6:12](https://ref.ly/Gal6:12)

1 1CO 3:1 It is possible that πνευματικός\[c] should be regarded as identical with πνευματικός\[a] ([12\.21](#12.21)), but in [1CO 3:1](https://ref.ly/Rom3:1) the focus seems to be upon the pattern of behavior rather than upon the state of having received God’s Spirit.

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Corinthians 2:13; 1 Corinthians 2:15; 1 Corinthians 14:37; Galatians 6:1; Romans 1:11; Romans 15:27; 1 Corinthians 12:1; 1 Corinthians 14:1; Ephesians 1:3; Ephesians 5:19; Colossians 1:9; 1 Corinthians 9:11; Colossians 3:16; 1 Corinthians 3:1; 1 Corinthians 15:44; 1 Corinthians 15:46; 1 Peter 2:5; Romans 7:14; 1 Corinthians 10:3; 1 Corinthians 10:4; Ephesians 6:12


## πνευματικῶς (id: 004056000000000)

πνευματικῶς
===========

πνευματικῶς (G4153)

adv \| πνέω

12\.21 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

spiritual, from the Spirit (derivative of πνεῦμα\[a] ‘Spirit,’ [12\.18](#12.18)) pertaining to being derived from or being about the Spirit, in reference to such matters as gifts, benefits, teachings, blessings, and religious songs The reference in [1CO 2:14](https://ref.ly/Rom2:14) may be interpreted to mean that the unspiritual person cannot receive the gifts of the Spirit; neither can such a person understand them because they can only be judged or evaluated on the basis of their being derived from the Spirit of God. For another interpretation of πνευματικῶς in [1CO 2:14](https://ref.ly/Rom2:14), see [26\.10](#26.10).

[1CO 2:14](https://ref.ly/Rom2:14)

26\.10 · Psychological Faculties

spiritual, of the spirit, on a spiritual basis, in a spiritual manner pertaining to the spiritual nature or being of a person For another interpretation of πνευματικῶς in [1CO 2:14](https://ref.ly/Rom2:14), see [12\.21](#12.21).

[1CO 2:14](https://ref.ly/Rom2:14)

33\.17 · Communication · Discourse Types

symbolically, allegorically, figuratively, so to speak pertaining to expressions which are not to be understood literally, but symbolically or figuratively In a number of languages it may be more satisfactory to translate ἐν παραβολῇ and πνευματικῶς\[c] by a negative expression, for example, ‘not literally’ or ‘not really.’ The expression in [REV 11:8](https://ref.ly/Jude11:8) might be translated as ‘that city might be called Sodom, but it isn’t really Sodom.’

[REV 11:8](https://ref.ly/Jude11:8)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Corinthians 2:14; Revelation 11:8


## πνέω (id: 004057000000000)

πνέω
====

πνέω (G4154)

aor. ἔπνευσα \| ἐκπνέω · ἐμπνέω · θεόπνευστος · πνεῦμα · πνευματικός · πνευματικῶς · πνοή · ὑποπνέω

14\.4 · Physical Events and States · Wind

wind, blowing, to blow air in relatively rapid movement, but without specification as to the force of the movement As in the case of English (which has, for example, such terms as breeze, zephyr, gust, storm, whirlwind, cyclone, tornado, etc.), other languages likewise normally have a number of different terms depending upon the strength of the wind and whether the wind may be carrying substances such as sand or dust. In some languages there may also be a problem involved in the use of terms for ‘wind,’ since some of these may have meanings of malicious or malevolent spirits, or they may imply a disease\-bringing event.

[MAT 7:25](https://ref.ly/Matt7:25) · [MAT 7:27](https://ref.ly/Matt7:27) · [LUK 12:55](https://ref.ly/Mark12:55) · [JHN 3:8](https://ref.ly/Luke3:8) · [JHN 6:18](https://ref.ly/Luke6:18) · [ACT 27:40](https://ref.ly/John27:40) · [REV 7:1](https://ref.ly/Jude7:1)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 7:25; Matthew 7:27; Luke 12:55; John 3:8; John 6:18; Acts 27:40; Revelation 7:1


## πνίγω (id: 004058000000000)

πνίγω
=====

πνίγω (G4155) — πνίγομαι

ἀποπνίγω · πνικτός · συμπνίγω

19\.53 · Physical Impact · Press

to choke to apply pressure around the neck in order to harm or kill

[MAT 18:28](https://ref.ly/Matt18:28)

23\.120 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

to cause plants to die (figurative extension of meaning employing the base πνίγω\[a] ‘to choke,’ [19\.53](#19.53)) to cause the death of plants by other plants crowding them out and/or overshadowing them1

[MAT 13:7](https://ref.ly/Matt13:7)

23\.119 · (πνίγομαι) · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

to drown to die as the result of drowning ‎2

[MRK 5:13](https://ref.ly/Matt5:13)

1 It is possible that ἀποπνίγω and συμπνίγω\[a] are somewhat more intensive in meaning than πνίγω\[b], but this is difficult, if not impossible, to determine on the basis of NT usage. 2 It is possible that ἀποπνίγομαι differs from πνίγομαι in being somewhat more intensive in meaning, but this cannot be determined with accuracy on the basis of existing contexts.

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 18:28; Matthew 13:7; Mark 5:13

πνικτός
=======

πνικτός (G4156)

adj \| \-ή, \-όν \| πνίγω

19\.54 · Physical Impact · Press

choked, strangled pertaining to being choked or strangled

[ACT 15:20](https://ref.ly/John15:20) · [ACT 15:29](https://ref.ly/John15:29) · [ACT 21:25](https://ref.ly/John21:25)

## πνοή (id: 004060000000000)

πνοή
====

πνοή (G4157)

noun \| \-ῆς \| πνέω

14\.4 · Physical Events and States · Wind

wind, blowing, to blow air in relatively rapid movement, but without specification as to the force of the movement As in the case of English (which has, for example, such terms as breeze, zephyr, gust, storm, whirlwind, cyclone, tornado, etc.), other languages likewise normally have a number of different terms depending upon the strength of the wind and whether the wind may be carrying substances such as sand or dust. In some languages there may also be a problem involved in the use of terms for ‘wind,’ since some of these may have meanings of malicious or malevolent spirits, or they may imply a disease\-bringing event.

[ACT 2:2](https://ref.ly/John2:2)

23\.187 · Physiological Processes and States · Breathe, Breath

breath, capacity to breathe the process of breathing Rather than rendering [ACT 17:25](https://ref.ly/John17:25) as ‘gives life and breath to all people,’ it may be better to say ‘causes all people to live and breathe.’ In some languages, however, ‘to breathe’ is synonymous with ‘life,’ and therefore to say ‘to live and to breathe’ is to be both redundant and repetitious. One may therefore translate ‘who causes all people to live.’

[ACT 17:25](https://ref.ly/John17:25)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 2:2; Acts 17:25

to make, to fashion to produce something new, with the implication of using materials already in existence (in contrast with κτίζω ‘to create,’ [42\.35](#42.35))

57\.189 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Earn, Gain, Do Business

to earn, to gain, to make a profit to gain by means of one’s activity or investment In many languages, it may be quite impossible to speak of ‘one mina has earned five minas’ or even ‘one coin has earned five coins’ ([LUK 19:18](https://ref.ly/Mark19:18)). It may be necessary to turn this into an expression involving a human agent, for example, ‘I have earned five minas with the one that you gave me.’

to bear fruit, to produce fruit, to produce seed, to yield (an idiom, literally to make fruit) to produce fruit or seed (of plants)3

[MAT 3:10](https://ref.ly/Matt3:10) · [MAT 7:17](https://ref.ly/Matt7:17) · [MAT 7:17](https://ref.ly/Matt7:17) · [MAT 7:18](https://ref.ly/Matt7:18) · [MAT 7:18](https://ref.ly/Matt7:18) · [MAT 7:19](https://ref.ly/Matt7:19) · [MAT 13:26](https://ref.ly/Matt13:26) · [LUK 3:9](https://ref.ly/Mark3:9) · [LUK 6:43](https://ref.ly/Mark6:43) · [LUK 6:43](https://ref.ly/Mark6:43) · [LUK 8:8](https://ref.ly/Mark8:8) · [LUK 13:9](https://ref.ly/Mark13:9)

25\.96 · Attitudes and Emotions · Acceptable To, To Be Pleased With

**Collocation:** ποιέω τὸ ἱκανόν

to act in a pleasing manner, to please (an idiom, literally: to do what is enough) to cause someone to be pleased by doing what will satisfy

[MRK 15:15](https://ref.ly/Matt15:15)

1 In [ROM 7:15](https://ref.ly/Acts7:15) πράσσω ([42\.8](#42.8)) and ποιέω seem to be semantically interchangeable, but there may be some subtle stylistic distinction. 2 Any meaning involving ‘to cause to be’ implies some change in state, and therefore one could classify these meanings under Subdomain B Change of State (Domain 13 Be, Become, Exist, Happen). However, the focus seems to be primarily upon the changed state rather than upon the process of moving from one state to another. The fact that a change of state has taken place seems to be semantically incidental. 3 By setting up certain highly generic meanings for βλαστάνω, δίδωμι, ἀποδίδωμι, and ποιέω, it would be possible to treat the phrase compounds in this series as being semantically endocentric. However, it seems more satisfactory to regard them as what might be called low\-grade idioms with specialized meanings of the semantically head expressions.

## ποιμαίνω (id: 004068000000000)

ποιμαίνω
========

ποιμαίνω (G4165)

fut. ποιμανῶ · aor.impv.2pl. ποιμάνατε \| ἀρχιποίμην · ποιμήν · ποίμνη · ποίμνιον

44\.3 · Animal Husbandry, Fishing

to shepherd, to take care of, to tend, to pasture to herd and tend flocks of sheep or goats In a number of cultures sheep are not herded or taken care of; they serve primarily as scavengers, and therefore it may be necessary to introduce some type of marginal note, especially in passages which have figurative significance.

[LUK 17:7](https://ref.ly/Mark17:7) · [JHN 21:16](https://ref.ly/Luke21:16) · [1CO 9:7](https://ref.ly/Rom9:7) · [1PE 5:2](https://ref.ly/Jas5:2)

36\.2 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Guide, Lead

to guide and to help, to guide and take care of (a figurative extension of meaning of ποιμαίνω\[a] ‘to shepherd,’ [44\.3](#44.3)) to lead, with the implication of providing for

[MAT 2:6](https://ref.ly/Matt2:6) · [ACT 20:28](https://ref.ly/John20:28) · [JUD 1:12](https://ref.ly/3John1:12) · [REV 7:17](https://ref.ly/Jude7:17)

37\.57 · Control, Rule · Rule, Govern

to rule, to govern to rule, with the implication of direct personal involvement

[REV 2:27](https://ref.ly/Jude2:27) · [REV 12:5](https://ref.ly/Jude12:5) · [REV 19:15](https://ref.ly/Jude19:15)

## ποιμήν (id: 004069000000000)

ποιμήν
======

ποιμήν (G4166)

noun \| \-ένος \| ποιμαίνω

44\.4 · Animal Husbandry, Fishing

shepherd (derivative of ποιμαίνω\[a] ‘to take care of sheep or goats,’ [44\.3](#44.3)) one who takes care of sheep or goats

[MAT 9:36](https://ref.ly/Matt9:36) · [MAT 25:32](https://ref.ly/Matt25:32) · [MAT 26:31](https://ref.ly/Matt26:31) · [MRK 6:34](https://ref.ly/Matt6:34) · [MRK 14:27](https://ref.ly/Matt14:27) · [LUK 2:8](https://ref.ly/Mark2:8) · [LUK 2:15](https://ref.ly/Mark2:15) · [LUK 2:18](https://ref.ly/Mark2:18) · [LUK 2:20](https://ref.ly/Mark2:20) · [JHN 10:2](https://ref.ly/Luke10:2) · [HEB 13:20](https://ref.ly/Phlm13:20) · [1PE 2:25](https://ref.ly/Jas2:25)

53\.72 · Religious Activities · Roles and Functions

pastor, minister (a figurative extension of meaning of ποιμήν\[a] ‘shepherd,’ [44\.4](#44.4)) one who is responsible for the care and guidance of a Christian congregation Note that in [EPH 4:11](https://ref.ly/Gal4:11) there are four classes of persons, not five, for the last class involves two complementary roles, that of pastor and teacher, in other words, to guide and help a congregation as well as to teach.

[JHN 10:11](https://ref.ly/Luke10:11) · [JHN 10:11](https://ref.ly/Luke10:11) · [JHN 10:12](https://ref.ly/Luke10:12) · [JHN 10:14](https://ref.ly/Luke10:14) · [JHN 10:16](https://ref.ly/Luke10:16) · [EPH 4:11](https://ref.ly/Gal4:11)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 9:36; Matthew 25:32; Matthew 26:31; Mark 6:34; Mark 14:27; Luke 2:8; Luke 2:15; Luke 2:18; Luke 2:20; John 10:2; Hebrews 13:20; 1 Peter 2:25; John 10:11; John 10:12; John 10:14; John 10:16; Ephesians 4:11


## ποίμνη (id: 004070000000000)

ποίμνη
======

ποίμνη (G4167)

noun \| \-ης \| ποιμαίνω

4\.28 · Animals · Animals

flock a collective for a group of sheep πρόβατον and/or goats αἴγειος In instances in which the reference is to a flock of sheep or goats, one can normally employ a designation for any group of animals, though there are often distinct terms for groups of domesticated animals in contrast with groups of wild animals. And in many languages there are a number of different terms for such groupings of animals depending upon the particular animals in question, for example, in English swarm, school, drove, pack, and bevy.

[MAT 26:31](https://ref.ly/Matt26:31) · [LUK 2:8](https://ref.ly/Mark2:8) · [1CO 9:7](https://ref.ly/Rom9:7) · [1CO 9:7](https://ref.ly/Rom9:7)

11\.31 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

people who are like a flock (a figurative extension of meaning of ποίμνη\[a] ‘flock,’ [4\.28](#4.28)) the followers of Christ constituting a well\-defined membership group It is often impossible to reproduce the figurative values of ἀρνίον\[b] ([11\.29](#11.29)), πρόβατον\[b] ([11\.30](#11.30)), ποίμνιον, and ποίμνη\[b], since such metaphors cannot be employed in areas of the world in which sheep serve primarily as scavengers around villages (something which is generally true in many parts of the tropics). It may be possible in some instances to preserve something of the metaphorical value of these four terms by translating in some such manner as ‘take care of my people as you would sheep’ or ‘take care of my people, who are like sheep,’ or ‘take care of my helpless people,’ but frequently one can only translate as ‘take care of my people.’

[JHN 10:16](https://ref.ly/Luke10:16)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 26:31; Luke 2:8; 1 Corinthians 9:7; John 10:16


## ποίμνιον (id: 004071000000000)

ποίμνιον
========

ποίμνιον (G4168)

noun \| \-ου \| ποιμαίνω

11\.31 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Religious

people who are like a flock (a figurative extension of meaning of ποίμνιον ‘flock,’ not occurring in the NT) the followers of Christ constituting a well\-defined membership group It is often impossible to reproduce the figurative values of ἀρνίον\[b] ([11\.29](#11.29)), πρόβατον\[b] ([11\.30](#11.30)), ποίμνιον, and ποίμνη\[b], , since such metaphors cannot be employed in areas of the world in which sheep serve primarily as scavengers around villages (something which is generally true in many parts of the tropics). It may be possible in some instances to preserve something of the metaphorical value of these four terms by translating in some such manner as ‘take care of my people as you would sheep’ or ‘take care of my people, who are like sheep,’ or ‘take care of my helpless people,’ but frequently one can only translate as ‘take care of my people.’

[LUK 12:32](https://ref.ly/Mark12:32) · [ACT 20:28](https://ref.ly/John20:28) · [ACT 20:29](https://ref.ly/John20:29) · [1PE 5:2](https://ref.ly/Jas5:2) · [1PE 5:3](https://ref.ly/Jas5:3)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 12:32; Acts 20:28; Acts 20:29; 1 Peter 5:2; 1 Peter 5:3

4:13](https://ref.ly/Heb4:13)

1\.89 · Geographical Objects and Features · Population Centers

city a population center of relatively greater importance (in contrast with κώμη\[a] ‘village,’ [1\.92](#1.92), and κωμόπολις ‘town,’ [1\.91](#1.91)), due to its size, economic significance, or political control over a surrounding area (it is possible that fortification of walls and gates also entered into the system of classification of a πόλις\[b], in contrast with other terms for population centers) Most languages spoken by people in urbanized and industrialized societies have a number of terms for different types of population centers, for example, metropolis, city, town, village, hamlet, etc. For so\-called tribal languages the primary means for making gradations consists of added attributives, for example, ‘a very large village,’ ‘a large village,’ ‘a village,’ ‘a small village.’ A translator must simply attempt to be as consistent as possible in referring to different types of population centers mentioned in the Scriptures.

22:14](https://ref.ly/Jude22:14) · [REV 22:19](https://ref.ly/Jude22:19)

11\.61 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Political

city, inhabitants of a city the inhabitants of a city2

[MAT 8:34](https://ref.ly/Matt8:34) · [MAT 12:25](https://ref.ly/Matt12:25) · [MAT 21:10](https://ref.ly/Matt21:10) · [MRK 1:33](https://ref.ly/Matt1:33) · [MRK 5:14](https://ref.ly/Matt5:14) · [LUK 4:43](https://ref.ly/Mark4:43) · [LUK 8:34](https://ref.ly/Mark8:34) · [ACT 13:44](https://ref.ly/John13:44) · [ACT 14:4](https://ref.ly/John14:4) · [ACT 17:5](https://ref.ly/John17:5) · [ACT 21:30](https://ref.ly/John21:30) · [2PE 2:6](https://ref.ly/1Pet2:6) · [JUD 1:7](https://ref.ly/3John1:7)

1 In contrast with [MAT 11:21](https://ref.ly/Matt11:21) in which Bethsaida is spoken of as a πόλις, in [MRK 8:23](https://ref.ly/Matt8:23) and [MRK 8:26](https://ref.ly/Matt8:26) Bethsaida is specifically referred to as a κώμη ([1\.92](#1.92)). This diversity may simply reflect a difference of usage between two authors. A similar distinction, however, exists also in the case of Bethlehem. In [JHN 7:42](https://ref.ly/Luke7:42) Bethlehem is called a κώμη, and in [LUK 2:4](https://ref.ly/Mark2:4), a πόλις. These differences can probably be attributed to differences in individual usage; that is to say, the distinctions in size between a πόλις and a κώμη were not fixed, or one author used πόλις in the generic sence in contrast with the specific sense. This does not affect the generic meaning of πόλις\[a] as a population center in contrast with a rural area. 2 It is possible that in the case of such meanings as τόπος\[f] ([11\.59](#11.59)), πόλις\[c] ([11\.61](#11.61)), κώμη\[b] ([11\.62](#11.62)), and χώρα\[e] ([11\.64](#11.64)) one should count on certain more or less automatic semantic rules by which the meaning of inhabitants could be derived by metonymy from expressions of place. Since, however, such metonymic developments are far from universal, the distinct meanings are listed here as a practical guide to translators. The same type of problem occurs with Ιεροσόλυμα ‘Jerusalem,’ Ἰουδαία ‘Judea,’ and περίχωρος ‘region’ in [MAT 3:5](https://ref.ly/Matt3:5).

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 2:23; Matthew 8:33; Matthew 9:1; Matthew 10:5; Matthew 10:11; Matthew 10:14; Matthew 10:15; Matthew 10:23; Matthew 11:1; Matthew 11:20; Matthew 14:13; Mark 1:45; Mark 6:33; ## πολιτάρχης (id: 004076000000000)

πολιτάρχης
==========

πολιτάρχης (G4173)

noun \| \-ου \| πολιτεύομαι \+ ἄρχω

37\.93 · Control, Rule · Rule, Govern

city official a public official responsible for administrative matters within a town or city and a member of the ruling council of such a political unit

[ACT 17:6](https://ref.ly/John17:6) · [ACT 17:8](https://ref.ly/John17:8)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 17:6; Acts 17:8


## πολιτεία (id: 004077000000000)

πολιτεία
========

πολιτεία (G4174)

noun \| \-ας \| πολιτεύομαι

11\.70 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Political

citizenship the right to be a citizen of a particular socio\-political entity (see [11\.67](#11.67) and [11\.68](#11.68)) In some languages [ACT 22:28](https://ref.ly/John22:28) may be rendered as ‘I obtained the right to be counted as a citizen’ or ‘I became a person who belongs to that nation.’

[ACT 22:28](https://ref.ly/John22:28)

11\.67 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Political

state, people a group of people constituting a socio\-political unit It is possible that in [EPH 2:12](https://ref.ly/Gal2:12) the use of πολιτεία suggests not merely a socio\-political but also a socio\-religious grouping. Such a conclusion would, in a sense, be true of any and all designations of a Jewish constituency, since religion and ethnic identification were so inextricably bound together.

[EPH 2:12](https://ref.ly/Gal2:12)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 22:28; Ephesians 2:12

πολίτευμα
=========

πολίτευμα (G4175)

noun \| \-τος \| πολιτεύομαι

11\.71 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Political

state, commonwealth, place of citizenship the place or location in which one has the right to be a citizen

[PHP 3:20](https://ref.ly/Eph3:20)

* **Associated Passages:** Philippians 3:20


## πολιτεύομαι (id: 004079000000000)

πολιτεύομαι
===========

πολιτεύομαι (G4176)

κωμόπολις · πόλις · πολιτάρχης · πολιτεία · πολίτευμα · πολίτης · συμπολίτης

41\.34 · Behavior and Related States · Particular Patterns of Behavior

to live, to conduct one’s life, to live in relation to others to conduct oneself with proper reference to one’s obligations in relationship to others, as part of some community

[ACT 23:1](https://ref.ly/John23:1) · [PHP 1:27](https://ref.ly/Eph1:27)

* **Associated Passages:** Acts 23:1; Philippians 1:27


## πολίτης (id: 004080000000000)

πολίτης
=======

πολίτης (G4177)

noun \| \-ου \| πολιτεύομαι

11\.68 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio\-Political

citizen a person having full status as a member of a socio\-political unit of people In a number of languages a citizen may be described as ‘a person who belongs to that country’ or ‘one who is a part of the people of that country’ or ‘one who is counted as a person of that country,’ but one could be a citizen of a country without necessarily living in such a geographical area.  
In [HEB 8:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm8:11) (καὶ οὐ μὴ διδάξωσιν ἕκαστος τὸν πολίτην αὐτοῦ ‘none of them will have to teach his fellow citizen’) πολίτης occurs with the possessive pronoun αὐτοῦ and hence is therefore equivalent to συμπολίτης ([11\.72](#11.72)) in meaning.

[LUK 15:15](https://ref.ly/Mark15:15) · [LUK 19:14](https://ref.ly/Mark19:14) · [ACT 21:39](https://ref.ly/John21:39) · [HEB 8:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm8:11)

* **Associated Passages:** Luke 15:15; Luke 19:14; Acts 21:39; Hebrews 8:11


## πολλάκις (id: 004081000000000)

πολλάκις
========

πολλάκις (G4178)

adv \| πολύς

67\.11 · Time · A Point of Time without Reference to Other Points of Time: Time, Occasion, Ever, Often

often, many times a number of related points of time

[MAT 17:15](https://ref.ly/Matt17:15) · [MAT 17:15](https://ref.ly/Matt17:15) · [MRK 5:4](https://ref.ly/Matt5:4) · [MRK 9:22](https://ref.ly/Matt9:22) · [JHN 18:2](https://ref.ly/Luke18:2) · [ACT 26:11](https://ref.ly/John26:11) · [ROM 1:13](https://ref.ly/Acts1:13) · [2CO 8:22](https://ref.ly/1Cor8:22) · [2CO 11:23](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:23) · [2CO 11:26](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:26) · [2CO 11:27](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:27) · [2CO 11:27](https://ref.ly/1Cor11:27) · [PHP 3:18](https://ref.ly/Eph3:18) · [2TI 1:16](https://ref.ly/1Tim1:16) · [HEB 6:7](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:7) · [HEB 9:25](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:25) · [HEB 9:26](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:26) · [HEB 10:11](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:11)

## πολυλογία (id: 004083000000000)

=========

## πολυμερῶς (id: 004084000000000)

πολυμερῶς
=========

πολυμερῶς (G4181)

adv \| πολύς \+ μερίζω

63\.19 · Whole, Unite, Part, Divide · Part

fragmentary, in many parts pertaining to that which occurs in many parts It is also possible to interpret πολυμερῶς in [HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1) as referring to many different occasions, and therefore it may be translated as ‘often’ or ‘many times’ ([67\.11](#67.11)). For yet another interpretation of πολυμερῶς in [HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1), see [89\.81](#89.81).

[HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1)

67\.11 · Time · A Point of Time without Reference to Other Points of Time: Time, Occasion, Ever, Often

often, many times a number of related points of time It is also possible to understand πολυμερῶς\[a] ([58\.29](#58.29)). Some scholars, however, understand πολυμερῶς in the sense of ‘fragmentary’ (see [63\.19](#63.19)). For yet another interpretation of πολυμερῶς in [HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1), see [89\.81](#89.81).

[HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1)

89\.81 · Relations · Manner

in many ways, in many different ways pertaining to a number of different manners in which something may be done In the case of this meaning of πολυμερῶς, the terms πολυτρόπως (see [89\.82](#89.82)) and πολυμερῶς may be regarded as mutually reinforcing, but for different interpretations of πολυμερῶς in [HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1), see [63\.19](#63.19) and [67\.11](#67.11).

[HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1)

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 1:1

4:13](https://ref.ly/Phil4:13) · [1TH 1:5](https://ref.ly/Col1:5) · [1TH 1:6](https://ref.ly/Col1:6) · [1TH 2:2](https://ref.ly/Col2:2) · [1TH 2:17](https://ref.ly/Col2:17) · [JAS 3:2](https://ref.ly/Heb3:2)

78\.3 · Degree · Much, Little \[Positive\-Negative Degree]

great, greatly, much, a great deal the upper range of a scale of extent, but probably somewhat less than for μέγας\[b], μεγάλως, and μέγεθος ([78\.2](#78.2)) In [MRK 3:12](https://ref.ly/Matt3:12) πολλά indicates only degree, but it may be rendered in English as ‘sternly’ because part of the meaning of ἐπετίμα may be appropriately transferred to the adverbial attributive so as to express the extent and nature of the warning. In [ACT 21:40](https://ref.ly/John21:40) the phrase πολλῆς δὲ σιγῆς γενομένης refers to the fact that the crowd became very quiet. πολλῆς clearly indicates the degree of quietness, though in many languages one cannot speak of something being ‘very quiet’; on the contrary, such a state can only be described in terms of the absence of noise, for example, ‘when nobody was saying anything.’

59\.8 · Quantity · Many, Few \[Countables]

**Collocation:** τὸ πλεῖστον

the most, not more than the largest number of objects or events possible under the circumstances

[1CO 14:27](https://ref.ly/Rom14:27)

78\.28 · Degree · More Than, Less Than \[Comparative Degree]

**Collocation:** πλείων

more, more than, to a greater degree, even more a degree which surpasses in some manner a point on an explicit or implicit scale of extent1

[MAT 5:20](https://ref.ly/Matt5:20) · [MAT 6:25](https://ref.ly/Matt6:25) · [MAT 12:41](https://ref.ly/Matt12:41) · [MAT 12:42](https://ref.ly/Matt12:42) · [MRK 12:43](https://ref.ly/Matt12:43) · [LUK 3:13](https://ref.ly/Mark3:13) · [LUK 7:42](https://ref.ly/Mark7:42) · [LUK 7:43](https://ref.ly/Mark7:43) · [LUK 11:31](https://ref.ly/Mark11:31) · [LUK 11:32](https://ref.ly/Mark11:32) · [LUK 12:23](https://ref.ly/Mark12:23) · [LUK 21:3](https://ref.ly/Mark21:3) · [JHN 21:15](https://ref.ly/Luke21:15) · [ACT 4:17](https://ref.ly/John4:17) · [ACT 15:28](https://ref.ly/John15:28) · [2TI 2:16](https://ref.ly/1Tim2:16) · [2TI 3:9](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:9)

66\.11 · Proper, Improper

**Collocation:** πλείων

more appropriate, better, more acceptable pertaining to being more appropriate or fitting

[HEB 11:4](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:4)

67\.11 · Time · A Point of Time without Reference to Other Points of Time: Time, Occasion, Ever, Often

**Collocation:** πολλά

often, many times a number of related points of time

[MAT 9:14](https://ref.ly/Matt9:14) · [MRK 5:10](https://ref.ly/Matt5:10) · [ROM 15:22](https://ref.ly/Acts15:22)

67\.89 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

**Collocation:** ἐπὶ πολύ

long, long time, for some time pertaining to a relatively long duration of time

[ACT 20:9](https://ref.ly/John20:9) · [ACT 28:6](https://ref.ly/John28:6)

1 It is possible that μείζων as an expression of degree differs somewhat in meaning from πλείων\[b], even as in the positive degree a distinction seems to occur between μέγας\[b] ([78\.2](#78.2)) and πολύς\[c] ([78\.3](#78.3)). In the comparative forms, however, it seems difficult to substantiate this type of distinction.

## πολύσπλαγχνος (id: 004087000000000)

πολύσπλαγχνος
=============

πολύσπλαγχνος (G4184)

adj \| \-ον \| πολύς \+ σπλαγχνίζομαι

25\.52 · Attitudes and Emotions · Love, Affection, Compassion

very compassionate, with much affection pertaining to great affection and compassion

[JAS 5:11](https://ref.ly/Heb5:11)

* **Associated Passages:** James 5:11

πολυτελής
=========

πολυτελής (G4185)

adj \| \-ές \| πολύς \+ τελέω

65\.3 · Value · Valuable, Lacking in Value

valuable, expensive pertaining to being of great value or worth, implying in some contexts a monetary scale

[MRK 14:3](https://ref.ly/Matt14:3) · [1TI 2:9](https://ref.ly/2Thess2:9) · [1PE 3:4](https://ref.ly/Jas3:4)

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 14:3; 1 Timothy 2:9; 1 Peter 3:4

πολύτιμος
=========

πολύτιμος (G4186)

adj \| \-ον \| πολύς \+ τιμάω

65\.3 · Value · Valuable, Lacking in Value

valuable, expensive pertaining to being of great value or worth, implying in some contexts a monetary scale

## πολυτρόπως (id: 004090000000000)

πολυτρόπως
==========

πολυτρόπως (G4187)

adv \| πολύς \+ τροπή

58\.29 · Nature, Class, Example · Class, Kind

in many ways pertaining to that which occurs in many kinds of ways For another interpretation of πολυτρόπως in [HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1), see [89\.82](#89.82).

[HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1)

89\.82 · Relations · Manner

in many ways pertaining to occurring in many ways or manners For another interpretation of πολυτρόπως in [HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1), see [58\.29](#58.29).

[HEB 1:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:1)

* **Associated Passages:** Hebrews 1:1


## πόμα (id: 004091000000000)

πόμα
====

πόμα (G4188)

noun \| \-τος \| πίνω

5\.6 · Foods and Condiments · Food

a drink liquids used for nourishment or to satisfy thirst Though in many languages the equivalent of πόσις and πόμα would be a verbal derivative meaning basically ‘that which is drunk,’ in other languages the equivalent may be ‘watery food’ or ‘liquid food.’  
In some languages, one cannot refer to ‘blood’ ([JHN 6:55](https://ref.ly/Luke6:55)) as being ‘drink,’ since blood is classified as food rather than as drink. On the other hand, it may be possible to translate ‘my blood is the real drink’ as ‘my blood is real nourishment.’ The expression ‘food and drink’ ([HEB 9:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:10)) may be better rendered in some languages as ‘all kinds of food.’

[1CO 10:4](https://ref.ly/Rom10:4) · [HEB 9:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:10)

* **Associated Passages:** 1 Corinthians 10:4; Hebrews 9:10


## πονηρία (id: 004092000000000)

πονηρία
=======

πονηρία (G4189) — πονηρίαι

noun \| \-ας \| πονηρός

88\.108 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Bad, Evil, Harmful, Damaging

wickedness an evil, wicked nature In a number of languages one can speak of ‘a wicked nature’ as ‘one who is bent on doing what is wicked’ or ‘one who habitually does what is wicked.’ For the total expression in [EPH 6:12](https://ref.ly/Gal6:12) as a title, see [12\.44](#12.44).

[MAT 22:18](https://ref.ly/Matt22:18) · [LUK 11:39](https://ref.ly/Mark11:39) · [ACT 3:26](https://ref.ly/John3:26) · [ROM 1:29](https://ref.ly/Acts1:29) · [1CO 5:8](https://ref.ly/Rom5:8) · [EPH 6:12](https://ref.ly/Gal6:12)

88\.109 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Bad, Evil, Harmful, Damaging

**Collocation:** πονηρίαι

wicked deeds, doing evil things deeds which are wicked and evil

[MRK 7:22](https://ref.ly/Matt7:22)

* **Associated Passages:** Matthew 22:18; Luke 11:39; Acts 3:26; Romans 1:29; 1 Corinthians 5:8; Ephesians 6:12; Mark 7:22


## πονηρός (id: 004093000000000)

πονηρός
=======

πονηρός (G4190)

adj \| \-ά, \-όν \| πονηρία

88\.110 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Bad, Evil, Harmful, Damaging

immoral, evil, wicked pertaining to being morally corrupt and evil In translating [MAT 5:11](https://ref.ly/Matt5:11) it is important to indicate that the words spoken are not evil in themselves, but the content of what is spoken involves attributing evil and wicked deeds to the followers of Jesus. It may, therefore, be necessary to say in some languages ‘when men speak against you by saying that you have done wicked deeds.’

1:21](https://ref.ly/Phil1:21) · [1TH 5:22](https://ref.ly/Col5:22) · [2TH 3:2](https://ref.ly/1Thess3:2) · [2TH 3:3](https://ref.ly/1Thess3:3) · [1TI 6:4](https://ref.ly/2Thess6:4) · [2TI 3:13](https://ref.ly/1Tim3:13) · [2TI 4:18](https://ref.ly/1Tim4:18) · [HEB 3:12](https://ref.ly/Phlm3:12) · [JAS 2:4](https://ref.ly/Heb2:4) · [JAS 4:16](https://ref.ly/Heb4:16) · [1JN 3:12](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:12) · [2JN 1:11](https://ref.ly/1John1:11) · [3JN 1:10](https://ref.ly/2John1:10)

65\.27 · Value · Good, Bad

bad, worthless pertaining to possessing a serious fault and consequently being worthless In a number of languages one must speak of ‘bad fruit’ in the context of [MAT 7:18](https://ref.ly/Matt7:18) as ‘fruit which does not taste good.’ It is often wrong to use an expression such as ‘rotten fruit,’ since a good tree may produce fruit which rots as a result of being overly ripe.

[MAT 7:17](https://ref.ly/Matt7:17) · [MAT 7:18](https://ref.ly/Matt7:18)

88\.314 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Sin, Wrongdoing, Guilt

guilty pertaining to guilt resulting from an evil deed In a number of languages it would be entirely misleading to speak of ‘a guilty conscience,’ for this would seem to imply that there is something sinful about the conscience itself. In reality, it is the conscience that says that a person is guilty, and therefore it may be necessary to translate [HEB 10:22](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:22) as ‘with hearts that have been purified from a condition in which their conscience has said that they are guilty.’

[HEB 10:22](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:22)

23\.149 · Physiological Processes and States · Sickness, Disease, Weakness

to be sick, to be diseased (a figurative extension of meaning of πονηρός\[a] ‘evil,’ [88\.110](#88.110)) a state of being sickly or diseased (in the NT with special reference to the eyes) For other interpretations of πονηρός with ὀφθαλμός, see [57\.108](#57.108) and [88\.165](#88.165). See also [23\.132](#23.132).

[MAT 6:23](https://ref.ly/Matt6:23) · [LUK 11:34](https://ref.ly/Mark11:34) · [REV 16:2](https://ref.ly/Jude16:2)

12\.35 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

**Collocation:** ὁ πονηρός

the Evil One, He who is evil (a title for the Devil, literally: the evil one) the one who is essentially evil or in a sense personifies evil

[MAT 6:13](https://ref.ly/Matt6:13) · [MAT 13:38](https://ref.ly/Matt13:38) · [JHN 17:15](https://ref.ly/Luke17:15) · [EPH 6:16](https://ref.ly/Gal6:16) · [2TH 3:3](https://ref.ly/1Thess3:3) · [1JN 2:13](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:13) · [1JN 2:14](https://ref.ly/2Pet2:14) · [1JN 3:12](https://ref.ly/2Pet3:12) · [1JN 5:18](https://ref.ly/2Pet5:18) · [1JN 5:19](https://ref.ly/2Pet5:19)

88\.165 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Envy, Jealousy

**Collocation:** ὀφθαλμὸς πονηρός

jealous(y) (an idiom, literally: evil eye) a feeling of jealousy and resentment because of what someone else has or does It is also possible to understand ὀφθαλμὸς πονηρός in [MAT 20:15](https://ref.ly/Matt20:15) as meaning ‘stinginess’ (see [57\.108](#57.108)). See also [23\.149](#23.149).

[MAT 20:15](https://ref.ly/Matt20:15) · [MRK 7:22](https://ref.ly/Matt7:22)

57\.108 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Give

**Collocation:** ὀφθαλμὸς πονηρός

stingy, miserly (an idiom, literally: evil eye) to be stingy It is also possible that ὀφθαλμὸς πονηρός in [MAT 20:15](https://ref.ly/Matt20:15) is to be understood in the sense of ‘jealous’ (see [88\.165](#88.165)). See also [23\.149](#23.149).

[MAT 6:23](https://ref.ly/Matt6:23) · [MAT 20:15](https://ref.ly/Matt20:15) · [MRK 7:22](https://ref.ly/Matt7:22) · [LUK 11:34](https://ref.ly/Mark11:34)

## πόνος (id: 004094000000000)

πόνος
=====

πόνος (G4192)

noun \| \-ου \| πένομαι (work for one's living, labor) · διαπονέομαι · καταπονέω · πένης · πενιχρός

24\.77 · Sensory Events and States · Pain, Suffering

pain, suffering the experience of pain, normally involving both continuity and intensity In a number of languages it may not be possible, or at least it may not be natural, to use nouns for ‘death,’ ‘grief,’ ‘crying,’ or ‘pain.’ Corresponding verb expressions may, however, be employed, for example, ‘people will no longer die, will no longer grieve, will no longer cry, and will no longer be in pain’ or ‘... suffer pain.’

[REV 16:10](https://ref.ly/Jude16:10) · [REV 16:11](https://ref.ly/Jude16:11) · [REV 21:4](https://ref.ly/Jude21:4)

42\.49 · Perform, Do · Work, Toil

hard work, burdensome labor hard work, implying accompanying pain and distress

[COL 4:13](https://ref.ly/Phil4:13)

## πόντος (id: 004097000000000)

πόντος
======

πόντος

\-ου

1\.73 · Geographical Objects and Features · Bodies of Water

open sea, high sea, the deep, ocean the relatively deep area of the sea or ocean sufficiently far from land as to be beyond the range of any protection from the seacoast In order to designate the high sea or the ocean, it may be necessary in some instances to speak of ‘the sea far from land’ or ‘the sea out of sight of land.’ People who live near the ocean and are seafaring frequently have quite distinct terms for different conditions of the sea, whether, for example, it is stormy, moderately rough, or relatively calm. Languages may also use different terms in speaking of different areas of the sea or ocean. Note, for example, in English the use of China Sea, North Sea, Bering Sea, in contrast with Indian Ocean, Pacific Ocean, Atlantic Ocean. In English this contrast is relatively fixed, but it does reflect a gradation of size, since sea is used normally in speaking of smaller areas than is the case with ocean.  
This word is found as a variant reading only: πᾶς ὁ ἐπὶ πόντον πλέων everyone sailing on the sea [REV 18:17](https://ref.ly/Jude18:17) (apparatus).

[REV 18:17](https://ref.ly/Jude18:17)

* **Associated Passages:** Revelation 18:17

1 πορεύομαι\[a] seems to contrast with πορεύομαι\[b] to travel, to journey ([15\.18](#15.18)) as well as with πορεύομαι\[c] to go away, to leave ([15\.34](#15.34)). In general, however, the meaning of πορεύομαι\[a] is more frequently rendered as ‘to go’ than as ‘to move’ or ‘to come.’ 2 MAT 17:27 It would also be possible to treat πορεύομαι in [MAT 17:27](https://ref.ly/Matt17:27) as being a case of πορεύομαι\[a] ‘to move,’ ‘to go’ ([15\.10](#15.10)). 3 Expressions occurring in numbered items [23\.101](#23.101)\-[23\.105](#23.105) involve euphemistic expressions for death. These have been subdivided on the basis of certain significant features of the underlying bases, as will be noted in the discussions.

## πόρνη (id: 004107000000000)

πόρνη
=====

πόρνη (G4204)

noun \| \-ης \| πορνεύω

88\.275 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Sexual Misbehavior

prostitute a woman who practices sexual immorality as a profession Since prostitution is an almost universal behavioral trait, it is not difficult to obtain terms for such a person or activity. The difficulty is usually involved in selecting a term which will not be vulgar. Expressions for a prostitute are often idiomatic, for example, ‘one who acts like a she\-dog,’ ‘one who sells her vulva,’ ‘one who sells herself,’ or ‘one who receives any man.’

[MAT 21:31](https://ref.ly/Matt21:31) · [MAT 21:32](https://ref.ly/Matt21:32) · [LUK 15:30](https://ref.ly/Mark15:30) · [1CO 6:15](https://ref.ly/Rom6:15) · [1CO 6:16](https://ref.ly/Rom6:16) · [HEB 11:31](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:31) · [JAS 2:25](https://ref.ly/Heb2:25) · [REV 17:1](https://ref.ly/Jude17:1) · [REV 17:5](https://ref.ly/Jude17:5) · [REV 17:15](https://ref.ly/Jude17:15) · [REV 17:16](https://ref.ly/Jude17:16) · [REV 19:2](https://ref.ly/Jude19:2)

πόρρωθεν
========

adv \| πρωτεύω

67\.46 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

far ahead of time, long before from a point of time considerably prior to another point of time It is also possible to interpret πόρρωθεν in [HEB 11:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:13) as meaning ‘from afar’ or ‘from a great distance’ (see [83\.31](#83.31)).

[HEB 11:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:13)

83\.31 · Spacial Positions · At, Beside, Near, Far

far away, at a distance, a long way off a position at a relatively great distance, with the possible implication of comparison1 In [HEB 11:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:13) πόρρωθεν may be interpreted as time (see [67\.46](#67.46)), but it is perhaps more satisfactorily interpreted as implying some visionary experience and hence a matter of space.

[LUK 17:12](https://ref.ly/Mark17:12) · [HEB 11:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:13)

1 It is possible that πόρρωθεν\[b] differs in meaning from πόρρω in emphasizing the degree of separation, but this cannot be determined from existing contexts.

## πορφύρα (id: 004111000000000)

πορφύρα
=======

πορφύρα (G4209)

noun \| \-ας \| πορφυρόπωλις · πορφυροῦς

6\.169 · Artifacts · Cloth, Leather, and Objects Made of Such Materials

scarlet cloth a reddish\-purple cloth dyed with a substance obtained from the murex shellfish; expensive to produce; associated with wealth and royalty In a number of languages there is no special color term for reddish\-purple, since the color in question is sometimes classified as a kind of blue and in other instances it is related to black. However, in all languages it is possible to be somewhat more precise about color terms by likening the color to that of a local flower or bird.

[MRK 15:17](https://ref.ly/Matt15:17) · [MRK 15:20](https://ref.ly/Matt15:20) · [LUK 16:19](https://ref.ly/Mark16:19) · [REV 18:12](https://ref.ly/Jude18:12)1

1 REV 18:12 From the context of [REV 18:12](https://ref.ly/Jude18:12) it is impossible to determine whether the referents of βύσσινον ([6\.167](#6.167)), σιρικόν ([6\.168](#6.168)), and πορφύρα ([6\.169](#6.169)) are cloth or garments made of linen, silk, and purple cloth respectively.

* **Associated Passages:** Mark 15:17; Mark 15:20; Luke 16:19; Revelation 18:12

## πόσις (id: 004115000000000)

πόσις
=====

πόσις (G4213)

noun \| \-εως \| πίνω

5\.6 · Foods and Condiments · Food

a drink liquids used for nourishment or to satisfy thirst Though in many languages the equivalent of πόσις and πόμα would be a verbal derivative meaning basically ‘that which is drunk,’ in other languages the equivalent may be ‘watery food’ or ‘liquid food.’  
In some languages, however, one cannot refer to ‘blood’ ([JHN 6:55](https://ref.ly/Luke6:55)) as being ‘drink,’ since blood is classified as food rather than as drink. On the other hand, it may be possible to translate ‘my blood is the real drink’ as ‘my blood is real nourishment.’ The expression ‘food and drink’ ([HEB 9:10](https://ref.ly/Phlm9:10)) may be better rendered in some languages as ‘all kinds of food.’

[JHN 6:55](https://ref.ly/Luke6:55)

23\.34 · Physiological Processes and States · Eat, Drink

drinking consumption of liquids, particularly water and wine In a number of languages a clear distinction is made between drinking water and drinking alcoholic beverages. Still other terms may be employed in speaking of drinking soups or broths.

[ROM 14:17](https://ref.ly/Acts14:17) · [COL 2:16](https://ref.ly/Phil2:16)

* **Associated Passages:** John 6:55; Romans 14:17; Colossians 2:16

inter.pron \| \-η, \-ον \| ποσάκις

how many interrogative of quantity of objects or events, usually implying a considerable amount

59\.17 · Quantity · Much, Little \[Masses, Collectives, Extensions]

[MAT 7:11](https://ref.ly/Matt7:11) · [MAT 10:25](https://ref.ly/Matt10:25) · [MAT 12:12](https://ref.ly/Matt12:12) · [MRK 9:21](https://ref.ly/Matt9:21) · [LUK 11:13](https://ref.ly/Mark11:13) · [LUK 12:24](https://ref.ly/Mark12:24) · [LUK 12:28](https://ref.ly/Mark12:28) · [LUK 16:5](https://ref.ly/Mark16:5) · [LUK 16:7](https://ref.ly/Mark16:7) · [ROM 11:12](https://ref.ly/Acts11:12) · [ROM 11:24](https://ref.ly/Acts11:24) · [PHM 1:16](https://ref.ly/Titus1:16)

78\.13 · Degree · Much, Little \[Positive\-Negative Degree]

how, very, how great, how much, intense, severe a relatively high point on a scale involving exclamation In a number of languages an expression of high degree in an exclamation can only be reproduced as an expression of intensive degree in a non\-exclamatory form. For example, the statement in [MAT 6:23](https://ref.ly/Matt6:23) ‘how great must the darkness be!’ must be rendered in a number of languages as ‘the darkness is very, very intense’ or ‘it is very, very dark there.’

## ποτήριον (id: 004123000000000)

ποτήριον
========

ποτήριον (G4221)

noun \| \-ου \| πίνω

6\.121 · Artifacts · Containers

cup an object from which one may drink In place of the expression ‘cup of water’ (in which cup not only identifies the container but indicates the quantity of water), some languages would use the expression ‘water in a cup,’ but it is even more likely that the typical equivalent of ‘cup of water’ would be ‘a drink of water.’ In some parts of the world a term such as ‘cup’ would suggest something rather strange and foreign; therefore, one might find that an expression ‘a gourd of water’ would not only be more natural, but would be the semantic equivalent of ‘drink of water.’

[MAT 10:42](https://ref.ly/Matt10:42) · [MAT 23:25](https://ref.ly/Matt23:25) · [MAT 23:26](https://ref.ly/Matt23:26) · [MAT 26:27](https://ref.ly/Matt26:27) · [MRK 7:4](https://ref.ly/Matt7:4) · [MRK 9:41](https://ref.ly/Matt9:41) · [MRK 14:23](https://ref.ly/Matt14:23) · [LUK 11:39](https://ref.ly/Mark11:39) · [LUK 22:17](https://ref.ly/Mark22:17) · [LUK 22:20](https://ref.ly/Mark22:20) · [LUK 22:20](https://ref.ly/Mark22:20) · [1CO 10:16](https://ref.ly/Rom10:16) · [1CO 10:21](https://ref.ly/Rom10:21) · [1CO 10:21](https://ref.ly/Rom10:21) · [1CO 11:25](https://ref.ly/Rom11:25) · [1CO 11:25](https://ref.ly/Rom11:25) · [1CO 11:26](https://ref.ly/Rom11:26) · [1CO 11:27](https://ref.ly/Rom11:27) · [1CO 11:28](https://ref.ly/Rom11:28) · [REV 17:4](https://ref.ly/Jude17:4) · [REV 18:6](https://ref.ly/Jude18:6)

90\.97 · Case · To Cause To Experience

**Collocation:** παραφέρω / παρέρχομαι τὸ ποτήριον … ἀπό

to cause someone not to experience, to take the cup from (an idiom, literally: to take the cup ... from) to cause someone to not undergo some trying experience

[MAT 26:39](https://ref.ly/Matt26:39) · [MRK 14:36](https://ref.ly/Matt14:36) · [LUK 22:42](https://ref.ly/Mark22:42)

24\.81 · Sensory Events and States · Pain, Suffering

**Collocation:** πίνω ποτήριον

to undergo a difficult experience, to drink the cup, to suffer (an idiom, literally: to drink a cup) to undergo a trying, difficult experience In a number of languages it is not possible to use the phrase ‘to drink the cup,’ since this will almost inevitably refer to the drinking of a poison cup as a means of proving innocence in an ordeal. Even if one uses a literal rendering of the idiom ‘to drink the cup,’ it is usually necessary to have some explanation as to the significance of the idiom.

[MAT 20:22](https://ref.ly/Matt20:22) · [MAT 20:23](https://ref.ly/Matt20:23) · [MRK 10:38](https://ref.ly/Matt10:38) · [MRK 10:39](https://ref.ly/Matt10:39) · [JHN 18:11](https://ref.ly/Luke18:11) · [REV 14:10](https://ref.ly/Jude14:10) · [REV 16:19](https://ref.ly/Jude16:19)

ποτίζω
======

ποτίζω (G4222)

πίνω

23\.35 · Physiological Processes and States · Eat, Drink

to give to drink to cause to drink In some languages it is impossible to avoid a reference to what is drunk. Therefore, one may translate [MAT 25:35](https://ref.ly/Matt25:35) as ‘I was thirsty and you gave me water to drink.’

[MAT 10:42](https://ref.ly/Matt10:42) · [MAT 25:35](https://ref.ly/Matt25:35) · [MAT 25:37](https://ref.ly/Matt25:37) · [MAT 25:42](https://ref.ly/Matt25:42) · [MAT 27:48](https://ref.ly/Matt27:48) · [MRK 9:41](https://ref.ly/Matt9:41) · [MRK 15:36](https://ref.ly/Matt15:36) · [LUK 13:15](https://ref.ly/Mark13:15) · [ROM 12:20](https://ref.ly/Acts12:20) · [1CO 3:2](https://ref.ly/Rom3:2) · [1CO 12:13](https://ref.ly/Rom12:13) · [REV 14:8](https://ref.ly/Jude14:8)

43\.9 · Agriculture

to water, to irrigate to provide water for plants In a number of languages one not speak of ‘watering a plant,’ but one may ‘water the ground’ or ‘cause the ground to drink’ or ‘make the ground muddy.’

[1CO 3:6](https://ref.ly/Rom3:6) · [1CO 3:7](https://ref.ly/Rom3:7) · [1CO 3:8](https://ref.ly/Rom3:8)

## πούς (id: 004130000000000)

8\.49 · Body, Body Parts, and Body Products · Parts of the Body

foot the part of the leg below the ankle on which a person or animal stands and moves In [REV 10:1](https://ref.ly/Jude10:1) (οἱ πόδες αὐτοῦ ὡς στῦλοι πυρός ‘his feet were like pillars of fire’) it is possible that πούς refers to the leg since the context of [REV 10:1](https://ref.ly/Jude10:1) would seem to favor this interpretation, but the meaning of ‘foot’ should not be excluded, especially since this passage refers to an image as part of a supernatural vision, and one cannot be certain of the specific intent.

34\.59 · Association · Show Hospitality

**Collocation:** πόδας νίπτω

to be very hospitable to (an idiom, literally: to wash the feet, derived from the practice of washing the feet of any guest entering the home) to show sincere and gracious hospitality to someone It is also possible that the idiom πόδας νίπτω means ‘to perform humble duties on behalf of someone’ (see [88\.58](#88.58)).

[1TI 5:10](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:10)

88\.58 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Humility

**Collocation:** πόδας νίπτω

to act humbly, to behave in a humble manner (an idiom, literally: to wash the feet) to show humility by doing humble tasks For another interpretation of πόδας νίπτω in [1TI 5:10](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:10), see [34\.59](#34.59).

[JHN 13:14](https://ref.ly/Luke13:14) · [JHN 13:14](https://ref.ly/Luke13:14) · [1TI 5:10](https://ref.ly/2Thess5:10)

57\.82 · Possess, Transfer, Exchange · Give

**Collocation:** τίθημι παρὰ τοὺς πόδας · τίθημι πρὸς τοὺς πόδας

to turn over to, to put at someone’s disposal (idioms, literally: to put at someone’s feet) to present something to someone who is reckoned as having superior status or position

[ACT 4:35](https://ref.ly/John4:35) · [ACT 4:37](https://ref.ly/John4:37) · [ACT 5:2](https://ref.ly/John5:2)

41\.30 · Behavior and Related States · Particular Patterns of Behavior

**Collocation:** τροχιὰς ὀρθὰς ποιέω τοῖς ποσίν

to live, to behave, to conduct oneself correctly (an idiom, literally: to make straight wheel tracks for the feet) to live or behave in strict conformance to a predetermined model for behavior

[HEB 12:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm12:13)

37\.8 · Control, Rule · Control, Restrain

**Collocation:** ὑπὸ τοὺς πόδας · ὑποκάτω τῶν ποδῶν · ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν

under the complete control of (idioms, literally under the feet of, and: footstool of the feet) to be under the complete control of someone In a number of languages it is simply not possible to preserve the idiom ‘under the feet of,’ though in some instances one may use a parallel idiom, for example, ‘to stand on,’ so that [1CO 15:25](https://ref.ly/Rom15:25) might be rendered ‘until he stands on all his enemies,’ but it is more likely that the idiom in this context is better rendered as ‘until he defeats all his enemies.’

[MAT 5:35](https://ref.ly/Matt5:35) · [MAT 22:44](https://ref.ly/Matt22:44) · [MRK 12:36](https://ref.ly/Matt12:36) · [LUK 20:43](https://ref.ly/Mark20:43) · [ACT 2:35](https://ref.ly/John2:35) · [ACT 7:49](https://ref.ly/John7:49) · [ROM 16:20](https://ref.ly/Acts16:20) · [1CO 15:25](https://ref.ly/Rom15:25) · [1CO 15:27](https://ref.ly/Rom15:27) · [EPH 1:22](https://ref.ly/Gal1:22) · [HEB 1:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm1:13) · [HEB 2:8](https://ref.ly/Phlm2:8) · [HEB 10:13](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:13)

## πρᾶγμα (id: 004131000000000)

πρᾶγμα
======

πρᾶγμα (G4229)

noun \| \-τος \| πράσσω

13\.105 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Happen

happening, event that which happens

[MAT 18:19](https://ref.ly/Matt18:19) · [LUK 1:1](https://ref.ly/Mark1:1) · [ACT 5:4](https://ref.ly/John5:4) · [2CO 7:11](https://ref.ly/1Cor7:11) · [HEB 6:18](https://ref.ly/Phlm6:18) · [HEB 10:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm10:1) · [HEB 11:1](https://ref.ly/Phlm11:1) · [JAS 3:16](https://ref.ly/Heb3:16)

42\.9 · Perform, Do · Do, Perform

undertaking, task an activity involving a measure of complexity and responsibility

[ROM 16:2](https://ref.ly/Acts16:2) · [1TH 4:6](https://ref.ly/Col4:6)

56\.2 · Courts and Legal Procedures · Lawsuit, Case

lawsuit, case, legal action legal action taken in a court of law against someone1 In most languages there are technical terms for lawsuits or cases in court, but where these do not exist, one may always use a descriptive phrase, for example, ‘to accuse someone before a judge’ or ‘to argue against someone in court’ or ‘to say in court that someone has done a person harm.’

[1CO 6:1](https://ref.ly/Rom6:1) · [1TH 4:6](https://ref.ly/Col4:6)

1 It is possible that πρᾶγμα\[c] is somewhat more generic in meaning than κριτήριον\[b] or κρίμα\[e], but it is difficult to determine this from existing contexts.
